This is by design, not a bug—enables MimicGen augmentation from error scenes Problems & Solutions Critical Issues 1. ARI stability analysis would be extremely slow (~30min/section/run) if rerunning entire pipeline (extract+align+fusion+cluster), 10 runs × 11 sections = 330 experiments infeasible Solution: Designed architecture to load existing gene/vision embeddings from cache, only rerun fusion+clustering stages. analyze_ari_stability.py directly calls CacheManager to load PCA/UNI2, uses different seeds to initialize STAIGTrainer fusion, then cluster. Single test verified acceleration to ~25 minutes (CPU)/section, GPU estimated 3-5 minutes
Key Insight: Key optimization point for repeated experiments is identifying where randomness exists - MIHD’s vision extraction is deterministic (UNI2 model fixed weights), gene PCA also fixed, only STAIG fusion’s GCN initialization, dropout, mclust random seed affect ARI. Through layered caching (data→gene emb→vision emb→fused emb→clustering) can resume at any stage
2. Collection script requirement ambiguous: ’error scene’ might refer to subtype level (like grasp_misalignment_D0) or specific scene file (like v5_train_xxx_000.json), quota count changed from user’s initial 5 to 10 Solution: Through ccplan Phase 2 used AskUserQuestion tool for requirement verification: confirmed subtype level, 10 per subtype, pre-recorded MP4 reference videos, preserve complete verification process
Key Insight: Ambiguous requirements must be verified through structured questioning, ccplan’s 9-dimensional intent extraction and AskUserQuestion are key tools, avoid implementing wrong solution
3. MIHD label mixing analysis had predicted labels not matching GT label values, resulting in only 2.6% accuracy Solution: Identified that pipeline stored pred_labels are original cluster IDs (not Hungarian matched), while ARI/NMI are permutation invariant so don’t need matching. Applied scipy.optimize.linear_sum_assignment() for Hungarian matching in analyze_label_mixing.py, used matched aligned_pred for visualization functions, accuracy corrected to 49-61%
Key Insight: Permutation invariant metrics like ARI/NMI hide label alignment issues - when generating confusion matrix or per-class metrics must first do Hungarian matching, otherwise will get misleading extremely low accuracy. Pipeline caching design choice to store original cluster IDs is correct (maintains flexibility), but downstream analysis needs to be aware of this
4. TokenMonitor project git pull conflict: local and remote each have 16 commits diverged, 54 file conflicts covering almost all Rust backend modules + core frontend components Solution: Through git log –format analyzed both sides’ commit messages and change statistics, identified essence as ‘independent identical modular refactoring + cross-platform support from v0.5.0 common ancestor’. Comparison found remote v0.7.2 contains all functionality of local v0.6.0 plus PR#4 code review fixes, rate limit unification, cache tier pipeline, etc. additional improvements. Decided to use remote and executed git reset –hard origin/main, avoiding manual resolution of 54 file conflicts
Key Insight: When local and remote do large-scale refactoring on same batch of files, merge conflict count doesn’t represent change difference magnitude - might be very similar refactoring. Key at this time is identifying ‘who is superset of whom’ rather than file-by-file comparison. Through version numbers (0.7.2 vs 0.6.0), commit message detail (PR review, bug fix vs simple ‘update’) can quickly judge which side is more complete
5. User wanted skip scene (right-click) or validation failure to not jump to next subtype, should continue collecting in current subtype Solution: Modified logic in round-robin loop: changed break (exit current subtype loop) to continue (stay in current subtype, move to next scene)
Key Insight: Round-robin is not just change in traversal order, also involves retry strategy when failed/skipped, need to carefully consider state machine transitions
6. MuJoCo 3.4.0 incompatible with Sawyer robot meshes (volume too small error for nut_assembly and pick_place tasks) Solution: Successfully rendered Panda-based tasks (coffee, coffee_preparation, mug_cleanup) first; switched to prepared_source HDF5 files that worked with current environment. Alternative: could use mimicgen_env with older MuJoCo version
Key Insight: Robot model compatibility is environment-dependent; having multiple conda environments with different MuJoCo versions provides fallback options for rendering tasks across different robot types
7. HD RM-IDEAL evaluation needs to support single section data (currently only hd_crop10large), but original benchmark_rm_ideal.py assumes at least 2 sections for cross-section query Solution: Check len(sections) before bidirectional cross-section evaluation, if =1 output prompt ‘single section mode: supports within-sample evaluation (query within same section) or cross-dataset evaluation’, don’t execute cross-section loop. Also load_hd_section_string_labels() supports fine_grained parameter to switch between 3 classes (merged CE+Tumor) or 4 classes (original STHD) annotations
Key Insight: Within-sample query is equally valuable in single-sample datasets - can evaluate method’s representation comparability between different niche types within single tissue section. HD data though only has 1 crop, but 17502 spots density is extremely high (8μm bins), sufficient to support statistically significant within-sample RM-IDEAL evaluation
8. Extraction planner currently doesn’t support passing extra_config.source_patch_size to vision extraction job params, limiting HD patch size experiment flexibility Solution: Created independent script extract_hd_intermediate_patches.py to bypass pipeline, directly instantiate UNI2VisionEncoder, loop call adata.extract() passing different source_patch_size. Manually manage cache (different patch sizes use different output directories), each size evaluates vision_only/concat/mean three fusion types. This is pragmatic implementation for experimental feature
Key Insight: When pipeline infrastructure doesn’t support certain experimental requirement, independent script vs modifying pipeline trade-off: if one-time exploratory experiment, independent script is faster and doesn’t pollute main pipeline; if recurring requirement, worth investing time in pipeline extension. HD patch size experiment belongs to former - goal is finding optimal size rather than supporting arbitrary size production pipeline
General Issues 9. User discovered repair files and didn’t understand their meaning, worried about affecting collection (pick_place some subtypes have 11-12 scenes instead of standard 10) Solution: Through comparing JSON content confirmed repair files have same format as normal scenes (only scene_id naming different), explained as v5 pipeline’s additional repair scenes, harmless and usable for collection
Key Insight: When code audit discovers ‘anomalies’ (like repair files) need to verify through actual data comparison, rather than just speculating from naming
10. Analysis pipeline implementation encountered matplotlib dependency missing, recovery_collect conda environment didn’t have matplotlib installed Solution: Identified problem but not solved in this session (session interrupted after detecting problem), need follow-up pip install matplotlib in recovery_collect environment
Key Insight: Independent scripts (analysis/) dependencies should have independent requirements.txt, avoid polluting main project environment
11. User ran bash collect.sh –task stack and only saw one agentview, not the expected three-camera display Solution: Check found collect.sh didn’t pass –dual_view parameter by default, modified collect.sh to add –dual_view when calling Python script
Key Insight: CLI wrapper scripts need to ensure passing key parameters, especially those affecting UX (like multi-camera display)
12. rclone failed to download logs because config path was ‘gdrive:summarize’ but remote directory had been migrated to ‘gdrive:gadget/summarize/logs’ Solution: Updated ~/.config/summarize/config.json’s rclone_remote field from ‘gdrive:summarize’ to ‘gdrive:gadget/summarize’ to match new remote structure
Key Insight: When migrating remote directory structure, all local config files referencing remote paths must be updated synchronously to avoid sync failures
13. How to run two completely independently configured Claude Code instances on same machine Solution: Research Claude Code configuration mechanism found CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR environment variable support, created wrapper script claude-code setting independent config directory ~/.claude-code
Key Insight: Claude Code supports environment variable override of config directory, similar to Git’s GIT_DIR, can achieve multi-instance isolation
14. Initial implementation generated 60 videos (10 demos per task) when user only needed 6 (1 demo per task) Solution: User clarified requirement mid-execution. Stopped batch job and created new script (render_best_success_videos.py) that picks highest-reward demo per task and outputs single {task}.mp4 files. Completed in ~8 seconds on GPU
Key Insight: ‘Demo video’ is ambiguous—could mean one per trajectory or one representative per task. Clarify scope and deliverable granularity upfront, especially for resource-intensive generation tasks
15. Slurm GPU job submission failed with ‘Invalid generic resource (gres) specification’ error Solution: Pivoted from Slurm to direct SSH access to an49 GPU node. Verified EGL rendering capability with smoke test before running full batch. Used bash -lc with full conda initialization path
Key Insight: When cluster resource managers have configuration issues, direct node access via SSH can be more reliable for interactive development and debugging. Always validate rendering pipeline with minimal test before full batch
Human vs AI Approaches Strategic Level MIHD task decomposition and prioritization Role Approach Human Provided 13 Chinese meeting task list, requested ‘see what else needs to be done? write to plan’, then called /ccplan skill and said ‘complete tasks one by one’ - high-level requirement expression, expecting AI autonomous decomposition and execution AI Systematic process: (1) read plans.md and meeting_0413_tasks.md cross-validation, (2) mapped 13 tasks to P0/P1/P2 classification identifying missing items, (3) created 5 implementable code tasks (skipped non-Zijia tasks 8-10/12), (4) sorted by dependency relationship (P1-22 highest priority as it unlocks most downstream), (5) implemented per-task (read source→design solution→edit files→syntax check→function test→update docs). Entire process created 5 new scripts, 4 SLURM configs, modified 2 existing scripts, updated plans.md Difference Analysis: Human provided strategic direction and task boundaries (’these are meeting requirements’), AI responsible for tactical execution and technical decisions (Hungarian matching, cache-based speedup, independent script vs pipeline extension, etc.). Key difference is AI autonomously judged implementation priority (P1-22→P1-18→P1-19→P1-20→P1-4) and parallelization strategy (3 HD RM-IDEAL backgrounds running simultaneously), human didn’t specify these details
Git conflict root cause analysis Role Approach Human After seeing 54 file conflicts, didn’t immediately request ‘help me merge’ or ‘resolve conflicts’, but asked ‘what problems did local and remote each solve?’ - demonstrates understanding-driven engineer mindset, wanting to clarify conflict nature before making decision AI Provided three-layer analysis: (1) git log comparison showing commit messages, (2) git diff –stat statistics of change magnitude (~23k vs ~24k lines), (3) inferred functionality from commit message content (‘modular refactoring + cross-platform’ vs ‘same refactoring + more bug fixes’). Finally gave conclusion ‘both independently did same large refactoring, remote is superset of local’ and 4 handling options (reset/force push/manual merge/rebase) Difference Analysis: Human demonstrated ‘understand first then act’ judgment - crucial when facing complex technical problems. If AI encountered a user directly saying ‘help me resolve git conflict’, might tend to provide merge steps rather than deep analysis. In this case human’s question guided better diagnostic process
Collection quota increased from 5 to 10 decision Role Approach Human User after auditing report proactively proposed ‘can we change loop to 10 times’ (each subtype 10 demos, corresponding to 10 scene files one-to-one mapping) AI AI initially planned 5 per subtype based on user requirements (total 680), only adjusted to 10 (total 1360) after user confirmation Difference Analysis: User after seeing scene file audit (each subtype has 10 scenes), naturally thought of 1:1 mapping to fully utilize data. AI needs explicit confirmation to adjust plan
HD RM-IDEAL single section evaluation design Role Approach Human Didn’t explicitly point out HD only has 1 section would cause cross-section evaluation failure, only mentioned P2-22 task ‘HD RM-IDEAL k=15 run’ AI When implementing P1-22 proactively identified len(sections)==1 edge case, added within-sample evaluation mode support and prompt output. Also designed docstring example showing HD single section usage, and distinguished cross_section_hd in output_dir naming Difference Analysis: AI preemptively handled edge case during implementation, human didn’t mention this detail in task description. This demonstrates AI’s ‘defensive programming’ mindset - not only completing explicit requirements, but also considering boundary conditions. But also exposes incompleteness of human task description (might be assuming AI will handle, or might be oversight)
Analysis pipeline’s MimicGen video generation strategy Role Approach Human User requested generating ‘all mimicgen videos’, in ccplan Phase 0 calibration, user chose ‘rerun augmentation’ rather than ‘only generate successful NPZ’ AI AI provided two options: (1) rerun augmentation (high diagnostic value but time-consuming) vs (2) only NPZ replay (fast but missing failure process) Difference Analysis: User explicitly chose diagnostic value priority, even if more time-consuming. If AI directly implemented might choose simpler NPZ replay solution
Requirement interpretation for video generation scope Role Approach Human User intended ‘generate success videos for all tasks’ to mean one representative video per task (6 total), prioritizing efficiency and downstream usability AI AI interpreted this as ‘replay all demos in all source HDF5 files’ (60+ videos), prioritizing completeness and preserving all available demonstration data Difference Analysis: Human optimized for practical sufficiency (minimal viable deliverable); AI optimized for exhaustive coverage. The mismatch arose from ambiguity in ‘all tasks’—human meant task-level granularity, AI assumed demo-level granularity. This reflects different default assumptions about what constitutes ’thorough’ execution
Implementation Level Three-camera display requirement clarification Role Approach Human User first said ‘change to 90 degree side camera’ (AI understood as replacement), second time said ‘also need side and wrist’ (AI realized it’s three cameras displayed simultaneously) AI AI first implementation was agentview + sideview dual-view (384×2), second time correctly understood as three-view (384 + 192×2) Difference Analysis: User’s requirement expression incomplete (first time missed saying ‘simultaneously’), AI should proactively clarify vs user habitually expecting AI to automatically understand context
Technical pathway selection when Slurm failed Role Approach Human User proactively suggested two parallel options (SSH or Slurm) upfront, indicating awareness of cluster infrastructure issues and preference for fallback strategies AI AI attempted Slurm first (appeared more ‘proper’ for GPU jobs), then debugged gres errors, and only pivoted to SSH after multiple failures. Sequential troubleshooting rather than immediate fallback Difference Analysis: Human demonstrated systems knowledge—anticipating that institutional Slurm configs may be broken—and valued pragmatic shortcuts. AI followed ‘correct’ workflow hierarchy (Slurm > ad-hoc SSH) and invested effort in debugging before fallback. Human’s suggestion revealed experience with this specific cluster’s quirks
AI Limitations Critical Limitations ccplan skill execution violated specifications: skill documentation emphasizes ‘ALWAYS use AskUserQuestion tool when you need user input’, should use AskUserQuestion in Phase 0 (prompt calibration confirmation), Phase 2 (hypothesis interrogation), Phase 3 (approach selection), Phase 9 (review gate). But actual execution I directly skipped these stages, created TaskCreate and immediately started implementation. Although user said ‘complete tasks one by one’ giving execution permission, but according to strict skill process should first confirm calibrated prompt and approach MIHD task implementation assumption unverified: I assumed user wanted to immediately execute all 5 tasks, but actually P1-19 and P1-20 are both SLURM jobs need to queue for GPU (might run several hours later), P1-18 analysis results also not shown to user to confirm if meets expectations. More robust process is to complete first task then show results, ask ‘is this direction right? continue next one?’ Git conflict analysis missing key context inquiry: after analyzing both sides’ changes, I directly provided 4 handling options, but didn’t first ask ‘were these two sides developed independently by you on different machines, or were other collaborators involved?’ This information is important for decision - if own two branches can freely reset, if has collaborators need to more carefully consider force push impact HD patch size experiment solution didn’t discuss trade-off: I directly chose ‘independent script’ rather than ‘modify pipeline’, reasoning given was ’experimental feature not worth polluting main pipeline’. But this architectural decision should discuss with user - if later need to use different patch sizes for all datasets, independent script will lead to duplicate work. I made technical judgment but didn’t confirm if fits project long-term direction General Limitations In Session 1, AI implemented complete code for analysis pipeline (3 modules), but didn’t check dependencies in current conda environment, only discovered matplotlib missing at the end. Should run ‘conda list matplotlib’ check before or during coding Understanding ‘simultaneously display multiple cameras’ for user requirement had delay: first modification only implemented dual-view, only correctly understood as three-view after user explicitly said ‘also need wrist camera’. Should proactively ask ’need to preserve other cameras?’ at first requirement In ccplan Phase 2 requirement verification, AI’s explanation of ‘repair files’ delayed until user proactively asked. Should proactively explain repair file meaning in audit report, avoid user confusion Spent multiple attempts debugging Slurm gres configuration issues before pivoting to SSH approach, despite user having already suggested SSH as viable alternative from the start Background task status monitoring not timely: after ARI stability test started output file remained empty for long time (0 lines), when user first asked ‘ok, help me see how to implement this plan’ I should have first checked that task status, rather than directly entering next stage. Only discovered completion at final task notification File upload progress monitoring inelegant: used repeatedly ssh query remote file size to monitor scp progress, although effective but not optimal solution. scp itself has progress output (-v flag), but because used run_in_background parameter, progress information was redirected to output file rather than real-time visible. Better solution is rsync –progress or not use background mode to directly show progress Misinterpreted scope of ‘generate videos for all tasks’: implemented exhaustive per-demo rendering (60 videos) instead of representative per-task rendering (6 videos), requiring mid-flight correction after user clarification Learnings Key Learnings ccplan skill’s value in complex requirement decomposition: 9-dimensional intent extraction (Task/Input/Output/Constraints/Context/Audience/Memory/Success/Examples) + AskUserQuestion structured questioning + ECL document persistence, can effectively handle ambiguous requirements and ensure cross-session consistency Hungarian matching is key step for converting clustering results to per-class metrics, but easily masked by permutation invariant metrics like ARI/NMI. When generating confusion matrix, per-layer accuracy, spatial misclassification map must first align labels, otherwise will get misleading extremely low accuracy (2.6% vs real 49-61%). Pipeline design should preserve original cluster IDs in cache (maintain flexibility), do matching on-demand in analysis scripts Performance optimization key for repeated experiments is identifying randomness source and layered caching. MIHD’s ARI stability analysis reduced from ~30min/run to ~3-5min/run (GPU), by only rerunning fusion+clustering while reusing vision/gene embeddings. Design principle: deterministic steps (vision extraction) cache results, randomness steps (STAIG GCN initialization, mclust seed) re-execute each time. This applies to any experiments needing parameter sweep/randomness quantification Round-robin collection mode implementation is not just change in traversal order, also involves: (1) state machine design (per-subtype count vs global rounds), (2) failure/skip retry strategy (stay vs move), (3) progress display multi-level aggregation (rounds/subtype/total progress) Error recovery benchmark v5 pipeline architecture: (1) collect_clean_trajectories from demos → (2) scan_opportunities calling skill.can_inject() per frame → (3) schedule_injections sampling from virtual timeline → (4) generate_training_scenes executing injection with InjectionEngine. Each scene stored as JSON metadata + NPZ sim state Large-scale git merge conflict may stem from ‘parallel implementation of identical refactoring’ rather than genuine logic conflicts. Identification method: (1) count similar change magnitude on both sides (23k vs 24k), (2) conflict files all in same modules (Rust commands/platform, Svelte components), (3) commit messages show similar architecture goals (‘modularize backend’, ‘cross-platform support’). At this time key is judging ‘who is superset of whom’ (version number, additional features, bug fix count) rather than file-by-file merge Pipeline extensibility vs independent script decision framework: (1) one-time exploratory experiment (like HD patch size)→independent script, (2) recurring but non-core requirement (like neighborhood query)→optional parameter integration, (3) core functionality (like HD dataset support)→complete pipeline integration. Independent script advantage is quickly verify hypothesis without polluting main code, disadvantage is if later productionized need to rewrite. Key judgment point is ‘will this feature become standard process?’ Label mixing analysis revealed spatial omics clustering patterns: Layer_4 (middle cortex) worst accuracy (19.3%) often misclassified as adjacent Layer_3/5, Layer_2 split into Layer_1/3 (40.1%), while outer Layer_1 and WM highest accuracy (83-100%). This reflects biological reality: (1) middle cortical layers have blurred boundaries (neuron type gradients), (2) outer layers have clear anatomical features (Layer_1 is molecular layer, WM is white matter myelin), (3) spot-level embedding based clustering difficult to capture inter-layer gradients. Corresponding solution may need neighborhood-level representation or spatial-aware clustering Robot teleoperation collection interface UX design principles: (1) multi-camera viewpoint assists operation (front+side+wrist), (2) real-time progress feedback (avoid ‘black box’ feeling), (3) reference video reduces learning cost, (4) automatic retry when validation fails (rather than manual restart) Error scene audit importance: before implementing collection process, must audit actual subtype count and scene file count for each task, avoid hardcoding wrong quota or traversal logic MuJoCo EGL rendering pipeline for headless servers requires careful environment configuration: MUJOCO_GL=egl, CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES matching MUJOCO_EGL_DEVICE_ID, and compatible robot meshes. MuJoCo 3.4.0 has Sawyer mesh incompatibility; using prepared HDF5 with compatible environments is more reliable than debugging mesh volumes robosuite/mimicgen environment registration: custom _D0 environments (PickPlace_D0, Coffee_D0) require ‘import mimicgen’ before robosuite.make(). State replay from HDF5 uses pre-computed sim states at each frame, enabling exact visual reproduction without action replay or physics determinism concerns id_eligible classification in error scenes: 249/1365 scenes marked as ‘similar to initial distribution’ is by design, not a bug. Error types like drop_in_transit, collision_empty, stuck_no_progress naturally produce table-stable states where object is on table, gripper empty, within workspace. Stack-like tasks have higher id_eligible rates (78/240) due to reset similarity. This enables MimicGen augmentation directly from error scenes Question quality determines analysis depth: in Git conflict case, user asked ‘what problems did local and remote each solve?’ rather than ‘help me merge’ or ‘resolve conflicts’, guided commit history comparison, version evolution analysis, architecture decision trade-off and other deep diagnostics. This demonstrates engineer’s ‘understanding-driven’ mindset - understand problem nature first then choose solution. AI should proactively provide this analysis when encountering ‘direct execution’ type requests, rather than waiting for user to ask Practical Learnings Claude Code’s CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR environment variable supports configuration isolation, similar to Git’s GIT_DIR, can be used for multi-project/multi-device independent configuration. Wrapper script is simple effective solution for implementing independent instances Project cleanup systematic method: (1) first audit (find .DS_Store/pycache/test-data), (2) confirm deletion scope (confirm with user), (3) categorized cleanup (garbage files/debug data/obsolete scripts/temporary files), (4) verify final state (du -sh + tree) The summarize module implements auto-finalization for reports: non-current-day dates with no new conversations are marked ‘_finalized: true’ to skip redundant reprocessing in future merge operations The gadget project uses a unified remote directory structure under gdrive:gadget/ with subdirectories for summarize/, website/, research/, and test/, with each having its own config file that must be kept in sync Conversation Summaries MIHD Spatial Omics Benchmark ✅ 04-13 meeting tasks implementation: HD support + label analysis + stability testing + patch experiments 00:20:00.852 | claude_code User provided 13 Chinese meeting tasks, requested integration into plans.md and implementation one by one. Used ccplan skill for systematic decomposition: identified 5 implementable code tasks, sorted by dependencies and executed. Completed P1-22 (HD RM-IDEAL data loading adaptation, added –dataset HD support, implemented load_hd_section_string_labels() for STHD annotation loading 100% match), P1-18 (Label mixing analysis revealing Layer_4 worst 19.3%, applied Hungarian matching correcting accuracy from 2.6% to 49-61%), P1-19 (ARI stability testing framework, cache-based acceleration, SLURM job 45484777 submitted), P1-20 (HD patch size experiment script, 4 size comparison, job 45484778 submitted), P1-4 (Neighborhood query integration –neighborhood parameter). Submitted 2 GPU jobs, launched 3 HD RM-IDEAL background evaluations, updated plans.md. Finally analyzed current blocking progress: 3 Teddy jobs running/pending, identified downstream dependencies
Checkpoint Upload ✅ Upload 18GB robotics learning checkpoint to athena server 02:57:32.374 | claude_code User requested upload two tar.gz files (libero_10 14GB + qcvla 4GB) to athena.egr.duke.edu:/home/zt81/. Launched two parallel scp background tasks, monitored progress by repeatedly ssh querying remote file size (qcvla completed first, libero from 0→7.6GB→11GB→14GB). User inquired progress twice (‘where uploaded to?’, ‘how much uploaded?’), provided timely percentage reports. Finally both files successfully uploaded
TokenMonitor ✅ Handle git pull conflict: analyze 54 file conflicts + identify identical refactoring + reset to remote 05:08:36.469 | claude_code User encountered git pull failure (divergent branches local/remote each 16 commits). Executed git merge –no-commit found 54 file conflicts. User asked ‘what problems did local and remote each solve?’ - key question. Analyzed git log and commit messages, identified both sides independently did identical modular refactoring + cross-platform support from v0.5.0 common ancestor, remote v0.7.2 is superset of local v0.6.0 (additionally includes PR#4 code review fixes, rate limit unification, cache tier pipeline, etc.). Provided 4 solutions, user chose ‘use remote’. Executed git reset –hard origin/main, branch restored to clean state
Error Recovery Benchmark • Complete workflow: analysis pipeline + data collection refactoring + video generation + training set analysis + documentation 04:55:26.290 | multiple Across multiple sessions completed error recovery benchmark comprehensive work: (1) Used ccplan to plan diagnostic analysis pipeline for failed demos, implemented three modules (video_generators, trajectory_visualizer, analyze_demo), supports state/action replay video generation, MimicGen rerun, 3D trajectory overlay and error curve comparison, blocked by matplotlib dependency; (2) Refactored data collection flow to user-friendly mode: created collect.sh entry script, modified Python main loop implementing round-robin collection (10 per subtype, 1360 total), added progress display, collected_data automatic archiving, three-camera interface (agentview+sideview+wrist), audited 6 tasks’ 136 subtypes confirming each subtype has ~10 scenes, updated TUTORIAL.md documentation, cleaned project folder freeing 200MB; (3) Generated single-demo success videos for 6 manipulation tasks using GPU-accelerated MuJoCo rendering on tianhe server an49 node, overcame technical challenges (MuJoCo compatibility, Slurm configuration, environment setup) by SSH to GPU node for EGL rendering, delivered 6 MP4 files; (4) Analyzed v5_training dataset structure: 1365 error scenes across 6 tasks, traced complete generation pipeline through 4 stages, investigated why some scenes appear similar to initial states—discovered id_eligible classification mechanism where 249 scenes intentionally preserved as ’table-stable’ states for MimicGen augmentation use cases
gadget ✅ Fix rclone remote path after directory migration 06:19:09.070 | claude_code User reported migrating remote directory to gdrive:gadget/summarize/logs. Diagnosed config mismatch (gdrive:summarize vs gdrive:gadget/summarize), edited ~/.config/summarize/config.json to correct path, verified successful sync of 184 log files. Also clarified that merge process both uploads reports and auto-finalizes non-current dates to prevent redundant processing
Token Usage Total: $235.94 — 152.8M tokens
summary: Completed 5 MIHD core modules (HD dataset support, label mixing analysis, ARI stability testing, patch size experiments, neighborhood query integration), uploaded 18GB checkpoint to athena server, resolved TokenMonitor git conflict, implemented error recovery benchmark analysis pipeline and data collection workflow, generated 6 task success videos, configured independent Claude Code environment on tianhe server, and fixed gadget rclone configuration
title: Bug Journal 2026-04-14

content:
Daily Report — 2026-04-13 Daily Overview What was done: High-impact work across computational biology (MIHD spatial transcriptomics benchmark paper), robotics (error recovery dataset and LeRobot conversion pipeline), AI tooling (meeting transcription with dual audio sources), and energy research (cross-chemistry battery degradation modeling) How it was done: Systematic multi-phase workflows: CCPlan for complex features, adversarial audit for architecture validation, parallel agent exploration for paper writing, root-cause diagnosis for fusion failures, O(n²) performance optimization, and domain generalization theory for battery modeling Impact: Publication-ready NeurIPS papers (MIHD benchmark + RecoverBench), production-grade tools (MeetingHelper dual-channel, LeRobot conversion), critical bug discoveries (Teddy 460x variance dilution, STAIG architectural flaw), and research foundation for cross-chemistry battery models DCC What was done: MIHD project: Complete NeurIPS 2026 paper draft (6 sections + appendix), P0/P1 task completion (Teddy+UNI2 fusion diagnosis, published baselines collection, GPU job orchestration, DE analysis multi-section extension, Bootstrap CI implementation) How it was done: Multi-agent exploration (3 parallel agents: results/methodology/plans) → structured paper outline → LaTeX implementation (main.tex 38KB + style + refs). Root-cause analysis via embedding norm comparison revealed 460x variance gap. SLURM job submission with GPU type constraints (RTX 2080 Ti → 5000 Ada escalation after OOM failures). Extended DE pipeline to batch mode with confusion matrix validation. Impact: Major milestone: first complete MIHD paper draft targeting NeurIPS D&B Track. Identified root cause of multimodal fusion failure (pre-L2-normalized Teddy embeddings lose discriminative power after dimension-agnostic fusion). Collected comprehensive published baseline data (13 methods × 12 sections). Discovered STAIG architectural bug (gene_emb parameter ignored). Workflow optimizations via auto-approval settings. MacBook What was done: MeetingHelper: rewrote CLAUDE.md with architecture docs, implemented dual audio source transcription (mic + system audio) via full ccplan workflow. error-recovery: created collection automation skills, fixed collect_all.sh configuration bug (–collection_mode quota parameter) How it was done: Applied ccplan Phases 0-10 (intent calibration → hypothesis validation → adversarial audit → implementation) with Explore/adversarial-audit subagents. Refactored live-subtitle.py to region-based architecture (independent cursors per audio source, split window MIC/SYS zones). Added BlackHole auto-detection and dual transcriber orchestration. Fixed shell script to align with recovery_collection.yaml default mode. Impact: MeetingHelper upgraded to simultaneous mic + system audio recording with isolated subtitle regions. error-recovery data collection pipeline standardized with reusable skills (/collect-status, /collect-stack, /collect-validate) and bug-free configuration alignment. tianhe What was done: error-recovery: NeurIPS 2026 RecoverBench paper outline, comprehensive bug documentation with diagnosis methods, cross-server data sync (270MB), collection plan CSV generation (1365 scenes), portable script refactor for dynamic task registry. BOSS: fixed 5 critical LeRobot 0.4.0 bugs (add_frame signature, video encoding dtype, O(n²) performance, multi-dataset mapping, validation logic). Battery research: designed domain-invariant degradation indicator framework with three-phase verification protocol. How it was done: Paper outline generated via skill-guided exploration (RecoverBench v5.0: 12 Error Skills, 5 RBGs, 6 tasks; core narrative: 3.7% RSR vs 60-70% normal SR catastrophic gap). Documented 5 bugs with specific diagnosis commands (OSC controller, quaternion, state_reset, timing, warp point). Used rsync for MacBook→tianhe sync. Fixed LeRobot dtype:‘image’→‘video’ for MP4 encoding, removed dataset.create_hf_dataset() from per-episode loop (O(n²) elimination), updated add_frame() to separate task parameter, implemented source_name:path multi-mapping format. Battery model: proposed z degradation indicator with contrastive learning encoder + normalization validation. Impact: RecoverBench paper foundation established (strong difficulty proof: Pi0.5 FT 3.7% RSR on 1,107 error scenes). Bug reference doc enables systematic debugging. LeRobot conversion pipeline fully functional (validated on boss_lerobot v2.1 + oss_single_lerobot v3.0). Portable collection scripts now support dynamic task discovery from task_registry.yaml (eliminates multi-file maintenance). Battery research roadmap targets domain-invariant representations for cross-chemistry transfer (Li-ion → Na-ion/Zn-ion). Completed major milestones across four projects: MIHD NeurIPS paper draft and multimodal fusion diagnosis, MeetingHelper dual-audio transcription upgrade, error-recovery benchmark architecture and NeurIPS outline, BOSS LeRobot pipeline bug fixes, and battery foundation model research
Tasks Architecture & Strategy ✅ Write complete NeurIPS 2026 paper for MIHD benchmark — Created full LaTeX paper (main.tex, neurips_2026.sty, references.bib) with 6 sections + appendix. Core thesis: fusion strategy dominates performance (ARI range 0.16-0.55 across fusions vs <0.05 across encoders). Includes Abstract, Introduction, Framework, Core Results (fusion dominance, scGPT paradox), HD validation, cross-section querying, Discussion. Added Chinese summary. Paper ready for Datasets & Benchmarks track. ✅ Implement MeetingHelper dual audio source transcription — Added –source {mic,system,both} and –system-device parameters via full ccplan workflow (Phases 0-10). Region-based subtitle display (split window with independent cursors per audio source), BlackHole auto-detection, dual transcriber orchestration, dual file output. Fixed single-cursor concurrency bug identified in adversarial audit. • Complete P0/P1 tasks from 04-13 meeting (MIHD project) — Completed 6/11 tasks fully, 2 with code ready, 2 running, 1 failed-resubmitted. Key outcomes: diagnosed Teddy+UNI2 WM degradation (460x variance dilution), collected published baselines (13 methods × 12 sections), extended DE analysis to multi-section batch mode, implemented Bootstrap CI, submitted GPU jobs (RTX 2080 Ti → 5000 Ada escalation), added neighborhood aggregation and RM-IDEAL multi-method comparison flags. 🔄 Generate NeurIPS 2026 RecoverBench paper outline — Created detailed 9-page outline (Abstract + 7 sections) with core narrative: Pi0.5 FT achieves only 3.7% RSR on error scenes vs 60-70% on normal scenes. Filled main results tables (Table 5: 5-task RSR), heatmaps (Fig 4: 24 subtypes), and RBG analysis (Fig 5). Missing: BC-RNN/Pi0.5 Base baselines, threading task eval, exact normal SR data. ✅ Fix 5 critical LeRobot 0.4.0 conversion bugs (BOSS project) — 1) Updated add_frame() signature to require task as separate positional argument. 2) Changed build_paired_mapping.py to source_name:path format for multi-dataset episode mappings. 3) Changed dtype:‘image’ → ‘video’ for MP4 encoding. 4) Wrote validate_lerobot_dataset.py supporting v2.1/v3.0. 5) Removed dataset.create_hf_dataset() from per-episode loop (O(n²) fix). 🔄 Design battery foundation model with domain-invariant degradation indicator — Proposed finding degradation indicator z derivable from V-I data where z=0 represents EOL across all chemistries (Li-ion, Na-ion, Zn-ion). Designed three-phase verification: (1) physical candidate quantities (internal resistance, coulombic loss, dQ/dV peaks), (2) normalization validation across chemistries, (3) contrastive learning encoder. Recommended IJCAI 2024 disentanglement + ICLR 2024 DIOSC papers. ✅ Refactor error-recovery portable scripts for dynamic task discovery — Replaced hardcoded 6-task lists with dynamic parsing from task_registry.yaml in pack_collection_portable.sh and run_collection.sh. Implemented fail-fast asset validation, automatic portable registry generation, and fixed shell SIGPIPE issues. Updated TUTORIAL.md to reflect dynamic system. ✅ Create comprehensive bug fixes documentation (error-recovery) — Created docs/bug_fixes_2026_04_12.md documenting 5 bugs (OSC controller bypass, quaternion format wxyz→xyzw, state_reset persistence, timing/ordering, warp point extraction) with detailed diagnosis commands, expected outputs, thresholds, and success criteria for each bug. ✅ Create and validate DE analysis script for DLPFC clustering — Built scripts/de_analysis_dlpfc.py to validate clustering biological significance. Performs Wilcoxon DE test, uses Hungarian matching for cluster-to-layer alignment, validates marker gene recovery (PCP4/NEFL/TLE4/MBP), generates dot plots and spatial marker plots. Added –sections batch mode. Fixed layer name matching bug (Layer_1 vs Layer1). Implementation & Fixes ✅ Sync error-recovery project between MacBook and tianhe — Used rsync to sync ~270MB (3486 files) from MacBook to tianhe; separately uploaded updated bug_fixes_2026_04_12.md; downloaded and integrated remote updates (11 changed files including per_scene mode, fine-grained subtasks, E10 D1 support). Problems & Solutions Critical Issues 1. Teddy+UNI2 multimodal fusion degrades white matter discrimination: Teddy-only ARI=0.186, concat ARI=0.151 (-19%), mean ARI=0.153 (-18%). Root cause: Teddy arrives pre-L2-normalized in 1024d with per-dim variance 4.3e-5 (460x smaller than PCA 50d at 2.0e-2). After pipeline L2-normalization, gene signal contributes only 13.3% of total variance in concat (2560d) while vision dominates at 86.7%. Solution: Background agent analyzed section 151673 embeddings and discovered variance distribution mismatch. Fix options: PCA-reduce Teddy before fusion, variance-weighted concatenation, or standard scaling instead of L2 normalization.
Key Insight: Pre-normalized high-dimensional embeddings lose signal strength after dimension-agnostic L2 normalization. Fusion strategies must account for input variance distributions. High dimensionality alone doesn’t guarantee discriminative power—variance per dimension is critical.
2. STAIG fusion shows no difference between Teddy and PCA encoders (Teddy avg=0.495, PCA avg=0.467, diff=+0.028 within noise std=0.119). All gene encoder comparisons using STAIG fusion appear invalid. Solution: Code inspection revealed models/Fusion.py line 1253: staig_fusion passes gene_feat=staig_gene_feat_3000.npz (raw HVG), completely ignoring the gene_emb argument. Both Teddy+STAIG and PCA+STAIG use identical inputs. Observed ARI differences are stochastic GCN training noise, not systematic gene encoder effects.
Key Insight: STAIG fusion architectural bug: gene encoder parameter is a no-op. All gene encoders collapse to raw HVG input regardless of specified encoder. This invalidates comparative evaluation of gene encoders when using STAIG as fusion method.
3. Single cursor (_current_line_start) in MeetingHelper cannot track two independent partial lines (mic and system audio), causing text corruption and overwrites during concurrent transcription output. Solution: Refactored to region-based architecture: split subtitle window into two zones (MIC/SYS), each with independent cursor, file handle, partial line buffer, and finalization logic. Adversarial audit by Opus subagent caught this fatal concurrency bug before implementation.
Key Insight: Concurrent transcription output requires isolated state management per source. Shared mutable state (cursor, buffer) is the root cause of concurrency bugs. ccplan adversarial audit phase prevents implementing fundamentally broken architectures.
4. Portable collection scripts hardcoded 6 tasks across pack/run scripts and tutorial, requiring manual updates in multiple files for each new task addition. Solution: Centralized task definition in task_registry.yaml and made all scripts dynamically derive task lists. Implemented automatic portable registry generation during packaging with fail-fast asset validation (task_config, HDF5, scenes).
Key Insight: Centralized configuration with dynamic derivation eliminates multi-file consistency problems and scales better than manual synchronization across scattered hardcoded lists.
5. Five interrelated bugs in error-recovery pipeline caused 100% MimicGen augmentation failure (0/10 success rate): controller cache desync, quaternion format mismatch, leading zero-action frames, missing settling mechanism, and demo validation order. Solution: Systematically fixed all 5 bugs in causal chain: (1) controller sync after state modification, (2) quaternion wxyz→xyzw conversion, (3) trim leading zero-action frames, (4) add settling mechanism, (5) validate demo before augmentation. Success rate improved to 40% (4/10). Fixes applied to recovery mainline but only partially propagated to training source data pipeline.
Key Insight: Complex system failures are rarely single-point issues but cascading effects of multiple subtle bugs. Requires systematic identification and repair of entire causal chain. Code reuse across dependency paths requires explicit tracking to ensure fixes propagate everywhere.
6. libero2lerobot_parallel.py slows down drastically after processing many episodes (O(n²) performance degradation as dataset grows). Solution: Removed dataset.hf_dataset = dataset.create_hf_dataset() from the per-episode loop (line 224). This call rescans all existing parquet files, causing O(n²) cumulative cost as dataset grows. save_episode() already persists data; hf_dataset rebuild only needed at end if dataset will be read.
Key Insight: Incremental data operations should avoid full dataset reconstruction in loops. Always profile long-running pipelines for hidden O(n²) patterns—particularly operations that ‘rescan from scratch’ on each iteration.
7. HD basic_contrastive and QFormer fusion failed with CUDA illegal memory access and OOM errors on RTX 2080 Ti (11GB VRAM). RTX 2080 Ti insufficient for HD 17,502 spots × 550 epochs. Solution: Resubmitted jobs to gpu-common partition with explicit GPU constraint gres=gpu:5000_ada:1 (24GB VRAM), increased memory to 64GB, and extended time limit to 2 days. Separated QFormer into dedicated experiment config to isolate memory-intensive fusions.
Key Insight: Large-scale spatial data (17K+ spots) requires ≥24GB VRAM for contrastive learning. SLURM GPU type specification syntax: –gres=gpu:: (e.g., gres=gpu:5000_ada:1) to request specific GPU models within heterogeneous partitions.
8. LeRobot 0.4.0 updated add_frame() signature to require task as separate positional argument instead of embedding in frame dict, breaking existing conversion code. Solution: Modified worker function to return task_instruction separately; changed add_frame call from add_frame(frame_data) to add_frame(frame_data, task=task_instruction); removed task injection into frame dict.
Key Insight: API breaking changes in dependencies require signature inspection and adaptation. Old pattern of embedding metadata in data dict replaced by explicit parameters in LeRobot 0.4.0.
Human vs AI Approaches Strategic Level Cross-chemistry battery foundation model approach Role Approach Human Intuited need for domain-invariant degradation indicator z where z=0 represents EOL universally; proposed learning when z reaches 0 rather than learning degradation rate (slope). Focus on endpoint alignment across chemistries. AI Connected human’s intuition to formal domain generalization theory (DANN, disentangled representations); identified physical candidates for z (SOH, internal resistance, coulombic loss, dQ/dV peaks); designed three-phase verification protocol (physics → normalization → learned representations). Added feasibility constraints and identified pitfalls (extrapolation risk, data imbalance). Difference Analysis: Human’s domain insight (endpoint invariance) mapped to ML literature by AI. Human focused on conceptual invariance; AI provided systematic validation roadmap and practical implementation constraints. Complementary strengths: human domain intuition + AI methodological rigor.
Paper writing approach: outline-first vs immediate drafting Role Approach Human User explicitly requested outline first (‘我要一个大纲先’) before proceeding to full paper, showing preference for staged approval and review at checkpoints for large deliverables. AI AI initially started exploration to write paper directly. After human clarification, AI adapted to create comprehensive outline, get approval, then execute full implementation in structured phases. Difference Analysis: Human preferred staged gate reviews for large deliverables (38KB paper) to ensure alignment before significant effort and reduce rework risk. AI default was exploratory-then-implement. Human’s phased approach is more efficient for high-stakes documents requiring structural consensus.
Intent articulation for dual audio source requirement (MeetingHelper) Role Approach Human User expressed need in casual natural language: ‘我想要录进去 meeting system 的声音，然后得到要用 transcript’ (non-technical, ambiguous phrasing without specifying simultaneous vs either-or). AI ccplan Phase 0 intent calibration: presented 3 concrete options (simultaneous dual-channel / system-only / either-or) with technical trade-offs, forcing user to clarify exact requirement before any implementation. Difference Analysis: Humans express needs in fuzzy natural language; AI uses structured questioning to calibrate ambiguous intent into precise technical requirements. This prevents implementing the wrong solution. ccplan workflow excels at disambiguation.
Task prioritization and GPU resource allocation strategy Role Approach Human User insisted on retrying failed GPU jobs with larger GPUs (A5000, A6000, 5000 Ada) rather than accepting RTX 2080 Ti failures as blocking issues. Directly asked ‘你用更好的 GPU 试试？’ after seeing OOM errors. AI AI initially reported GPU failures as completion blockers without proactively suggesting alternative GPU types. Only after user prompt did AI check available GPU partitions (sinfo) and create new SLURM scripts with explicit GPU constraints. Difference Analysis: Human prioritizes resource escalation over task abandonment; AI defaults to reporting constraints rather than exploring cluster resources. User’s domain knowledge of HPC cluster heterogeneity drove solution. AI should proactively check available resources before declaring blockers.
Comprehensiveness of bug fix verification (error-recovery) Role Approach Human Human asked ‘is training source data mimicgen augmentation also fixed?’, demonstrating holistic understanding of system dependency chains and potential for fixes to not propagate across all code paths. AI AI initially focused only on recovery mainline fix status, expanding to training source data pipeline only after human follow-up. Discovered partial missing fixes (collect_success_from_error.py and generate_success_from_error.py). Difference Analysis: Human excels at architectural-level thinking about ‘which other modules might be affected’. AI tends to focus on directly asked scope. Highlights human advantage in systematic dependency analysis. AI should proactively map shared code paths during verification.
AI Limitations Critical Limitations Did not proactively suggest GPU resource escalation after initial RTX 2080 Ti failures (HD basic_contrastive CUDA error, QFormer OOM). Only checked sinfo for alternative GPU types after explicit user prompt ‘你用更好的 GPU 试试？’. Should immediately explore cluster resources and propose A6000/5000 Ada options when encountering resource-related failures. When analyzing bug fix status in error-recovery, AI initially did not proactively identify the risk of ‘multiple code paths sharing underlying implementation but fixes not fully synchronized’. Required human prompting (‘is training source data also fixed?’) to expand analysis scope and discover partial missing fixes. Adversarial audit requires independent subagent for effective ‘red-blue confrontation’. During ccplan Phase 5, AI cannot perform adversarial review alone—needs dual perspectives. Must spawn Opus subagent as critic to uncover design flaws (e.g., single-cursor concurrency bug in MeetingHelper that would have been missed without adversarial review). Hit API 429 rate limit errors during battery foundation model discussion, interrupting critical research conversation about domain-invariant degradation indicators. When encountering sandbox permission limits in BOSS performance diagnosis, AI spent excessive tokens trying various technical workarounds (different find commands, path variants, privilege methods) instead of promptly explaining the issue to user and requesting help or strategy adjustment. Required three rounds of clarifying questions to nail down implementation scope for portable script refactor (full-chain vs partial, auto-sync vs manual, fail-fast vs warn). Could not infer complete requirements from initial vague request ‘put new tasks into portable scripts’. General Limitations Did not proactively suggest workflow optimization (auto-approval settings) despite repeated user confirmations indicating friction. Required explicit user request to address systemic inefficiency in MIHD project exploratory work. Learnings Key Learnings NeurIPS Datasets & Benchmarks track paper structure: 9-page main body with Methods in appendix, ML-oriented language, emphasis on systematic evaluation over algorithmic novelty. Core contribution: fusion strategy dominance (ARI range 0.16-0.55 across fusions vs <0.05 across encoders). Strong ‘difficulty proof’ essential: Pi0.5 FT 3.7% RSR vs 60-70% normal SR catastrophic gap. Pre-normalized high-dimensional embeddings lose discriminative power in dimension-agnostic fusion: Teddy 1024d pre-L2-normalized has 460x less per-dim variance than PCA 50d; after pipeline L2-normalization, gene signal is invisible in concat/mean with UNI2 1536d. Solution: variance-weighted fusion, PCA-reduce high-dim embeddings before fusion, or standard scaling instead of L2 norm. ccplan workflow prevents ‘implementing the wrong thing’ and ‘missing critical edge cases’. Multi-stage pipeline (intent calibration → hypothesis validation → adversarial audit → review gate) caught 9 issues in MeetingHelper including fatal single-cursor concurrency bug before implementation. Structured divergence + convergence superior to straight-line coding. Adversarial audit by independent Opus subagent is essential—cannot self-critique effectively. CCPlan (Evolving Constraint Planning) workflow scales well for multi-task projects: 11 P0/P1 tasks organized into 3 waves (immediate → analysis → refinement) with parallel agent execution and structured ECL document tracking. Key: continuous execution mandate (phases flow without user prompts) and AskUserQuestion tool for checkpoints. Multi-agent exploration pattern for complex codebases: launch 3 parallel agents (results, methodology, plans) with targeted prompts, then synthesize findings into structured deliverable. Effective for large projects with distributed documentation. Used successfully for MIHD paper writing. Cross-chemistry battery degradation modeling can leverage CV domain generalization: disentangle domain-invariant (z ~ degradation state) from domain-specific (chemistry) representations. Physical anchoring (internal resistance, coulombic loss, dQ/dV peaks) provides interpretable initialization for learned z. Three-phase verification: physics candidates → normalization validation → contrastive learning encoder. LeRobot 0.4.0 dtype semantics: ‘image’=bytes inline in parquet (PNG per frame), ‘video’=MP4 in videos/ directory. meta.video_keys only returns dtype:‘video’ features. For large datasets, always use dtype:‘video’ for efficient MP4 encoding rather than storing PNG bytes per frame. Incremental data pipelines must avoid O(n²) operations: rebuilding entire dataset after each element adds cumulative cost. libero2lerobot_parallel’s dataset.create_hf_dataset() in loop caused dramatic slowdown after ~1000 episodes. Always profile long-running pipelines for hidden O(n²) patterns—particularly operations that ‘rescan from scratch’. Centralized configuration with dynamic derivation scales better than scattered hardcoded lists. Fail-fast validation at build time prevents incomplete artifacts better than runtime warnings. Applied in error-recovery portable scripts: task_registry.yaml as single source of truth eliminates multi-file consistency problems. STAIG fusion architectural insight: gene_emb parameter is ignored in models/Fusion.py line 1253; fusion always uses raw HVG 3000d from staig_gene_feat_3000.npz. This explains why Teddy+STAIG and PCA+STAIG have identical performance (diff within noise). All gene encoder comparisons using STAIG are invalid. DE analysis validation workflow: load raw adata (not HVG subset) → run rank_genes_groups with wilcoxon → use Hungarian matching to align predicted clusters to ground truth layers → check marker gene ranks in expected layers. Key markers for DLPFC: PCP4/NEFL (Layer 5), MBP/MOBP (WM), CARTPT (Layer 3). Poor marker recovery can reflect genuine biological clustering difficulty, not just algorithmic failure—always validate GT baseline. Bootstrap confidence intervals essential for small-n comparisons: STAIG 10-section avg ARI=0.495 [0.387, 0.597] vs PCA=0.265 [0.230, 0.296] shows non-overlapping CIs confirming significance. Implemented via utils/statistics.py with n=1000 resampling; 95% CI = (percentile_2.5, percentile_97.5). SLURM GPU type specification syntax: use –gres=gpu:: format (e.g., gres=gpu:5000_ada:1, gres=gpu:a6000:1) to request specific GPU models within heterogeneous partitions. gpu-common and scavenger-gpu both have mixed GPU types; explicit constraint prevents allocation to underpowered GPUs. RTX 2080 Ti (11GB) insufficient for HD spatial data; need ≥24GB VRAM. Complex system failures are rarely single-point issues but cascading effects of multiple subtle bugs. error-recovery pipeline: 5 interrelated bugs (controller cache, quaternion, zero-action frames, settling, validation order) caused 100% failure. Requires systematic identification and repair of entire causal chain. Code reuse across dependency paths requires explicit tracking to ensure fixes propagate. Region-based UI architecture ideal for concurrent output: when multiple async data sources need to update the same UI simultaneously, allocating independent regions (cursor, buffer, draw logic) per source is cleaner and less error-prone than shared state + locks. Applied in MeetingHelper dual audio transcription. Practical Learnings Project-level Claude Code settings (.claude/settings.json) can dramatically improve iteration speed for exploratory work by using global ‘Bash’ allow with targeted ‘deny’ list for destructive ops (rm, rmdir, git push –force, git reset –hard, git clean -f). Settings propagate to all subdirectories and sessions. Applied in MIHD project. Shell SIGPIPE handling requires care when downstream commands (head, grep -m) exit early. Use dedicated extraction functions or process substitution to avoid broken pipe errors polluting output. Fixed in error-recovery run_collection.sh by replacing pipeline with direct first-match function. Conversation Summaries MIHD ✅ Write complete NeurIPS 2026 paper for MIHD benchmark 21:12:23.748 | claude_code User requested NeurIPS paper writing with outline first. Launched 3 parallel agents to explore results, methodology, and plans. Created comprehensive outline targeting NeurIPS D&B Track (9 pages + appendix). After user approval, implemented full LaTeX paper: main.tex (38KB, 6 sections + Appendix), neurips_2026.sty, references.bib (18 citations). Core thesis: fusion strategy dominates performance. Also created Chinese summary. All tasks completed. LaTeX not available on HPC cluster; files ready for Overleaf or local compilation.
🔄 P0/P1 task completion via CCPlan workflow 20:52:25.736 | claude_code User requested completion of 11 P0+P1 tasks from 04-13 meeting plan. Deployed CCPlan skill with 3-wave execution: (1) immediate code+GPU jobs, (2) analysis (DE diagnosis, WM degradation root cause via agent, published baselines via agent), (3) query improvements. Submitted 3 SLURM GPU jobs; 2 failed on RTX 2080 Ti, resubmitted to 5000 Ada. Key findings: Teddy pre-L2-norm causes 460x variance dilution vs PCA; STAIG fusion ignores gene_emb (architectural bug); published baselines collected (13 methods × 12 sections). Final status: 6/11 fully complete, 2 code-ready, 2 running, 1 failed-resubmitted. Deliverables: docs/meeting_0413_tasks.md report, docs/ecl/p0_p1_completion.yaml, 6 modified Python files, 2 new SLURM scripts.
✅ Create DE analysis script and validate DLPFC clustering 02:36:33.586 | claude_code Implemented scripts/de_analysis_dlpfc.py to validate biological significance of PCA+UNI2+STAIG clustering results. Script performs Wilcoxon DE test, uses Hungarian matching for cluster-layer alignment, validates marker gene recovery, generates dot plots and spatial visualizations. Tasks Architecture & Strategy 🔄 MIHD P0 task planning (HD experiments, ARI data collection, DE analysis) — Plan implementation for 3 P0 blocking tasks for MIHD spatial transcriptomics paper. Discovered HD GPU experiments already completed. Need to collect per-section ARI data from published methods and implement DE analysis pipeline. ✅ MimicGen augmentation compatibility diagnosis — Diagnosed why SpaceMouse demos cannot be augmented by MimicGen while MimicGen-generated demos can. Revealed datagen_info metadata requirements and clean vs error state semantic assumptions. ✅ Recovery Pipeline comprehensive documentation — Created 600+ line technical reference doc covering RBG definitions, MimicGen augmentation, data flow, and pipeline stages. Updated project summary Recovery section from 40 to 100 lines. Added cross-references. ✅ LiPM battery foundation model literature review — Analyzed LiPM (KDD 2025) paper’s core contributions (MMAE, CIR, dual-scale encoder). Recommended 30+ papers across 6 dimensions: battery foundation models, physics-informed fusion, irregular time series, transfer learning, time series foundation models, enhancement techniques. Implementation & Fixes ✅ Training source demo dual-path documentation — Created documentation clarifying RecoveryDemo (human collection) vs AugmentedRecovery (MimicGen augmentation) dual pipelines. Iterated twice to adjust tone from ‘correction-style’ to ’team onboarding’. Specified exact file paths for both chains. Problems & Solutions Critical Issues 1. SpaceMouse-collected demos cannot be augmented by MimicGen while MimicGen-generated demos can Solution: Revealed MimicGen requires complete datagen_info metadata (eef_pose, object_poses, target_pose, gripper_action, subtask_term_signals) and clean initial state semantics. SpaceMouse demos typically start from error states and lack required metadata. Identified two separate demo pipelines: recovery demo (simplified format) vs success demo (MimicGen-compatible format).
Key Insight: Format compatibility is not just about file structure - it includes semantic assumptions (clean state vs error state) and metadata completeness. Recovery demos follow a different augmentation pipeline than standard MimicGen source demos.
2. Confusion about ’training source demo’ terminology across different pipeline contexts Solution: Clarified that in recovery context, ’training source demo’ means ’training recovery demo’ (RecoveryDemo + AugmentedRecovery), distinct from MimicGen’s success_demos.hdf5. Created clear documentation mapping: outputs/recovery/demos/ for human-collected RecoveryDemo, outputs/recovery/augmented/ for MimicGen-style augmented demos.
Key Insight: Technical terminology can have project-specific meanings. In multi-pipeline projects, explicit context is required to avoid mapping errors. The prepared_source/*.hdf5 files are dependencies for augmentation, not the training source demos themselves.
General Issues 3. Documentation tone inappropriate for target audience (team onboarding) Solution: Rewrote training_source_demo_dual_path.md twice. First version had ‘correction-style’ tone because it emerged from debugging a conceptual misunderstanding. Second version reframed as ’explaining pipeline to teammates’ with clear flow, file paths, and current status snapshot.
Key Insight: Documentation generated during problem-solving tends to inherit the debugging narrative. Need explicit audience-awareness: technical reference vs team introduction require different framing and level of detail.
Human vs AI Approaches Strategic Level MimicGen datagen_info requirements discovery Role Approach Human Noticed practical problem: ‘This demo works but that demo doesn’t’ and asked ‘what’s the difference?’. Continued probing until the full picture emerged. AI Performed systematic code archaeology: read 8 framework modules, 5 pipeline scripts, validation helpers, MimicGen base classes, and subtask termination docs. Traced from user-facing scripts down to DataGenerator’s get_datagen_info() method to uncover the contract. Difference Analysis: Human recognized the anomaly through hands-on experience. AI needed explicit direction to the problem area, then excelled at exhaustive technical investigation. Human’s iterative questioning kept AI focused on the practical gap rather than getting lost in technical details.
Terminology mapping in project-specific context Role Approach Human Knew from project context that ’training source demo’ specifically meant ’training recovery demo’ in their pipeline, corrected AI’s interpretation immediately when mismatch detected. AI Initially mapped ’training source demo’ to the most common technical meaning: MimicGen’s success_demos.hdf5. Required explicit correction to understand the project-specific usage. Once corrected, quickly reorganized understanding and rewrote documentation. Difference Analysis: Human has implicit project context and domain-specific terminology understanding. AI defaults to common technical definitions without project-specific context, but adapts quickly once corrected. Demonstrates AI’s strength in information reorganization after ground truth is established.
Implementation Level Documentation audience and tone correction Role Approach Human Immediately recognized inappropriate tone: ‘your file’s tone is strange, I need to explain my pipeline to teammates who don’t know it’. Single sentence correction. AI Initially wrote documentation in ’term correction’ style because it emerged from resolving a conceptual misunderstanding. Required explicit feedback to reframe from ‘here’s what was wrong’ to ‘here’s how the pipeline works’. Difference Analysis: Human has intuitive sense of audience and communication context. AI treats documentation as knowledge artifact without inherent audience model - tends to document its own reasoning process unless explicitly redirected to target audience’s information needs.
AI Limitations Critical Limitations Concept mapping error: interpreted ’training source demo’ as MimicGen’s success_demos.hdf5 instead of project-specific ’training recovery demo’. Required explicit human correction despite extensive codebase context. Over-reliance on common definitions: without explicit project context, AI defaults to most frequent usage of technical terms rather than inferring project-specific meanings from surrounding code patterns. General Limitations Audience-inappropriate documentation tone: generated ‘correction narrative’ style docs when task required ’team onboarding’ style. Needed explicit feedback to reframe for target audience. Learnings Key Learnings MimicGen source demos require complete datagen_info metadata (eef_pose, object_poses, target_pose, gripper_action, subtask_term_signals) and semantic assumptions (clean initial state, complete task trajectory, valid subtask transitions). Recovery demos from error states need separate augmentation pipeline. Format compatibility encompasses three layers: file structure (NPZ vs HDF5), metadata completeness (datagen_info fields), and semantic assumptions (starting state, task coverage). A file can pass structural checks but fail semantic requirements. In multi-pipeline projects, technical terminology needs explicit project-specific definition. ‘Source demo’ can mean different things in recovery pipeline (RecoveryDemo) vs standard MimicGen pipeline (success_demos.hdf5). Documentation should establish clear namespace. LiPM paper demonstrates physics-informed pretraining for battery tasks: MMAE for cross-modal coupling, CIR for coulombic integration (charge conservation), dual-scale encoders for irregular sampling. Key insight: domain-specific foundation models outperform generic time series models when physical constraints are properly encoded. Practical Learnings Documentation generated during problem-solving inherits the debugging narrative. For team onboarding docs, need explicit reframing: state current design, not the path to discovering it. Audience awareness must be externally injected, not emergent.
