profiled 13+ researchers (Sergey Levine h-index 166, Pieter Abbeel h-index 164, Chelsea Finn, Yuke Zhu, Yunzhu Li, Mingyu Ding, Ruoshi Liu, Xiaolong Wang, Shuran Song), identified PhD advisors, generated detailed trajectory reports with breakthrough papers and research themes, discovered Open X-Embodiment, MineDojo (NeurIPS 2022 Best Paper), Value Iteration Networks (NeurIPS 2016 Best Paper) ✅ Implement homepage-based student discovery — Added source/status fields to StudentCandidate model, created research/homepage_discovery.py with HTMLParser-based text extraction and LLM student name extraction, restructured discover_students() to use homepage-first + co-authorship supplement strategy with polite rate limiting (1 req/2s) ✅ Update comprehensive documentation (CLAUDE.md, TUTORIAL.md, README.md) — Rewrote root CLAUDE.md with MCP server docs and sub-project references, updated summarize/CLAUDE.md for new import structure, rewrote research/CLAUDE.md covering profiler architecture and unified CLI, completely rewrote Chinese TUTORIAL.md from 10 to 13 chapters adding multi-source search/profiler/citations sections and 4 new workflow examples, updated README.md with missing tools Implementation & Fixes ✅ Deploy researcher profiles to separate Hugo section — Created website/content/research/ section for researcher profiles (separate from bugJournal temporal reports), added deploy_to_hugo() function in research/output.py, updated config.yml with research menu item, added –deploy and –hugo-site CLI flags ✅ CLAUDE.md analysis and similar projects research — Analyzed CalendarPro CLAUDE.md (comprehensive, no changes needed), researched similar open-source projects (FluidCalendar, CoPaw, Khoj, OpenDAN, Cal.com, Leon, Rasa, Home Assistant), concluded CalendarPro’s energy-aware scheduling + three-tier architecture + dual verification combination is unique ✅ Fix mcp_server.py import error — Fixed function name mismatch: globally replaced _load_known_arxiv_ids with _load_known_paper_ids in 4 locations across import statements and call sites Problems & Solutions Critical Issues 1. Co-authorship analysis returned 0 students for all tested professors (Sergey Levine, Pieter Abbeel, Chelsea Finn) despite correct implementation Solution: Human recognized faculty homepages explicitly list students; implemented homepage-first discovery with LLM extraction of student names from faculty pages, using co-authorship data only as supplementary enrichment with source tracking for transparency
Key Insight: Faculty homepages are the authoritative source for student lists and more reliable than inferring relationships from co-authorship patterns. When heuristic algorithms fail due to data sparsity, switch to direct information sources (professor webpages) rather than debugging scoring algorithms. Ground truth beats inference.
2. Semantic Scholar matched wrong researchers for 6+ names due to name ambiguity (Ming Yu→explosives chemist, Xiaolong Wang→veterinary scientist/geologist/NLP researcher, 5 out of 6 initial queries failed) Solution: Human provided full name clarification (Mingyu Ding); relied on ArXiv paper matching which was more accurate; AI correctly identified data quality issues by recognizing incompatible research domains, returned explicit warnings rather than generating false trajectories; recommended ORCID+institution verification for common Chinese names
Key Insight: Common Chinese names create severe disambiguation problems in academic databases requiring mandatory institution/ORCID matching. Same-name aggregation is a critical data quality issue - need field-based clustering or institution filtering before analysis. Multi-source data integration (homepage + ArXiv + S2) with explicit source tracking provides robustness against individual failures. Better to return ‘data unusable’ than propagate false profiles.
3. Output files scattered across tool-specific subdirectories (summarize/logs/, research/cache/, test/benchmark_results.csv) with hardcoded Path(file).parent patterns created tight coupling and difficult disk space management Solution: Designed unified outputs/ structure organized by type (reports/, logs/, cache/, data/) then by tool, introduced canonical path constants in common/paths.py, replaced all hardcoded paths atomically, updated environment variable fallbacks
Key Insight: Type-first organization (reports/, logs/, cache/) is more maintainable than tool-first when multiple tools share similar output patterns. Centralized path management enables atomic refactoring - changing one constant propagates everywhere without breaking code. Phased execution with verification checkpoints prevents cascading failures.
4. Two overlapping research tools in same directory (monolithic research_scout.py + modular profiler package) causing code duplication, unclear single entry point, and undocumented features. Initial /init attempt interrupted by user suggesting AI was heading toward documentation improvements rather than architectural changes. Solution: User provided explicit 9-step integration plan with precise function signatures, line counts, and architectural decisions. AI executed plan: kept research_scout.py as stable CLI entry point, refactored modular package as importable library, added profile/citations subcommands, implemented three-stage evaluation with citation analysis, added multi-backend LLM support
Key Insight: For complex architectural changes, detailed written plans prevent scope creep and ensure AI stays on track. Hybrid integration (preserving stable CLI while refactoring internals) reduces migration risk vs full rewrites. Human architectural vision + AI tactical execution works better than letting AI improvise. Gradual migration is safer than big-bang changes.
5. Major new modular tool (researcher profiler with ~15 Python modules) completely undocumented in CLAUDE.md causing confusion about codebase capabilities; AI initially missed ‘New feature’ directory entirely Solution: Used Agent exploration to thoroughly map package structure, then rewrote CLAUDE.md to document both tools with separate sections for architecture, usage, and configuration
Key Insight: Documentation rot happens rapidly when new features are added without updating guidance files; requires proactive documentation maintenance or automated detection of undocumented code paths. Update CLAUDE.md immediately after structural changes to prevent drift.
6. JSON parsing failed for Yuke Zhu trajectory analysis after three-tier LLM repair attempts (haiku → sonnet → opus), resulting in incomplete reports Solution: Accepted partial results (paper list without trajectory analysis) and documented the failure; analysis completed with degraded output quality
Key Insight: LLM repair escalation has limits; when all three model tiers fail, underlying issue may be prompt/context rather than model capability. Persistent failures indicate need to revisit input structure or question decomposition.
7. arXiv search returned 0 results for robotics leaders despite using correct author names due to sorting/recency issues in search API Solution: Switched from arXiv search API to Researcher Profiler which uses both arXiv and Semantic Scholar data sources for more robust multi-source fetching
Key Insight: API search behavior is not always intuitive; multi-source data fetching is more robust than single-API queries. Don’t debug API quirks when better data sources exist.
General Issues 8. Researcher profile reports initially deployed to bugJournal section mixing profiles with daily scout reports; lack of semantic content separation Solution: Human pointed out profiles should go to separate research/ folder; created new website/content/research/ section with dedicated menu item and _index.md; updated deploy_to_hugo() to target new section
Key Insight: Different content types benefit from separate Hugo sections for better organization and navigation; researcher profiles are reference material while bugJournal is temporal reporting. Semantic taxonomy matters for content discoverability.
9. Research profiler failed with ‘No module named research’ when running research_scout.py directly Solution: Changed execution method from ‘python research/research_scout.py profile’ to ‘python -m research analyze’ to properly respect package structure
Key Insight: Complex Python packages need correct entry points; using -m flag ensures proper module resolution for packages with internal imports
10. pip install -e . failed with Windows file lock on gadget-mcp.exe during package reinstallation Solution: Verified that common/ package imports work directly from sys.path since editable install already has package root available; skipped reinstall and proceeded
Key Insight: Editable installs add project root to sys.path, so new packages are immediately importable without reinstalling when executable is locked
Human vs AI Approaches Strategic Level Student discovery methodology Role Approach Human Recognized that faculty homepages explicitly list students and proposed homepage scraping as primary method after co-authorship analysis failed, applying domain knowledge that professors maintain authoritative student rosters AI Initially implemented only co-authorship analysis based on Semantic Scholar data with 4-dimensional scoring (first-author 40%, time concentration 25%, collaboration frequency 20%, recency 15%); would have continued debugging scoring algorithm rather than switching approaches Difference Analysis: Human directly identified the most reliable information source based on real-world knowledge of academic practices, while AI relied on quantitative signals from publication metadata. Human knew where ground truth lives; AI defaulted to available data APIs and statistical inference. This highlights human advantage in knowing authoritative sources vs AI’s strength in processing structured data patterns.
CalendarPro root cause diagnosis Role Approach Human User described high-level symptoms (‘overall experience poor’, ‘intent understanding inaccurate’, ’task completion unreliable’) and specified using Claude Local CLI (strong model), wanting ‘comprehensive improvement’ AI Immediately performed structured root cause analysis decomposing system-level issues into 7 specific technical problems across intent classification layer (no confidence threshold, LLM timeout, no learning) and task execution layer (no provider retry, crude token estimation, incomplete agent implementation) Difference Analysis: User provided symptoms and context; AI systematically debugged complex system to identify actionable technical problems. AI demonstrated strength in systematic debugging where humans see overall dysfunction, recognizing the problem is not in the model but in pipeline design.
Architectural integration strategy Role Approach Human User specified ‘Keep research_scout.py as unified entry point. Import from modular package as library code.’ Preserves monolithic entry while leveraging modular code. Provided detailed 9-step plan with specific file paths and line counts. AI Initially attempted to improve existing CLAUDE.md documentation without proposing architectural changes. Only after receiving explicit integration plan did AI execute unification. Would have focused on incremental documentation improvements. Difference Analysis: Human saw strategic value of unified interface for better UX and provided prescriptive specification. AI focused on incremental improvements and needed architectural guidance for cross-cutting changes. Human provided ‘what’, AI provided ‘how’.
Citation scoring approach for paper evaluation Role Approach Human Specified citation count should be used for sorting/tiebreaking only, not influence composite relevance score; recognized popularity is orthogonal to project relevance AI Initially proposed making citation count part of composite evaluation score, conflating ‘important paper’ with ‘relevant to project’ Difference Analysis: Human recognized highly-cited papers may not match project focus; AI defaulted to treating citation count as quality signal rather than discovery hint. Reveals AI’s tendency to optimize for global importance rather than context-specific relevance.
Researcher trajectory analysis depth Role Approach Human Provided raw paper lists with basic metadata (title, year, citations) and requested high-level trajectory analysis AI Synthesized comprehensive multi-paragraph narratives covering phase transitions, breakthrough papers with ‘why not before’ reasoning explaining historical context, research themes, and methodology evolution Difference Analysis: Human requested analysis at high level; AI generated multi-dimensional synthesis including causal reasoning about why breakthroughs happened when they did. AI went beyond pattern recognition to historical contextualization.
Researcher name disambiguation Role Approach Human Immediately recognized ‘Ming Yu’ was too generic and provided full name ‘Mingyu Ding’ when reviewing erroneous results; knew common Chinese names would have disambiguation issues AI Accepted first Semantic Scholar match without questioning correctness; flagged data quality issue only after seeing obviously wrong papers (explosives chemistry, veterinary science) Difference Analysis: Human preemptively knew common names need disambiguation; AI detected problem only post-hoc from result inspection. Human applied prior knowledge of systemic issues; AI relied on empirical detection.
When to trigger citation analysis in report pipeline Role Approach Human Always run automatically for top-5 papers; prioritized convenience over explicit control based on actual usage patterns (would be wanted in most cases) AI Proposed making citation analysis opt-in via –citations flag; defaulted to explicit user choice for potentially expensive operations Difference Analysis: Human considered real-world workflow friction while AI focused on theoretical flexibility and cost optimization. Human prioritized reducing friction in common workflow over architectural purity.
Refactoring plan execution approach Role Approach Human Provided detailed multi-phase plan with specific file paths, line counts, expected changes, and explicit checkpoints - prescribed exact steps AI Started by reading files in parallel to understand current state before making changes, verified import chain worked after each phase, added verification steps and parallel I/O optimization not in original plan Difference Analysis: Human used prescriptive specification with clear checkpoints; AI added verification steps and parallelization. Both valued systematic phased execution over atomic big-bang changes. Human’s detailed plan eliminated ambiguity and ensured consistent implementation.
Hugo content organization Role Approach Human Specified researcher profiles should go in new research/ section, not bugJournal/, understanding semantic difference between reference material and temporal reporting AI Would have followed existing pattern of deploying to bugJournal/ since that’s where research_scout.py reports go, seeing both as ‘research content’ Difference Analysis: Human distinguished between temporal content (daily reports) and reference content (researcher profiles) requiring separate sections; AI saw both as same category and would have lumped them together. Human understood content taxonomy for navigation.
Implementation Level Handling missing conference award data Role Approach Human Expected AI might have award information in training data; asked AI to identify papers with conference awards AI Recognized knowledge limitation - could not confirm specific awards with high confidence, returned empty list with explanation rather than guessing, maintained conservative ‘unknown’ response Difference Analysis: Human assumed AI training data might include award info; AI understood uncertainty boundaries and chose not to propagate potentially false information. Shows AI understanding of when to say ‘I don’t know’.
Documentation language choice Role Approach Human Explicitly requested ‘Now please use Chinese to update tutorial.md’ after code implementation, recognizing target audience (Chinese researchers) needed native-language tutorial AI Had been using English for code/comments but immediately switched to comprehensive Chinese documentation when requested, defaulting to English for technical docs Difference Analysis: Human recognized end-user needs based on target audience; AI used neutral default. Reflects user’s understanding of user demographics vs AI’s language-agnostic approach.
AI Limitations Critical Limitations Failed to proactively detect Semantic Scholar name disambiguation issues in initial researcher analyses - accepted obviously wrong matches (explosives chemistry, veterinary science, geology) and required human-observable symptoms or explicit clarification to trigger error detection. Did not question matches despite domain mismatches. Required explicit 9-step integration plan to execute complex architectural changes; initial /init attempt was interrupted by user suggesting AI was heading toward documentation improvements rather than structural refactoring. Needed architectural guidance for cross-cutting changes. Initially designed co-authorship-only student discovery without considering that faculty homepages are the authoritative and explicit source for student lists; required human suggestion to switch approaches Initially missed entire ‘New feature’ directory when first analyzing codebase; required explicit user prompt pointing it out before discovering undocumented profiler tool with ~15 Python modules Failed to parse Yuke Zhu’s trajectory analysis JSON even after three-tier LLM repair escalation (haiku → sonnet → opus), resulting in incomplete report with only paper list but no analytical insights Required explicit user correction to understand that researcher profiles belong in separate Hugo section from daily research scout reports, lacking semantic understanding of content taxonomy (reference vs temporal) Proposed citation count as part of composite relevance score without recognizing that popularity and project-specific relevance are orthogonal dimensions; conflated ‘important paper’ with ‘relevant to project’ General Limitations Cannot automatically test end-to-end functionality after large refactoring - verified imports work but did not run actual tool commands with new paths to confirm correctness Cannot reliably verify conference awards (Best Paper, Spotlight, Oral) from training data alone - requires real-time access to official conference websites or paper metadata APIs. Returned empty arrays with disclaimers rather than guessing. Did not propose cost optimization alternatives for citation analysis (e.g., citation_count threshold, opt-in flag); blindly implemented ‘always analyze top-5’ as specified without questioning API cost implications Defaulted to English documentation despite codebase being part of Chinese-language user project; required explicit instruction to switch to Chinese for TUTORIAL.md Cannot automatically aggregate final state across multiple parallel agents; human verification needed to confirm all 230 CalendarPro tests passed after 5 agents completed their work When pip install failed due to Windows file lock, did not suggest alternative approaches like stopping MCP server process or using –user flag until after first failure Defaulted to making citation analysis opt-in (–citations flag) rather than considering usage patterns that would favor automatic execution for convenience Learnings Key Learnings For large architectural refactoring, detailed written plans with function signatures, line counts, and architectural decisions dramatically improve AI execution accuracy and prevent scope creep. Human architectural vision + AI tactical execution works better than AI improvisation. Large-scale refactoring should be parallelized by phase/domain: CalendarPro’s 7 phases implemented by 5 concurrent agents each owning a subset of changes, then integrated with test verification. Phased execution with verification checkpoints prevents cascading failures that are hard to diagnose. Academic profiling systems must implement name disambiguation as first-class concern - common Chinese names create severe disambiguation problems requiring mandatory ORCID/institution matching. Same-name aggregation creates entirely false researcher profiles that appear plausible until domain expertise reveals incompatibility. Better to return ‘data unusable’ than propagate false information. Multi-source data integration (homepage scraping + co-authorship + ArXiv + Semantic Scholar) with explicit source tracking provides robustness against individual data source failures or inaccuracies. Faculty homepages are more authoritative than co-authorship analysis for student discovery as they explicitly list current and former students with status. Re-export shims with lazy imports enable large-scale code refactoring while maintaining backward compatibility and avoiding circular dependency issues. Centralized infrastructure modules (paths.py, cache.py, llm.py) enable atomic refactoring - changing one constant propagates everywhere without breaking downstream dependencies. Modular architecture enables clean integration: keeping research_scout.py as monolithic CLI entry point while treating profiler package as library code separates concerns beautifully. Hybrid integration approaches (preserving stable CLI while refactoring internals) reduce migration risk compared to full rewrites. Academic research analysis tools enable rapid domain landscape understanding: researcher profiler quickly reveals field leaders’ trajectories, current directions, and breakthrough works. Systematic framework for tracking cutting-edge research directions. Type-first organization (reports/, logs/, cache/) is more maintainable than tool-first organization when multiple tools share similar output patterns. Centralized path management enables atomic refactoring across entire codebase. Parameter threading through nested call chains requires backward-compatible defaults (backend=‘claude_cli’) and incremental syntax testing (py_compile) to prevent cascading errors. When adding parameters to deeply-nested call chains, default values preserve backward compatibility. Use parallel Agent exploration for thorough codebase understanding before making architectural decisions; sequential exploration misses connections between components. Documentation files like CLAUDE.md become stale rapidly as features are added; need active maintenance or automated detection of undocumented code paths. Documentation-driven development pays off: updating CLAUDE.md and TUTORIAL.md immediately after code changes prevents doc rot and ensures knowledge transfer to future sessions. Documentation synchronization critical for maintainability. Heuristic algorithms depend critically on data quality: student discovery with Semantic Scholar co-authorship failed when data was sparse; need multiple sources or ground truth (homepages). When heuristics fail, switch to direct information sources rather than debugging scoring algorithms. LLM repair escalation (haiku → sonnet → opus) has limits; persistent JSON parse failures may indicate prompt or context issues rather than model capability problems. When all three model tiers fail, revisit input structure or question decomposition. Always ask users about design tradeoffs (scoring weights, automatic vs opt-in features, performance vs convenience) rather than assuming defaults; user context reveals non-obvious priorities like preferring automatic citation analysis over cost optimization. When extracting shared utilities, best-of-breed merging (taking most robust implementation from each source) produces better results than arbitrary selection. API search behavior is not always intuitive; multi-source data fetching is more robust than single-API queries. Practical Learnings Hugo content organization benefits from semantic separation: reference material (researcher profiles) and temporal content (daily reports) should go in different sections for better navigation and user experience. Python package entry points matter: ‘python -m package’ is more robust than direct script execution for complex module structures with internal imports. Using -m flag ensures proper module resolution. Polite web crawling (1 request per 2 seconds) with rate limiting is essential when scraping academic homepages at scale to avoid being blocked or overloading servers. Conversation Summaries CalendarPro ✅ Comprehensive optimization and similar projects research 21:29:45.386 | claude_code User reported poor CalendarPro experience with intent misunderstanding and task completion failures. AI analyzed codebase, identified 7 root causes (no confidence thresholds, missing retries, crude token estimation, LLM timeout, no learning loop, incomplete agent implementation), designed 7-phase optimization plan addressing intent classification and task execution layers. Executed plan using 5 parallel agents: semantic routing thresholds, hybrid keyword+embedding routing, split system prompts (530 lines → base + 11 fragments), provider retry with exponential backoff, scheduling scoring, auto-learning from mismatches, ThoughtStore caching. All 230 tests passed. Also analyzed existing CLAUDE.md (comprehensive, no changes needed) and researched similar open-source projects (FluidCalendar, CoPaw, Khoj, OpenDAN, Cal.com, Leon, Rasa, Home Assistant), concluding CalendarPro’s energy-aware scheduling + three-tier architecture + dual verification + unified life management is unique combination in open source.
Gadget Research Toolkit ✅ Unified research toolkit integration, documentation, and comprehensive researcher profiling 02:16:04.409 | claude_code Discovered completely undocumented modular researcher profiler tool alongside existing research_scout.py paper discovery script. User requested integration of both tools plus citation graph analysis. AI explored both codebases using parallel Agent tasks, then executed detailed 9-step integration plan: deleted duplicate ‘New feature/’ directory, added Semantic Scholar citation APIs (get_paper_by_id/citations/references), created profile and citations subcommands, implemented three-stage evaluation with citation impact analysis (always for top-5 papers), added multi-backend LLM support (claude_cli/anthropic/openai), completely rewrote Chinese TUTORIAL.md from 10 to 13 chapters. Created robotics-leaders project and profiled 13+ researchers: Sergey Levine (h-index 166, 160K citations), Pieter Abbeel (h-index 164, 177K citations, identified three phases: manipulation/LfD → deep RL revolution with TRPO/GAE/MAML/SAC → generative models/LLM integration), Chelsea Finn (wrong S2 match initially but recognized as Stanford professor), Yuke Zhu (MineDojo NeurIPS 2022 Best Paper), Yunzhu Li (Open X-Embodiment NeurIPS 2023 Best Paper), Mingyu Ding (VRDP, DaViT, CAE, Open X-Embodiment breakthroughs), Ruoshi Liu (Zero-1-to-3, Objaverse-XL, evolution from physics to 3D vision to embodied intelligence), Xiaolong Wang (name disambiguation failure, incompatible fields), Shuran Song (FlingBot, UMI framework, video world models). Successfully identified PhD advisors for six researchers: Mingyu Ding→Jitendra Malik, Ruoshi Liu→Carl Vondrick, Xiaolong Wang→Abhinav Gupta, Shuran Song→Thomas Funkhouser, Yunzhu Li→Antonio Torralba, Yuke Zhu→Li Fei-Fei. Encountered multiple Semantic Scholar name disambiguation issues and JSON parsing failures for some analyses. Updated all CLAUDE.md documentation for root, summarize, and research directories. Result: production-ready unified academic research toolkit with complete paper→researcher→citation workflow.
Gadget Toolkit Infrastructure ✅ Extract common/ package and consolidate output directories 21:37:49.486 | claude_code Implemented comprehensive 4-phase refactoring to consolidate ~400 lines of duplicated LLM calling code, JSON parsing, atomic writes, and config loading from summarize/, research/, test/ into new pip-installable common/ package. Created 6 modules (io, cache, json_utils, llm, hugo, paths) with re-export shims for backward compatibility (summarize/llm_backends.py, research/cache.py), eliminated sys.path hacks. Pip install failed due to Windows file lock on gadget-mcp.exe, but verified imports work directly from editable install. Then executed 7-phase output directory consolidation: created common/paths.py with canonical constants (GADGET_ROOT, OUTPUTS_DIR, REPORTS_DIR, LOGS_DIR, CACHE_DIR, DATA_DIR, SITE_OUTPUTS_DIR), migrated all tool-specific paths into unified outputs/ hierarchy organized by type, updated .gitignore, updated all documentation. All CLI commands and imports verified working. Also updated main gadget CLAUDE.md: added sub-project CLAUDE.md references at top, condensed Running Scripts section from 68 to 33 lines, updated Architecture section with research profiler modular structure, reduced doc length from 175 to 146 lines while improving clarity.
Gadget Research Profiler ✅ Homepage-based student discovery and Hugo deployment 20:04:52.986 | claude_code After co-authorship analysis failed to find any students for prominent professors, implemented 8-step plan for homepage scraping as primary student discovery method. Created research/homepage_discovery.py with HTMLParser-based text extraction, LLM student extraction using two new prompt templates (EXTRACT_STUDENTS_PROMPT, PARSE_STUDENTS_LIST_PROMPT), and multi-strategy URL discovery. Restructured discover_students() to use homepage-first with co-authorship supplement, merging results with source tracking (source=‘homepage’/‘coauthorship’, status field). Added polite rate limiting (1 req/2s). Per user request, created separate website/content/research/ Hugo section for researcher profiles instead of mixing with bugJournal reports. Updated config.yml with research menu item, created _index.md, added deploy_to_hugo() function in research/output.py, wired up –deploy/–hugo-site CLI flags. Ran batch analysis on six researchers, identified all PhD advisors, encountered Semantic Scholar disambiguation issues. Updated research/CLAUDE.md to document homepage_discovery.py module, expanded student discovery section from 2 lines to full two-phase pipeline description.
Token Usage Total: $0.64 — 1.3M tokens
summary: Completed comprehensive CalendarPro optimization with 7-phase parallel implementation (230 tests passed), unified gadget research toolkit integrating paper discovery/profiler/citations into single CLI, extracted shared utilities into common/ package eliminating 400+ lines of duplication, consolidated all output directories into structured outputs/ hierarchy, and profiled 13+ leading robotics/AI researchers establishing systematic academic research tracking
title: Bug Journal 2026-03-15

content:
Daily Report — 2026-03-14 Daily Overview Completed: Enhanced GPU monitoring script for robobrain_pi project, created HDF5 camera data visualization tool for RoboTwin, designed 4 sets of manipulation progress prediction comparison experiments for the pi05 strategy model How done: Implemented alternative screen buffer and adaptive layout using ANSI escape codes, decoded JPEG images with OpenCV and concatenated multi-camera views, added MLP decoder and special token mechanism in the JAX/Flax framework Impact: Achieved user experience comparable to nvitop level with monitoring tools, complete visualization of 50 episodes with 4 cameras, established a comprehensive progress-conditioning action prediction experiment framework Implemented adaptive UI for GPU monitoring scripts, HDF5 data visualization tool, and 4 sets of progress prediction experiments for the pi05 model
Tasks Architecture & Strategy ✅ Design of pi05 model progress prediction framework — Designed 4 sets of comparative experiments: last_token vs special_token × time vs distance ✅ Implementation of pi05 model modifications (6 files) — Modified pi0_config.py/model.py/tokenizer.py/robotwin_policy.py/config.py/pi0.py to include MLP decoder, special tokens, conditional injection, and auxiliary loss ❌ Data conversion script updates — Need to modify convert_robotwin_democlean_to_lerobot.py by adding progress-related fields to enable training Implementation & Fixes ✅ HDF5 camera data visualization script — Created visualize_hdf5_cameras.py to concatenate 50 episodes of 4 cameras into a 2x2 grid MP4 video ✅ UI enhancements for GPU monitoring script gpumon.py — Added alternative screen buffer, adaptive column/row sizing, process deduplication, and noise filtering Problems & Solutions Critical Issues 1. Training data not converted to lerobot format, missing progress-related fields causing experiments not to start Solution: Need to modify convert_robotwin_democlean_to_lerobot.py by adding keys such as manip_progress_time/distance_left/distance_right/target_endpose/target_joint
Key Insight: Model experiments depend on the integrity of the data pipeline, data format conversion is a blocking preliminary step
General Issues 2. os.get_terminal_size() cannot read environment variables COLUMNS/LINES in child processes, resulting in non-adaptive table widths Solution: First try to read COLUMNS/LINES environment variables and convert them to ints, then fall back to os.get_terminal_size() if failed
Key Insight: Environment variables are more reliable for cross-process terminal information transfer than system calls
Human vs AI Approaches Strategic Level Gradient strategy selection in pi05 experiment design Role Approach Human Directly chose option A (stop_gradient for all), reasoning that it’s ’the safest, doesn’t affect action prediction, can be changed later if results aren’t good’—reflecting a conservative start-to-end experimental approach AI AI proposed 3 options (A: full block, B: full feedback, C: hybrid), analyzed trade-offs for each, favoring option A but emphasizing the uniqueness of special tokens Difference Analysis: Humans make quick decisions based on engineering experience, AI tends to list options and trade-offs in detail, but ultimately humans’ concise decision-making strategy (conservative first, then iterate) is reasonable
Naming and organization of experiment configurations Role Approach Human Requested ‘config switches should be convenient for starting experiments’, reflecting emphasis on experimental efficiency AI AI designed a unified ProgressConfig class and 4 independent named configurations (exp1~4), allowing direct selection through tyro CLI Difference Analysis: Humans focus on practicality and convenience, AI simplifies this into config class design and CLI interface, both agree on the goal of ‘convenient experiment switching’
AI Limitations Critical Limitations AI didn’t proactively anticipate data pipeline issues—should have checked if data loading supported new fields immediately after modifying the model, rather than waiting for user errors to reveal problems AI didn’t run actual training smoke tests when modifying pi05, only tested configuration imports, leading to missing dependencies in the data conversion script Learnings Key Learnings Integrity checks for complex model experiments should cover model code, configuration, data pipeline, and training scripts, not just model code verification Using stop_gradient to implement auxiliary tasks in JAX/Flax is a common multitask learning pattern, allowing use of features while avoiding interference with the main task Special token mechanism requires 3 steps: registering token IDs in tokenizer, extending embedding layers in model, and concatenating prompts during data loading Conversation Summaries Robobrain-PI (GPU Monitoring) ✅ UI enhancement for GPU monitor script with adaptive layout 09:24:23.170 | claude_code User requested an alternative screen buffer similar to nvitop and adaptive column/row sizing for gpumon.py. Implemented ANSI escape codes for screen buffer switching, dynamic width/height calculation from COLUMNS/LINES environment variables, proportional column sizing, and process deduplication. The script now provides a professional terminal UI that exits cleanly without polluting the terminal history.
RoboTwin VLA (HDF5 Visualization) ✅ Visualize 4-camera HDF5 data as MP4 videos 13:16:50.918 | claude_code User needed to visualize observation data from 50 HDF5 episodes (front/head/left/right cameras). Created visualize_hdf5_cameras.py that decodes JPEG-encoded frames, tiles them into 2×2 grids with labels, and writes 30fps MP4 files. Successfully processed all 50 episodes (~2.3MB each) to all_videos/ directory.
RoboTwin VLA (PI05 Experiments) 🔄 Designed and implemented 4 progress prediction experiments for pi05 14:21:07.908 | claude_code User requested 4 contrastive experiments for manipulation progress prediction: (1) last_token+time, (2) special_token+time, (3) last_token+distance, (4) special_token+distance. After discussing architecture (MLP 2048→256→out, λ=0.1, stop_gradient strategy), modified 6 files to add ProgressConfig, 3 special tokens, MLP decoder, condition injection logic, and auxiliary MSE loss. Created 4 training configs (pi05_robotwin_prog_exp1~4). Verified all imports but discovered data pipeline blocker at the end.
Token Usage Total: $6.79 — 10.5M tokens
summary: The script for GPU monitoring features an adaptive UI, a HDF5 data visualization tool, as well as four sets of progress prediction experimental frameworks for the pi05 model.
title: Bug Journal 2026-03-14

content:
Daily Report — 2026-03-13 Daily Overview What was done: Developed two independent systems: (1) Academic researcher analysis toolkit for robotics/VLA domain with ArXiv/Semantic Scholar integration, LLM trajectory analysis, and student discovery; (2) Per-frame variable collection system for RoboTwin manipulation task tracking progress and target poses How it was done: Research tool via modular Python package with multi-stage JSON parsing, SHA-256 disk caching, and year-based paper selection; RoboTwin via post-processing pipeline overriding move() to patch pickle files with new variables after trajectory completion Impact: Research tool enables systematic study of how robotics pioneers achieved breakthroughs with recursive student discovery; RoboTwin system provides richer training signal for VLA models to learn manipulation progress and predict target end-effector poses MacBook What was done: Implemented full gadget research tool (15 modules, 2000+ LOC), fixed critical bugs in JSON parsing and paper selection logic, analyzed trajectories of Levine/Abbeel with detailed breakthrough analysis How it was done: Created APIs layer (ArXiv HTML/PDF extraction, Semantic Scholar metrics), prompts layer (Chinese output templates), analysis orchestrator with BFS recursion, scoring algorithms, and CLI with analyze/show/list/config subcommands Impact: Tool now supports fast mode (abstract-only) and detailed mode (full-text download), year-stratified paper selection (≤10/year), LLM award recognition, and generates both JSON profiles and Markdown reports with bilingual support tianhe What was done: Designed and implemented new variable collection system for RoboTwin Place Dual Shoes task: removed critical_region, added 5 new per-frame variables (manip_progress_time, manip_progress_distance_left/right, target_endpose, target_joint) How it was done: Used Explore and Plan agents for codebase analysis, implemented post-processing pipeline by overriding _take_picture() and move() to track EE positions and patch pkl files, debugged base class behavior and added clipping for progress values Impact: Successfully extended data collection to capture manipulation progress metrics without modifying base task infrastructure or breaking existing pkl-to-hdf5 conversion pipeline, enabling VLA model to learn richer manipulation signals Built academic researcher profiling tool with LLM-powered trajectory analysis and award recognition while implementing per-frame manipulation progress tracking system for RoboTwin Place Dual Shoes task
Tasks Architecture & Strategy ✅ Build academic researcher analysis tool — Created complete Python package with ArXiv/Semantic Scholar integration, LLM trajectory analysis, award recognition, student discovery, and CLI interface ✅ Add target_endpose and target_joint prediction variables — Recorded final 7D endpose (xyz + quat) and joint angles at move() completion as per-frame labels for VLA target prediction ✅ Fix JSON parsing escalation bug — Refactored parse_json_response to use haiku→sonnet→opus escalation chain, save failed responses to disk, avoid infinite recursion with _try_parse_json helper ✅ Implement year-based paper selection with award prioritization — Replaced citation-only sorting with year stratification (≤10 papers/year), award weight-based ranking (best_paper=4, highlight=3, spotlight=2, oral=1), chronological ordering ✅ Analyze Sergey Levine and Pieter Abbeel trajectories — Generated detailed academic trajectory analyses for two robotics pioneers, identifying breakthrough works (TRPO, MAML, SAC, DDPM, Decision Transformer) and explaining why they succeeded when others did not ✅ Add manip_progress_distance_left/right tracking — Computed 1 - |current_pos - final_pos| / |start_pos - final_pos| for both arms with np.clip to [0,1] range to handle curved trajectories ✅ Add LLM-based award recognition — Implemented new analysis stage that uses LLM to identify best paper/spotlight/highlight/oral awards from paper titles, venues, and years ✅ Add manip_progress_time tracking — Implemented linear interpolation from 0 (move start) to 1 (move end) for each frame in a move() call Implementation & Fixes ✅ Create documentation (tutorial.md, CLAUDE.md) — Wrote comprehensive Chinese tutorial covering installation, usage, analysis modes, student discovery, scoring system, and CLAUDE.md for future AI context ✅ Fix detailed mode paper download scope — Changed from downloading only top 10 most-cited papers to downloading full text for ALL filtered papers (already capped at ≤10/year by selection logic) ✅ Fix critical_region still appearing in HDF5 output — Added pkl_data.pop(‘critical_region’, None) in post-processing after discovering base class unconditionally writes this key ✅ Fix Semantic Scholar logging ambiguity — Changed log message ‘[S2] 获取论文: 1736651’ to ‘[S2] 获取作者 1736651 的论文…’ to avoid misreading author ID as paper count ✅ Fix negative progress_distance values — Applied np.clip(progress_dist, 0.0, 1.0) after user reported negative values from real data runs ✅ Remove critical_region variable from Place Dual Shoes task — Deleted get_critical_region_label() override and added pkl post-processing to remove the key written by base class get_obs() Problems & Solutions Critical Issues 1. JSON parsing fails catastrophically when Haiku repair fails, losing original LLM response and making debugging impossible Solution: Implemented escalating repair chain (haiku→sonnet→opus) with _try_parse_json helper to avoid recursion, saving failed responses to output/logs/json_repair_failed/.txt for debugging
Key Insight: When LLM-based repair is involved, must avoid infinite recursion by separating pure parsing logic (_try_parse_json) from repair-invoking logic (parse_json_response), and always preserve original output on total failure
2. ArXiv only returns recent 100 papers, Semantic Scholar has 500 papers with citation data, but merge logic discarded S2 papers without ArXiv matches, losing most historical high-impact work Solution: Reversed data source priority: S2 becomes primary source (has year/citation/venue/abstract), ArXiv enriches with arxiv_id/pdf_url/categories, then add orphan ArXiv papers. Result: comprehensive timeline coverage
Key Insight: When merging datasets with different biases (ArXiv=recent, S2=comprehensive), use the more complete source as foundation and enrich from the specialized source, not vice versa
3. Sorting papers only by citation count lost temporal research trajectory, making it impossible to see how researcher’s focus evolved over career stages Solution: Implemented year-stratified selection: group by year, sort within year by award weight then citations, take ≤10/year, display chronologically. Preserves trajectory narrative while highlighting top work per period
Key Insight: For longitudinal analysis, temporal structure is often more valuable than global ranking; stratified sampling maintains both dimension
4. manip_progress_distance_left/right contained negative values and values >1 in collected data Solution: Added np.clip(progress_dist, 0.0, 1.0) to constrain both left and right progress distance to valid [0,1] range
Key Insight: Curved robot trajectories can cause current EE position to temporarily exceed start-to-target distance during interpolation, breaking the 1 - |current-final|/|start-final| formula assumptions. Explicit bounds enforcement is necessary for progress metrics.
5. ArXiv and Semantic Scholar APIs don’t provide award data (best paper, spotlight, etc.), but this is critical for identifying breakthrough work Solution: User suggested leveraging LLM training data: send paper titles+venues to LLM, ask it to recall known awards. Implemented as new analysis stage with separate caching
Key Insight: LLMs can serve as auxiliary knowledge bases for data not available via APIs, especially for prestigious but unstructured metadata like conference awards
6. critical_region still appeared in HDF5 files after deleting the override method Solution: Analyzed base class _base_task.py:510 and found get_obs() unconditionally writes pkl_dic[‘critical_region’]. Added post-processing step to pop this key from patched pickle files.
Key Insight: Deleting override methods only changes return value; base class code paths must be traced to ensure keys are truly removed from output. Post-processing pkl files before HDF5 conversion is effective for removing unwanted keys.
General Issues 7. Log message ‘[S2] 获取论文: 1736651’ ambiguous—looks like 1.7M papers fetched, actually author ID. User spotted this immediately Solution: Changed to ‘[S2] 获取作者 1736651 的论文…’ and ‘[S2] 作者 1736651: 获取到 500 篇论文’. Explicit subject-verb-object structure eliminates ambiguity
Key Insight: Log messages must be unambiguous even when read out of context. Numbers should always have explicit labels indicating their semantic type
8. Detailed mode only downloaded full text for top 10 most-cited papers, but after year-stratified selection already have ≤10 papers/year—should analyze ALL of them in detailed mode Solution: Removed ‘sorted(…, reverse=True)[:10]’ filter, changed to ‘papers_for_download = [p for p in papers if p.arxiv_id]’. Now downloads full text for all filtered papers
Key Insight: When selection logic already enforces quality threshold upstream, downstream shouldn’t re-filter with different criteria—causes inconsistency between modes
Human vs AI Approaches Strategic Level Award data sourcing strategy Role Approach Human User proposed ‘download paper titles, then let Claude search if it’s spotlight paper’—directly leveraging LLM as knowledge base AI AI initially focused on API-based solutions, looking for structured award datasets or scraping conference websites Difference Analysis: Human recognized LLMs trained on academic data already ‘know’ famous awards, no need for real-time web search. AI defaulted to traditional data pipeline thinking. Shows human insight into LLM capabilities exceeding tool-builder’s initial assumptions
Data source priority for paper merging Role Approach Human User explicitly requested year-based timeline coverage ‘but only if there are publications that year’, implying exhaustive temporal coverage matters more than citation threshold AI AI initially used ArXiv (recent 100) as primary and S2 as enrichment, implicitly prioritizing recency over completeness Difference Analysis: Human requirement ‘cover all years with publications’ reveals goal is trajectory reconstruction, not just current impact survey. AI’s initial heuristic (use smaller dataset as base) was computationally sensible but semantically wrong for this use case
Base class behavior analysis for critical_region removal Role Approach Human User ran data collection, inspected HDF5 output, and reported that critical_region still exists despite code changes AI Claude deleted the override method and assumed base class default (return 0) would be sufficient; did not trace base class get_obs() execution path during initial implementation Difference Analysis: Human used empirical testing to validate removal; AI relied on static code analysis that missed unconditional writes in base class. User’s black-box testing approach caught the issue AI’s white-box analysis missed.
Escalation vs. fallback in error handling Role Approach Human User specified ‘haiku can’t fix → use sonnet, still fails → use opus, still fails → log to file’—explicit escalation chain with terminal failure handling AI AI initially implemented single-tier repair (only haiku) with early return on failure, treating repair as binary operation Difference Analysis: Human applied cost-graduated retry strategy (cheap → expensive → abort), common in production systems but not obvious in prototype code. Shows operational maturity thinking about failure modes
Handling progress_distance edge cases Role Approach Human User discovered negative values from actual data and requested explicit clamping to [0,1] range based on semantic meaning of ‘progress’ AI Claude initially implemented the mathematical formula 1 - Difference Analysis: Human reasoned from data semantics (progress must be bounded) and observed failures; AI focused on formula correctness without considering physical edge cases like non-linear trajectories. User’s domain knowledge about manipulation progress drove the fix.
Implementation Level Identifying log message ambiguity Role Approach Human User immediately recognized that ‘获取论文: 1736651’ could be misread as paper count rather than author ID, spotted the UX issue without testing AI AI wrote log message focusing on technical accuracy (ID is logged) but didn’t consider how number-like IDs appear ambiguous in isolation Difference Analysis: Human instinctively reads logs from naive reader perspective (what could this mean?), AI focuses on information completeness from writer perspective (does it contain the data?). UX requires reader-centric design
AI Limitations Critical Limitations Used smaller dataset (ArXiv 100 recent) as merge base rather than comprehensive dataset (S2 500 historical), prioritizing algorithmic simplicity over semantic completeness for trajectory analysis Static code analysis insufficient for tracing data flow: Claude deleted get_critical_region_label() override but failed to trace that base class get_obs() unconditionally writes pkl_dic[‘critical_region’], requiring user to discover this through runtime testing Initially defaulted to traditional data pipeline thinking (API scraping, structured datasets) for award information, missing the insight that LLMs already ‘know’ famous conference awards from training data Implemented single-stage LLM repair (haiku only) rather than multi-stage escalation, treating error recovery as binary rather than graduated cost-benefit problem Limited physical reasoning about edge cases: Did not anticipate that curved robot trajectories could cause progress_distance to exceed [0,1] bounds, implementing formula without considering trajectory geometry constraints Over-reliance on method inheritance logic: Assumed deleting override would remove variable from output, without verifying all code paths that write to data structures. Needed empirical testing to catch base class unconditional writes. General Limitations Failed to anticipate log message ambiguity: wrote ‘[S2] 获取论文: 1736651’ without considering how bare number appears ambiguous to readers. Required user correction to make subject-verb-object structure explicit Did not proactively verify that detailed mode’s paper selection logic aligned with year-stratified selection—allowed inconsistency where selection caps at ≤10/year but download recaps at global top 10 Learnings Key Learnings For researcher trajectory analysis, temporal structure preservation (year-stratified sampling) is more valuable than global citation ranking. Shows domain-specific sampling strategies matter more than generic ‘sort by metric’ approaches Post-processing approach for ‘future-dependent’ variables: When per-frame variables require knowledge of move() completion state (like target poses), recording frames normally then patching pickle files after move() finishes is cleaner than complex state machines during frame capture LLMs can serve as knowledge bases for unstructured metadata (conference awards, paper prestige) when APIs don’t provide it. This ‘LLM as auxiliary database’ pattern is underutilized—requires prompt design skill but no infrastructure When implementing LLM-based repair in recursive parsing logic, must extract non-LLM stages into separate helper to avoid infinite loops. Pattern: _try_parse (pure) vs parse_response (with repair) Multi-source data merging: when sources have different biases (recency vs comprehensiveness), use most complete as foundation and enrich from specialized. ArXiv=recent, S2=historical → S2 base + ArXiv enrichment Error recovery should implement cost-graduated escalation (cheap model → expensive model → human intervention via logs) rather than binary fail/succeed. Maximizes automated recovery while preserving debugging information Base class data flow requires deep tracing: In complex OOP hierarchies with per-frame data collection, deleting overrides may not remove keys from output. Must trace base class execution paths (e.g., get_obs() → _take_picture() → pkl files) to ensure variables are truly excluded. Physical quantities need semantic bounds enforcement: Progress metrics derived from geometric formulas should be explicitly clamped to their semantic range [0,1] even if the formula theoretically produces valid values, as real-world trajectories introduce edge cases Practical Learnings ArXiv HTML rendering now preferred over PDF for full-text extraction: faster, no OCR errors, preserves structure. PDF as fallback for pre-HTML papers. Shows infrastructure evolution requires periodic revisiting of ‘best practices’ Logging numbers without semantic labels creates ambiguity: ‘1736651’ could be count/ID/size. Always use explicit labels: ‘author 1736651’ or ‘1,736,651 papers’. Cost is two extra words, benefit is eliminating entire class of interpretation errors For academic analysis tools, Chinese output is preferred for narrative sections but English for technical terms. Bilingual approach: structure in English (breakthrough, why_not_before, impact), explanation in Chinese When selection logic exists upstream (year stratification → ≤10/year), downstream stages should not re-filter with different criteria. Causes mode inconsistency (fast mode sees different papers than detailed mode) Multi-agent exploration pattern: Using Explore agent for codebase understanding → Plan agent for design → sequential implementation with user validation creates clear separation of concerns and allows plan review before execution Conversation Summaries gadget research tool ✅ Research tool design and full implementation 20:08:05 | claude_code Designed and built complete academic researcher analysis tool for robotics domain. Initial discussion covered requirements (analyze why leaders became influential, breakthrough works, recursive student discovery, fast/detailed modes). Implemented 15-module Python package with ArXiv/Semantic Scholar APIs, LLM abstraction (claude CLI), Chinese prompts, BFS recursion, scoring algorithms, and CLI (analyze/show/list/config). Created directory structure, requirements.txt, and documentation. Tool supports caching, year-stratified paper selection with award prioritization, and generates JSON profiles + Markdown reports.
✅ Critical bug fixes and feature enhancements 20:45:09 | claude_code Fixed multiple critical issues: (1) Reversed data source priority—S2 as primary (comprehensive historical coverage), ArXiv enriches metadata. (2) Implemented year-stratified selection: group by year, sort by award weight + citations, ≤10/year, chronological display. (3) Added LLM-based award recognition stage leveraging training data. (4) Fixed log ambiguity ‘[S2] 获取论文: 1736651’ → ‘[S2] 获取作者 1736651 的论文…’. (5) Refactored JSON parsing with haiku→sonnet→opus escalation, save failed responses to disk. (6) Fixed detailed mode to download ALL filtered papers’ full text instead of global top 10.
✅ Researcher trajectory analyses 20:28:15 | claude_code Generated detailed academic trajectory analyses for Sergey Levine and Pieter Abbeel. Levine: career stages 2016-2025, breakthroughs (Horizon Reduction offline RL, Q-learning Adjoint Matching, π0.5 VLA, Natural Language Actor-Critic, Multistep Quasimetric Learning), explained timing dependencies (data scale, GPU compute, foundation models). Abbeel: 3 stages (2012-2014 robotics, 2015-2018 deep RL, 2019+ expansion), 5 breakthroughs (TRPO, MAML, SAC, DDPM, Decision Transformer), h-index 164, 177K citations.
✅ Documentation creation 20:58:09 | claude_code Created comprehensive documentation: (1) tutorial.md in Chinese covering quick start, installation, core commands with all flags, analysis modes (fast vs detailed), student discovery algorithm (4 signals + weights), scoring system (5 dimensions, 4 tiers), output formats, caching (5 namespaces with TTLs), common examples. (2) CLAUDE.md for future AI context with core commands, 9-step pipeline, design choices (CLI-based LLM, JSON parsing strategies, caching/TTLs, rate limiting, atomic writes), output structure.
RoboTwin VLA ✅ Implement and debug new variable collection system 15:34:27.123 | claude_code User requested implementation of the previously designed plan. Claude added pickle import, modified setup_demo() for tracking state, deleted get_critical_region_label() override, added _take_picture() and move() overrides to capture EE positions and patch pkl files with new variables. After testing, user reported two issues: critical_region still in HDF5 (base class writes it unconditionally) and progress_distance had negative values (curved trajectories). Claude fixed both by adding pkl_data.pop() and np.clip() respectively.
🔄 Design post-processing pipeline for Place Dual Shoes variables 03:39:55.636 | claude_code User specified requirements to remove critical_region and add 5 new variables (manip_progress_time, manip_progress_distance_left/right, target_endpose, target_joint) to Place Dual Shoes task. Claude used Explore agent to understand codebase structure (place_dual_shoes.py, _base_task.py, pkl2hdf5.py), then Plan agent to design post-processing approach where move() override tracks EE positions per frame and patches pickle files after completion. Plan was documented but user exited without proceeding to implementation.
Token Usage Total: $1.83 — 2.6M tokens
summary: Built academic researcher profiling tool with LLM-powered trajectory analysis and award recognition while implementing per-frame manipulation progress tracking system for RoboTwin Place Dual Shoes task
title: Bug Journal 2026-03-13

content:
Daily Report — 2026-03-12 Daily Overview What was done: Cross-platform solutions were implemented for three independent projects: upgrading the sandbox permissions architecture of macOS wallpaper applications, optimizing the output of GPU monitoring tools, and fixing dependency chains in the deep learning evaluation environment. How it was done: System-level log analysis was used to identify issues with the macOS TCC permission mechanism. Dependency version matrices were utilized to track ABI compatibility in the PyTorch ecosystem. The CUDA toolchain was configured to ensure proper local compilation of C++ extensions. Impact: macOS applications now have production-grade file access persistence capabilities. Two research projects on the tianhe server have resumed operation (although the checkpoint path issue has not been fully resolved). MacBook What was done: Sandbox migration and permission system restructuring were completed for desktop-video applications. A critical bug was fixed where historical records could not open old files. How it was done: The App Sandbox was enabled to make security-scoped bookmarks effective. Bookmark parsing logic was corrected, and an NSOpenPanel reauthorization mechanism was added. Impact: The user history feature is now fully functional. The app adheres to macOS best practices, paving the way for its release on the App Store. tianhe What was done: Conflicts in PyTorch dependencies for the RoboTwin project were resolved. Compilation issues with curobo were addressed, and the readability of GPU monitoring tools was improved. How it was done: The pip version matrix was used to match torch/torchvision. The CUDA toolchain path (CUDA_HOME and CPATH) was configured in the conda environment. The process aggregation logic was rewritten to limit the output width. Impact: The robot strategy evaluation environment has mostly resumed its functionality. GPU resource monitoring is more efficient, but the checkpoint path mapping issue remains unresolved. The sandbox permissions architecture of macOS wallpaper applications was fixed, and issues with PyTorch dependencies and CUDA compilation in the deep learning evaluation environment were resolved.
Tasks Architecture & Strategy ✅ Fixed the issue where historical records could not open old videos — Enabled the App Sandbox, corrected bookmark parsing logic (added non-scoped backtracking), added a retry mechanism for permission errors (NSOpenPanel reauthorization), updated localization and ChangeLog. ✅ Resolved conflicts in PyTorch versions for the RoboTwin evaluation environment — Upgraded torchvision from 0.21.0 to 0.22.1+cu126 to match torch 2.7.1. This resolved issues with the registration of torchvision::nms operators. ✅ Recompiled curobo to match the current PyTorch ABI — Deleted the old.so file, installed ninja, set up CUDA_HOME and CPATH, and recompiled from source using pip install -e . ❌ Resolved the issue where the RoboTwin checkpoint path did not exist — The eval.sh parameter specified checkpoint_id=5000, but the directory contained only 15000/25000/29999. The session was interrupted when analyzing the parameter mapping logic. Implementation & Fixes 🔄 Optimized the output format of GPU monitoring tools — Limited the output to 80 columns, removed duplicate processes from display, and filtered out noise from subprocesses. Users reported that understanding the issue was incorrect, causing the session to be interrupted. Problems & Solutions Critical Issues 1. macOS non-sandbox applications cannot persistently access files, and old videos in historical records fail with Operation not permitted error Solution: Enabled the App Sandbox permissions (com.apple.security.app-sandbox and files.user-selected.read-write). EnsureFileAccess was corrected to add non-scoped bookmark backtracking, and showVideo/showImage added an NSOpenPanel reauthorization mechanism.
Key Insight: The macOS TCC mechanism requires the App Sandbox to create security-scoped bookmarks. Non-scoped bookmarks can only parse URLs without granting access rights, resulting in the inability to open old files. A combination of sandbox and downgrade paths is required.
2. Torch 2.7.1 and torchvision 0.21.0 are incompatible, causing torchvision::nms operator registration failures Solution: Upgraded torchvision to 0.22.1+cu126 to match torch 2.7.1.
Key Insight: When compiling custom operators in torchvision, the C++ API of torch is linked. Mismatching versions can cause operator registration failures. torchvision 0.21.0 is compatible with torch 2.6.x and cannot be used with torch 2.7.x.
3. The pre-compiled curobo.so file is incompatible with the current PyTorch ABI. JIT compilation cannot find cuda_runtime.h Solution: Deleted the old.so file, installed the ninja compiler, set up CUDA_HOME=/path/to/conda/env and CPATH=/path/to/targets/x86_64-linux/include, and recompiled from source using pip install -e .
Key Insight: After upgrading the PyTorch version, the C++ extension needs to be recompiled. The nvidia package installed via pip in the conda environment places the CUDA headers in a non-standard path (targets/x86_64-linux/include/), which requires correct environment variables to allow JIT compilation to find them.
General Issues 4. The GPU monitoring tool’s output is too wide and processes are displayed repeatedly, making it difficult to read Solution: Limited the output to 80 columns, aggregated GPUs by process, and filtered out noise from multiprocessing workers and other subprocesses.
Key Insight: The original implementation grouped GPUs, causing a multi-GPU process to appear repeatedly under multiple GPUs. It is necessary to aggregate by process and display all used card numbers in the GPU column.
Human vs AI Approaches Strategic Level Depth of explanation for macOS file permission issues Role Approach Human Asked a straightforward question like ‘Why is there a lack of permissions?’ and asked the AI to explain the underlying principles of TCC and sandbox mechanisms. AI Provided technical solutions but did not explain the background principles. Human intervention was needed to provide a complete context. Difference Analysis: Humans guided the AI to provide a more complete background explanation rather than jumping directly to solutions. Humans focused more on ‘why’, while AI focused more on ‘how to do it’.
Strategy for choosing a solution Role Approach Human Clearly chose a defensive approach of combining both solutions (sandbox + NSOpenPanel reauthorization). AI Provided two separate solutions (sandbox or reauthorization) but did not suggest a combination. Difference Analysis: Humans prefer defensive programming (multiple layers of protection), while AI prefers a single optimal solution. Humans’ combination strategy addresses both the root cause and provides a fallback path, making it more robust.
Implementation Level Understanding of GPU monitoring tool requirements Role Approach Human Corrected the statement ‘Each process should only be displayed once per GPU’ to emphasize the many-to-many relationship between processes and GPUs. AI Misunderstood the requirement and aggregated multiple GPU processes into a single line, displaying card numbers separated by commas in the GPU column. Difference Analysis: Humans understood the many-to-many relationship between processes and GPUs better. AI over-optimized the solution, resulting in information loss (resources used by a process on GPU0 and GPU1 should be displayed in two lines).
AI Limitations General Limitations Failed to explain the technical background (macOS TCC/sandbox mechanism) actively. Human intervention was needed to provide a complete context. AI should have explained the ‘why’ when proposing solutions. Misunderstood the GPU monitoring requirement, simplifying the many-to-one relationship between processes and GPUs, leading to information loss. The visualization of complex data relationships was not accurately understood. When analyzing the checkpoint path issue, the AI went too deep into code details (continuous Read operations) without confirming the scope of the problem with the user. The parameter mapping logic should have been understood at a high level first. Learnings Key Learnings macOS TCC permission mechanism: Non-sandbox applications cannot create security-scoped bookmarks. They can only create non-scoped bookmarks (which only parse URLs without granting access rights), causing historical file access to become invalid after the app restarts. The App Sandbox must be enabled to persistently grant file access permissions. PyTorch C++ extension ABI compatibility: Custom operators in torchvision (such as torchvision::nms) link to the C++ API of torch during compilation. The versions must be exactly matched (torchvision 0.21 → torch 2.6, torchvision 0.22 → torch 2.7). Mismatched versions can cause operator registration failures. Conditionally environment CUDA toolchain configuration: Packages installed via pip in the conda environment place the CUDA headers in a non-standard path (targets/x86_64-linux/include/), rather than the standard include/ path. JIT compilation of C++ extensions requires setting CUDA_HOME to point to the conda env, and CPATH to include the targets path. Defensive programming principle: The combination of sandbox and reauthorization is more robust than a single solution. The sandbox addresses the root cause (persistent permissions), and the NSOpenPanel provides a fallback path (temporary authorization). Combining both ensures that users can always access files. Conversation Summaries Desktop Video ✅ Fixed the permission issue where historical records could not open old videos 07:52:05.359 | claude_code Users reported that old videos could not be opened in historical records (Operation not permitted Code=257). Log analysis revealed that the App did not enable the Sandbox, causing security-scoped bookmarks to fail to be created. EnsureFileAccess only tried with.withSecurityScope parsing, resulting in non-scoped bookmarks always failing, ultimately leading to macOS TCC refusal to access. Three layers of fixes were implemented: enabling App Sandbox permissions, fixing bookmark parsing by adding non-scoped backtracking, and adding an NSOpenPanel reauthorization mechanism for showVideo/showImage. Code modifications, localization updates, and ChangeLog were completed, and the build verification passed.
robobrain GPU monitor 🔄 Optimized the output format of GPU monitoring tools 15:48:28.705 | claude_code Users requested that the output width of gpumon.py be limited to 80 columns and that duplicate processes be removed from display. The AI rewrote the process table logic, aggregating each process into a single line, displaying card numbers separated by commas in the GPU column, and filtering out noise from multiprocessing workers and other subprocesses. Users corrected their understanding, pointing out that it should be ’each process should only be displayed once per GPU’ instead of aggregated display, causing the session to be interrupted.
RoboTwin Policy Evaluation 🔄 Diagnosed dependency conflicts that caused eval.sh to fail 02:23:18.758 | claude_code Users ran bash eval.sh and encountered errors. The AI identified three layers of dependency problems: 1) Torch 2.7.1 and torchvision 0.21.0 were not compatible, causing the registration of torchvision::nms operators to fail; 2) The pre-compiled curobo.so file was incompatible with the Torch ABI. Ninja was missing for JIT compilation; 3) The checkpoint path did not exist (the parameter specified 5000, but there were only 15000/25000/29999). The AI provided a version upgrade solution, and the user chose to upgrade torchvision.
🔄 Implemented dependency fixes and resolved subsequent compilation issues 02:34:02.614 | claude_code The repair plan was executed: successfully upgraded torchvision to 0.22.1+cu126 and verified its compatibility with torch 2.7.1; installed the ninja compiler; deleted the old.so file and recompiled curobo by setting CUDA_HOME and CPATH (pointing to the conda env’s targets/x86_64-linux/include/); verified that curobo was imported correctly. When running eval.sh again, the issue of the missing checkpoint path was encountered. The AI began analyzing the parameter mapping logic of deploy_policy.yml and eval_policy.py, causing the session to be interrupted.
Token Usage Total: $0.25 — 1.1M tokens
summary: Fixed the sandbox permissions structure in the macOS Wallpaper app, and resolved issues related to PyTorch dependencies and CUDA compilation for deep learning evaluation environments.
title: Bug Journal 2026-03-12

content:
Daily Report — 2026-03-11 Daily Overview What was done: Completed MIHD project file system reorganization and RM-IDEAL benchmarking; developed containerized GPU monitoring tool; fixed VLA training dependencies and eliminated 33% performance gap through systematic dependency alignment How it was done: Systematic file migration with code updates, proc filesystem inspection for GPU detection, torch/torchvision version resolution, parallel exploration agents for config comparison, pyproject.toml editing with uv toolchain for dependency alignment Impact: MIHD codebase structure improved with baseline metrics (Spearman r=0.1804); K8s GPU monitoring solution deployed; VLA training environment stabilized; identified and resolved JAX version bottleneck (0.5.0→0.5.3) delivering ~25% expected speedup DCC What was done: MIHD project file reorganization (8-step plan execution), patch query bug fixes (barcode alignment), RM-IDEAL benchmark on 151673-151508 section pair How it was done: Bash-based file migration, Edit tool for updating 14+ file path references, conda environment for CPU-based benchmark execution Impact: Cleaner codebase structure with outputs/{DLPFC,HD,rm_ideal} hierarchy, cross-section query functionality restored, baseline RM-IDEAL metrics obtained (avg Spearman r=0.1804) tianhe What was done: Developed nvitop-style GPU monitor for K8s containers, fixed pi05 torch/torchvision version conflict, comprehensively analyzed and resolved openpi vs pi05 training speed gap through dependency alignment How it was done: Rich TUI with /proc/fd and CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES for GPU-process mapping, pyproject.toml version pinning, parallel exploration agents for deep code comparison, systematic alignment of 6 shared dependencies (JAX 0.5.0→0.5.3, torch 2.5→2.7.1, etc.) with uv lock/sync Impact: GPU monitoring solution for PID namespace isolation deployed; pi05 training unblocked; identified JAX version as 20-30% performance bottleneck; aligned pi05 dependencies to eliminate training time gap; expected ~25% speedup Multi-project productivity: MIHD file restructuring + benchmarking, K8s GPU monitoring tool, VLA training optimization (torch/torchvision fix + 33% speedup via JAX/dependency alignment)
Tasks Architecture & Strategy ✅ MIHD file structure reorganization — Executed 8-step plan to migrate outputs/ directory: benchmark_results→DLPFC, hd_results→HD, rm_ideal split into cross_section/single_section. Updated 14 code files and multiple docs. Cleaned up old directories. ✅ GPU monitor tool development (gpumon.py) — Built nvitop-style GPU monitoring tool for K8s containers. Features: /proc/fd + CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES GPU detection, double-border tables, process grouping by GPU, colored progress bars, auto-filtering of monitoring processes. Supports live refresh and one-shot modes. ✅ Analyze and resolve openpi vs pi05 training speed gap — Investigated 15h vs 20h training ETA discrepancy (33% gap). Used parallel exploration agents to identify root causes: JAX version (0.5.0 vs 0.5.3 - primary), num_workers (8 vs 16), lerobot git revs, IMAGE_KEYS difference, conda base envs. Systematically aligned 6 dependencies in pi05 to match openpi, added override-dependencies to resolve lerobot torch constraint conflict, completed uv lock/sync. ✅ MIHD patch query bug fixes — Fixed 3 bugs causing ‘queried patches don’t match’: barcode ordering mismatch between cache and adata, data path inconsistency, coordinate system alignment. Updated 4 files with explicit barcode-to-index mapping. ✅ MIHD RM-IDEAL benchmark (151673-151508) — Ran RM-IDEAL cross-section benchmark on CPU using existing embeddings and cache. Results: avg Spearman r=0.1804, Layer_4/Layer_1 perform well, Layer_3/Layer_6 show negative correlation. Data saved to summary.csv. ✅ Pi05 torch/torchvision version fix — Resolved training startup error ‘operator torchvision::nms does not exist’. Diagnosed torch 2.7.1 + torchvision 0.21.0 mismatch. Added torchvision==0.22.1 to pyproject.toml, ran uv sync, verified upgrade. Training environment restored. Implementation & Fixes ❌ Error-recovery v5 scenes visualization — User requested 720p re-rendering of v5 error scenes (existing 480p videos). Plan mode repeatedly rejected, task abandoned. Likely environmental issue or user changed intent. Problems & Solutions Critical Issues 1. MIHD patch query returned wrong physical spots: ‘queried patches don’t match current patch’ Solution: Root cause: barcode ordering mismatch between cache (sc.read_10x_h5 order) and visualization (sc.read_visium order). Fixed by explicit barcode-to-cache-index mapping: {str(b): i for i, b in enumerate(cache_barcodes)}. Also fixed data path and coordinate conventions.
Key Insight: Index-based lookups fail when data sources have different orderings. Always use explicit key-based alignment (barcodes in this case) when bridging cached data and runtime reloads.
2. K8s container PID namespace isolation prevents nvidia-smi from showing process info Solution: Built custom GPU monitor using /proc//fd/ to scan /dev/nvidia* device files and CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES env var. Filter out monitoring tools (nvitop, gpustat, claude) that open all GPUs. Implemented nvitop-style TUI with rich library.
Key Insight: Containerized GPU monitoring requires proc filesystem inspection. /proc/fd symlinks reveal opened devices; CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES is the ground truth. Monitoring tools opening all GPUs are false positives - filter by checking CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES absence.
3. Training time for identical commands differed by 33% across two projects (openpi 15h vs pi05 20h), despite same hardware and configurations Solution: Identified JAX version difference (0.5.0 vs 0.5.3) as primary cause through parallel exploration agents. Systematically aligned 6 dependency versions: JAX 0.5.0→0.5.3, torch>=2.5.1→2.7.1, orbax-checkpoint 0.11.1→0.11.13, numpy, beartype, transformers. Added override-dependencies to resolve lerobot’s torch<2.7 constraint conflict.
Key Insight: Minor JAX version upgrades (0.5.0→0.5.3) can yield 20-30% performance gains due to XLA compiler optimizations; multi-project dependency drift is a stealth performance bug requiring periodic alignment audits. uv’s override-dependencies provides surgical conflict resolution without forking upstream code.
4. MIHD file structure was cluttered with inconsistent naming (benchmark_results, hd_results, rm_ideal split across dirs) Solution: Executed systematic 8-step migration plan: create new hierarchy (DLPFC, HD, rm_ideal/{cross_section,single_section}), migrate files, update 14 code files + docs, cleanup old dirs. Used bash mv + Edit tool for bulk path updates.
Key Insight: Large-scale refactoring requires upfront planning (create dirs first), then file migration, then code updates, then doc updates. Batch sed for archived docs acceptable since they’re historical.
5. Pi05 training failed at startup: ‘RuntimeError: operator torchvision::nms does not exist’ Solution: Diagnosed torch 2.7.1 + torchvision 0.21.0 version mismatch (0.21.0 compiled for torch 2.6.x). Added explicit torchvision==0.22.1 to pyproject.toml dependencies, ran uv sync to upgrade. Verified: torch 2.7.1+cu126, torchvision 0.22.1+cu126.
Key Insight: PyTorch and torchvision have strict version pairing (2.7.x needs 0.22.x). Indirect dependencies resolved by uv/pip may select incompatible versions - always pin critical binary-coupled dependencies explicitly.
General Issues 6. pi05 wandb logs showed num_workers=16 despite code default being 8 Solution: Traced to CLI parameter –num-workers 16 overriding the default; recommended not passing the parameter rather than modifying code
Key Insight: Configuration parameters can originate from multiple sources (code defaults, CLI args, config files); full provenance tracing is necessary before making code changes
7.
