Initial gpumon.py layout had line wrapping, column truncation, and cluttered process lists Solution: Iterative improvements: 1) Fixed column width calculations for GPU info, 2) Switched to table-based layout, 3) Adopted nvitop double-border style, 4) Filtered monitoring tool processes, 5) Grouped processes under respective GPUs.
Key Insight: Terminal UI design requires multiple iterations. Reference existing tools (nvitop) for user expectations. Auto-filtering noise (monitoring processes) improves signal-to-noise ratio.
Human vs AI Approaches Strategic Level MIHD file reorganization execution approach Role Approach Human Human provided detailed 8-step plan: create dirs first, migrate files by category, update code refs, update docs, verify final structure. Clear separation of phases. AI AI would likely explore first, then propose changes incrementally. Less upfront planning, more discovery-driven. Difference Analysis: Human’s systematic planning prevented mid-execution issues (like updating code before files moved). AI tends toward exploratory, iterative execution. Human’s approach better for large-scale refactoring with many interdependencies.
Error recovery v5 visualization plan rejection loop Role Approach Human After multiple plan rejections, human said ‘just run it now’ (wanted immediate execution, not more planning). But continued rejecting exit plan mode attempts. AI AI interpreted ‘just run it now’ as ’exit plan mode and execute’. Kept trying ExitPlanMode tool. Didn’t recognize user might have environmental blocker or changed intent. Difference Analysis: Communication breakdown: user’s repeated rejections signaled an unspoken issue (GPU node unavailable? task no longer needed?). AI should have asked ‘what’s blocking this?’ after 2-3 rejections instead of retrying same approach.
Dependency alignment strategy Role Approach Human Direct instruction: ‘align all pi05 dependencies to openpi and set num_workers to 8’ AI Granular analysis: compared both pyproject.toml files, categorized dependencies into shared (6 to update), pi05-specific (RoboTwin packages to keep), openpi-specific (not needed in pi05), and pinned git revs (lerobot to preserve) Difference Analysis: AI decomposed the request into surgical edits (6 specific version changes + 1 new config section) rather than wholesale replacement, preserving project-specific dependencies and avoiding compatibility risks from changing lerobot git rev
GPU monitor UI design expectations Role Approach Human Human explicitly requested ‘refer to nvitop layout’, ‘group processes by GPU’, ‘don’t show processes not using GPU’. Clear reference point and priorities. AI AI’s initial design was generic TUI with process lists - didn’t reference domain-standard tool (nvitop). Only adapted after multiple user corrections. Difference Analysis: Human knew the established pattern (nvitop) and expected it. AI didn’t proactively check existing tools for UX patterns. Lesson: when building CLI tools, research category leaders first.
Implementation Level num_workers configuration fix Role Approach Human Requested explicit code change to set num_workers=8 AI Investigated actual execution context, found code default already 8, traced override to CLI parameter –num-workers 16, recommended non-invasive solution (omit the CLI flag) Difference Analysis: AI chose behavioral correction over code modification after discovering the real source of the discrepancy, avoiding unnecessary code churn
AI Limitations Critical Limitations Plan mode death spiral: In v5 visualization task, AI attempted ExitPlanMode 4+ times, all rejected. Didn’t fallback to asking user ‘what’s preventing us from proceeding?’ until very end. Should detect rejection loops earlier and switch to clarification mode. General Limitations Did not anticipate lerobot’s torch<2.7 constraint conflict when initially editing pyproject.toml; only discovered the issue after uv lock failed, then reactively added torch to override-dependencies. Could have pre-analyzed transitive dependency graphs. Used two parallel exploration agents for near-identical codebases (openpi and pi05 share the same OpenPI framework), which yielded redundant findings. A single agent with sequential exploration would have been more efficient. Initial GPU monitor design was generic, not domain-aware. Didn’t proactively check nvitop (category leader) for UX patterns. Required multiple user corrections to reach expected layout. Should research existing tools when building CLI utilities. Didn’t predict torch/torchvision version incompatibility until user provided error traceback. Could have proactively validated dependency compatibility when reading pyproject.toml (torch 2.7.1 without explicit torchvision pin was a red flag). In training speed analysis, launched two parallel Explore agents but didn’t initially compare uv.lock files (the ground truth for installed versions). First checked train.py, config.py, etc. Lock files should be priority #1 for dependency-related issues. Learnings Key Learnings K8s PID namespace isolation prevents nvidia-smi process detection. Solution: /proc//fd/ inspection for /dev/nvidia* device files + CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES env var. Monitoring tools opening all GPUs without CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES are false positives. JAX/XLA minor version upgrades (0.5.0→0.5.3) can deliver 20-30% training speedups through compiler optimizations; even patch-level framework updates merit performance audits in compute-intensive ML projects. Multi-project dependency drift is a stealth performance bug requiring periodic alignment. PyTorch/torchvision strict version pairing: torch 2.7.x requires torchvision 0.22.x, 2.6.x needs 0.21.x, etc. C++ operator registration breaks if versions mismatch. Always explicitly pin both in dependencies when using torchvision, as indirect resolution can fail. Barcode ordering pitfall in spatial transcriptomics: Cache created with sc.read_10x_h5() may have different barcode order than runtime sc.read_visium(). Index-based lookups silently map to wrong spots. Always use explicit barcode-to-index mapping: {str(b): i for i, b in enumerate(cache_barcodes)}. When plan mode repeatedly rejected, user may have unspoken blocker (environment unavailable, changed priority, technical constraint). After 2-3 rejections, switch from ’let me fix the plan’ to ‘what’s preventing us from proceeding?’. RM-IDEAL centroid computation: For a query niche, take embeddings of all spots in that niche, compute mean vector → centroid. Compare centroid to all ref section spots via cosine similarity. This compresses N-to-M comparison into 1-to-M, improving efficiency and robustness. uv’s override-dependencies is a precise tool for resolving transitive dependency conflicts without forking or patching upstream packages; particularly useful when pinning git revisions with incompatible constraints. Practical Learnings Configuration parameter provenance must be traced across all sources (code defaults, CLI args, config files, environment variables) before assuming a fix; the actual runtime value may differ from code inspection. Large-scale codebase refactoring requires phase separation: 1) Create new structure, 2) Migrate files, 3) Update code references, 4) Update docs (bulk sed acceptable for archived docs), 5) Verify final structure, 6) Cleanup old dirs. Doing code updates before file migration causes breakage. Conversation Summaries MIHD ✅ File structure reorganization, patch query fixes, and RM-IDEAL benchmark 00:20:01.434 | claude_code Executed comprehensive MIHD maintenance across three work streams: 1) File reorganization - detailed 8-step plan to migrate outputs/ directory (benchmark_results→DLPFC, hd_results→HD, rm_ideal split into cross_section/single_section), updated 14 code files + docs, batch-updated archived docs. 2) Patch query fixes - resolved ‘queried patches don’t match’ bugs caused by barcode ordering mismatch between cache (sc.read_10x_h5) and runtime (sc.read_visium), data path inconsistency, coordinate conventions; fixed with explicit barcode-to-cache-index mapping in 4 files. 3) RM-IDEAL benchmark - ran cross-section benchmark on 151673-151508 pair using CPU, obtained baseline metrics (avg Spearman r=0.1804, Layer_4/Layer_1 strong positive, Layer_3/Layer_6 negative). User asked about centroid computation - explained mean embedding vector approach for N-to-M compression.
robobrain_pi (GPU monitoring) ✅ Develop nvitop-style GPU monitor for K8s containers 03:05:47.900 | claude_code User requested improving GPU monitor UI (initial version had wrapping/truncation). Referenced nvitop for layout expectations and requested GPU-centric process grouping (not interested in non-GPU processes). Implemented solution using /proc//fd/ inspection for /dev/nvidia* devices + CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES env var to map processes to GPUs. Built nvitop-style double-border table layout with rich library, colored progress bars, auto-filtering of monitoring tools (nvitop, gpustat, claude). Final gpumon.py supports live refresh (default 2s) and one-shot mode, processes grouped under respective GPU cards.
error-recovery-benchmark (v5 visualization) ❌ Attempt to visualize v5 error scenes (task abandoned) 00:07:14.853 | claude_code User requested visualizing error scenes. Explored codebase and found all v5 tasks already have 480p MP4 videos. User wanted 720p re-rendering and selected ‘All v5 tasks’ with 3-5 samples. Discovered existing videos, proposed re-running 1_v5_execute_injections.py with –render –resolution 720. Entered plan mode but user rejected ExitPlanMode multiple times despite saying ‘just run it now’. After 4+ rejections, user stopped responding. Task abandoned, likely due to environmental constraints or changed priority.
pi05 (VLA training) ✅ Fix torch/torchvision version incompatibility 06:47:41.428 | claude_code User encountered training startup error: ‘RuntimeError: operator torchvision::nms does not exist’. Diagnosed as torch 2.7.1 + torchvision 0.21.0 version mismatch (0.21.0 compiled for torch 2.6.x, incompatible with 2.7.1’s operator registration). User confirmed needed help. Read pyproject.toml, added explicit ’torchvision==0.22.1’ dependency, ran uv sync (upgraded from 0.21.0→0.22.1), verified: torch 2.7.1+cu126, torchvision 0.22.1+cu126. Training environment restored.
VLA Training Performance (openpi vs pi05) ✅ Diagnose and resolve 33% training time gap via dependency alignment 07:07:16.613 | claude_code User reported identical training commands (pi05_robotwin2, batch_size=32, 30K steps) showing different ETAs: openpi 15h vs pi05 20h (33% gap). Launched parallel exploration agents for deep comparison. Stage 1 (initial analysis): Found train.py, configs, model architecture identical; identified differences in lerobot git rev, IMAGE_KEYS (2 vs 3 cameras affecting JIT), conda base envs, dependency versions. Stage 2 (root cause + fix): Pinpointed JAX version (0.5.0 vs 0.5.3) as primary cause of 20-30% performance difference. Systematically aligned 6 shared dependencies in pi05 pyproject.toml to match openpi (JAX, torch, numpy, orbax-checkpoint, beartype, transformers). Added [tool.uv] override-dependencies to resolve lerobot’s torch<2.7 constraint conflict. Completed uv lock/sync successfully. Expected outcome: pi05 now matches openpi’s 15h training time (~25% speedup from JAX 0.5.3 XLA optimizations).
summary: Multi-project productivity: MIHD file restructuring + benchmarking, K8s GPU monitoring tool, VLA training optimization (torch/torchvision fix + 33% speedup via JAX/dependency alignment)
title: Bug Journal 2026-03-11

content:
Daily Report — 2026-03-10 Daily Overview What was done: Cross-device multi-project work spanning documentation engineering (MIHD project cleanup: 16 files archived, README enhanced with RM-IDEAL algorithm), spatial transcriptomics debugging (3 critical barcode alignment bugs fixed), robot learning optimization (VLA training loss analysis identifying 56% gradient dilution, Error Recovery Benchmark v5.0 architecture redesign), infrastructure tooling (GPU monitoring for Kubernetes containers), and environment setup (CALVIN/Openpi/RoboTwin installation fixes, Node.js proxy configuration) How it was done: Systematic approach combining parallel agent exploration for codebase audits, mathematical tensor dimension analysis for loss computation, architectural pattern matching for code-documentation consistency, and layered diagnostic troubleshooting for environment issues. Used git archaeology, barcode-based coordinate alignment, offline two-stage pipeline design, and locale propagation tracing. Impact: MIHD documentation reduced from 25+ files to 4 core docs while exposing 1 critical ImportError bug + 6 dimension discrepancies; spatial visualization now correctly aligns coordinates; Robobrain Pi training efficiency significantly improved; Error Recovery Benchmark architecture redesigned for VLA temporal context requirements; GPU monitoring gap filled for containerized environments; three separate research codebases unblocked (CALVIN, Openpi, RoboTwin) DCC What was done: MIHD spatial transcriptomics project: comprehensive documentation cleanup (16 files archived, README clustering section enhanced with 145-line RM-IDEAL algorithm breakdown, code-documentation consistency audit revealing 1 critical bug + 6 discrepancies), project file cleanup (4-step plan creating 6 reference guides totaling 48.9KB, later deemed over-detailed), outputs directory reorganization (DLPFC/HD separation), removal of deprecated staig_fusion_e2e feature (14 files cleaned), and fixing 3 critical patch query visualization bugs (barcode alignment, coordinate system mismatches) How it was done: Parallel exploration agents for multi-file analysis, bash scripting for bulk file operations, Read/Edit tools for documentation updates, systematic grep-based audit for code-doc consistency, barcode-based coordinate alignment fixes, iterative plan refinement based on user feedback Impact: Established 4-document core structure (README/tutorial/plans/RESULTS), exposed critical ImportError preventing staig_fusion_e2e execution, created comprehensive reference documentation (though later rejected as too detailed by user), improved outputs navigability, fixed patch query spatial alignment issues. User’s iterative feedback revealed preference for ‘minimal usage-oriented’ docs over ‘comprehensive classification guides’ tianhe What was done: Five major threads: (1) Error Recovery Benchmark v5.0 architecture redesign (11 error types × D0/D1/D2 deformation dimensions, offline scan+quota injection pipeline, Error Skill modular design), (2) GPU monitoring tool gpumon.py development (psutil+nvidia-smi TUI for Kubernetes containers), (3) Robobrain Pi VLA training optimization (diagnosed 56% MSE loss dilution from zero-padded action dimensions, corrected task completion classifier imbalance from task_pos_weight=1.0→5.0), (4) Node.js proxy configuration (fixed npx ccusage unable to fetch LiteLLM pricing via HTTPS_PROXY), (5) robot learning environment fixes (CALVIN calvin_agent import, Openpi-moe EGL rendering conflicts, RoboTwin instruction sampling, terminal UTF-8 encoding) How it was done: Parallel Explore/Plan agents for Error Recovery codebase analysis (framework/configs/pipeline), rich+psutil for TUI implementation, mathematical tensor broadcasting analysis (batch×50×32 loss dimensions), curl vs Node.js fetch testing for proxy diagnosis, conda environment isolation verification, CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES GPU index remapping analysis, locale propagation tracing through SSH/tmux stack, code pattern matching against reference implementations Impact: Error Recovery Benchmark redesigned to match VLA temporal context requirements (60% existing code reusable, detectors/injectors/validators require rewrite as Error Skill modules); gpumon.py solved Kubernetes PID namespace isolation monitoring gap; Robobrain Pi training identified severe gradient dilution and class imbalance issues; ccusage restored from 46→2696 models; CALVIN/Openpi/RoboTwin environments unblocked for experiments; CLAUDE.md documentation completed with 4 missing make targets Multi-project development day spanning documentation cleanup, spatial transcriptomics debugging, robot learning optimization, and environment troubleshooting across 6+ codebases
Tasks Architecture & Strategy 🔄 Error Recovery Benchmark v5.0 architecture redesign — Major architectural shift from online physics-force injection (impulse/friction/pose_perturb) to offline two-stage pipeline: Stage 0 collect clean trajectories (human demo + 10×each VLA policy) → Stage 1 offline scan injection opportunities → Stage 2 quota scheduler (100 per task×error category) → Stage 3 context replay (replay N-1 frames for VLA context, inject at frame N). Defined 11 error types (E1-E11) × 3 deformation dimensions (D0/D1/D2) = 26 subtypes. Analyzed v4 codebase: 60% reusable (EnvWrapper/StateExtractor), detectors/injectors/validators require complete rewrite as Error Skill modules. Identified 7 high-feasibility error types via injection + 6 medium + 7 requiring natural capture ❌ MIHD code-documentation consistency audit — Systematic verification of README claims against actual code via 3 parallel exploration agents. Found 1 critical bug: STAIGTrainerE2E.py deleted but Fusion.py + pipeline_config.yaml still reference staig_fusion_e2e → ImportError on invocation. Found 6 dimension/architecture discrepancies: MLP encoder not self-supervised (random init no training), attention fusion 256d not 64d, adaln_attention 256d not 64d, llava_mlp 512d (256×2 concat) not 64d, element_wise_sum min(256,D1,D2) not max(D1,D2), GCN 50d not 64d. User chose ‘cleanup all references’ strategy but rejected plan execution ✅ Fix MIHD patch query visualization coordinate misalignment — Fixed 3 critical bugs in spatial transcriptomics visualization: (1) path inconsistency between cache creation (Dataset/DLPFC/) and viz loading (top-level), (2) barcode alignment missing in score assignment (position-based matching breaks when adata reordered), (3) marker coordinates from cache instead of adata.obsm[‘spatial’]. Applied barcode-based alignment with bc_to_cache_idx dictionary ✅ Diagnose Robobrain Pi training loss computation issues — Investigated loss oscillation pipeline, identified 56% MSE loss dilution from zero-padded dimensions: 14-dim real actions padded to 32-dim for model compatibility, but loss computed over all 32 dims (18/32 = 56% from trivial zero→zero mapping). Recommended masking loss to real dimensions: action_loss = mean(square(v_t[…, :14] - u_t[…, :14])) ✅ MIHD documentation simplification: 25+ files → 4 core docs — Archived 16 files (8 guides + 3 experiment reports + 3 research docs + RM_IDEAL_SCORE + CROSS_SAMPLE_QUERY) to docs/archived/, deleted overview.md (merged into plans.md), updated CLAUDE.md and MEMORY.md indexes. Final structure: README.md (features), tutorial/THOROUGH_TUTORIAL.md (usage), docs/plans.md (project roadmap), docs/RESULTS.md (experiment results) ✅ Optimize Robobrain Pi task completion classifier hyperparameters — Evaluated task_loss_weight (0.1) and task_pos_weight (1.0), identified severe class imbalance (only last 20 frames of ~200-frame episodes are positive = 9:1 ratio). Recommended: keep task_loss_weight=0.1-0.15 (avoid overpowering 50-step action loss), increase task_pos_weight to 5.0 (address imbalance via per-class weighting) ✅ Fix npx ccusage unable to fetch LiteLLM pricing — npx ccusage@latest fallback to cached 46 models (actual: 2696). Root cause: Node.js v24’s built-in fetch (undici) doesn’t use HTTPS_PROXY environment variable by default, 1.4MB JSON download through campus proxy takes ~28s exceeding timeout. Solution: NODE_OPTIONS="–use-env-proxy" npx ccusage@latest ✅ Fix RoboTwin instruction sampling in dataset conversion — convert_robotwin_democlean_to_lerobot.py always selected first ‘seen’ instruction instead of randomly sampling. Changed line 68 from instruction_data.get(‘seen’, [’’])[0] to np.random.choice(seen_instructions) for per-episode randomization, matching reference implementation pattern ✅ Develop GPU monitoring tool for Kubernetes containers — nvidia-smi cannot display processes in Kubernetes pods (PID namespace isolation). Developed gpumon.py combining psutil (container-internal process info: CPU/memory/runtime) with nvidia-smi (GPU hardware state: VRAM/utilization/temperature), using rich library for TUI visualization similar to nvitop ✅ README clustering & evaluation section enhancement — Replaced brief sections (L119-150) with comprehensive 145-line ‘Clustering & Evaluation’ section: evaluation flow diagram, 3 clustering methods (KMeans/mclust/Leiden) with selection logic and zero-variance fallback, spatial majority voting refinement, RM-IDEAL algorithm 5-step breakdown (WWL message passing K=3 → Hamming distance → Wasserstein optimal transport → RM=1-W), cross-section evaluation workflow (Spearman ρ, P@K, SL@K metrics), command reference ✅ Fix CALVIN calvin_agent import failure — install.sh ran in wrong conda environment; reinstalled calvin_models in active univla environment using pip install -e . –no-deps to avoid PyTorch version conflicts ✅ Fix Openpi-moe EGL device ID conflict — CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=6 limits to single GPU remapped as device 0, but MUJOCO_EGL_DEVICE_ID=6 exceeded remapped range causing RuntimeError. Added os.environ.setdefault(‘MUJOCO_EGL_DEVICE_ID’, ‘0’) at script startup in examples/libero/main.py Implementation & Fixes ✅ MIHD project file cleanup and reference guide creation — 4-step execution: (1) Archived 4 dead code files + 11 obsolete experiment dirs + 34 timestamped summaries, (2) Cleaned pipeline_config.yaml (removed image_only configs), (3) Updated 5 docs marking archived items, (4) Created 6 feature guides (GENE_ENCODER/FUSION_STRATEGY/EVALUATION/PIPELINE/VISUALIZATION/FEATURE_CATALOG, 48.9KB total). User immediately requested re-simplification to 4 docs ✅ Remove deprecated staig_fusion_e2e feature from MIHD — Cleaned 14 files to remove all staig_fusion_e2e references (core code, configs, docs), fixed 6 README dimension/description errors (MLP random projection, GCN 50d, fusion strategies), added GCNGeneEncoder to init exports ❌ MIHD outputs directory reorganization — Created comprehensive plan to separate DLPFC/HD datasets and consolidate RM-IDEAL evaluations into outputs/{DLPFC,HD,rm_ideal/{cross_section,single_section},figures,logs}. Approved by user but execution blocked by repeated ExitPlanMode rejection ✅ CLAUDE.md documentation improvements for Error Recovery Benchmark — After multiple /init attempts, successfully identified and added 4 missing VLA make targets to Commands section: analyze-rejected, vla-server-pi0, vla-inject, vla-natural 🔄 Resolve tmux Chinese character rendering issues — Diagnosed root cause as Windows client-side code page configuration (not server/tmux). Chinese displays as underscores due to non-UTF-8 terminal encoding. Solution requires Windows system setting change or chcp 65001 before SSH Problems & Solutions Critical Issues 1. Error Recovery Benchmark v4’s physics-force injection cannot generate VLA-compatible temporal context - errors injected as initial states lack the behavioral history VLA models require Solution: Designed v5.0 architecture with offline two-stage pipeline: Stage 0 collect clean trajectories (human + 10×VLA policy success runs) → Stage 1 offline scan opportunities per frame → Stage 2 quota scheduler (100 per task×category) → Stage 3 context replay (replay first N-1 frames to build VLA context window, inject error at frame N, let VLA freely infer from N onward). Shifted from force-based injection to action-based Error Skill modules (EEF movement/gripper control), each independently handling detect/inject/validate for one error type
Key Insight: VLA models have temporal context dependencies - cannot simply initialize with error states. Errors must ‘grow naturally’ from correct trajectories. Offline scan+quota approach efficiently achieves dataset balance while preserving temporal realism
2. MIHD spatial transcriptomics patch query visualization showing misaligned coordinates - query patches don’t match expected spatial locations Solution: Identified 3 root causes: (1) Dataset/DLPFC/ path vs top-level path mismatch in adata loading, (2) position-based assignment without barcode mapping (cache order ≠ adata order after scanpy operations), (3) marker overlay using cache coords instead of adata.obsm[‘spatial’]. Fixed by using consistent paths, bc_to_cache_idx dictionary for explicit barcode alignment, and barcode-based coordinate lookup
Key Insight: Spatial transcriptomics workflows require explicit barcode-based alignment at every junction (cache→load→render) - implicit position matching fails when adata gets reloaded from different sources or reordered by scanpy. Never assume index preservation across file I/O boundaries
3. Robobrain Pi training loss oscillates significantly - suspected dimension processing issue in action prediction Solution: Deep analysis revealed 14-dim real actions (right arm 6DOF+gripper + left arm 6DOF+gripper) padded to 32-dim for model architectural compatibility, but MSE loss computed over all 32 dimensions. This means 56% of loss (18/32) comes from zero-padded dimensions where model learns trivial noise→zero mapping, severely diluting gradient signals. Recommended: action_loss = mean(square(v_t[…, :14] - u_t[…, :14]))
Key Insight: Zero-padding for architectural compatibility severely dilutes gradient signals when included in loss computation - always mask loss to real dimensions only. In this case, 56% of gradient signal was wasted on learning a trivial mapping. This is a subtle but critical bug affecting training efficiency
4. STAIGTrainerE2E.py file deleted but still referenced in Fusion.py and pipeline_config.yaml, causing ImportError when staig_fusion_e2e strategy is invoked in MIHD pipeline Solution: User chose ‘cleanup all references’ strategy. Removed staig_fusion_e2e from Fusion.py, pipeline_config.yaml, data_preparer.py, pipeline runner, STAIG utils, MEMORY.md, and 8 other files across codebase. Also fixed 6 README dimension/description errors exposed during audit
Key Insight: Orphaned references are critical production bugs. Systematic grep-based audit is essential before file deletion. Automated import tests would catch these earlier. The code-documentation consistency audit (via parallel exploration agents) successfully identified 1 critical + 6 medium discrepancies
5. RoboTwin dataset conversion always uses first instruction from JSON, reducing training diversity for language-conditioned policies Solution: Replaced instruction_data.get(‘seen’, [’’])[0] with np.random.choice(seen_instructions) to randomly sample one instruction per episode, matching pattern in reference implementation convert_aloha_data_to_lerobot_robotwin.py
Key Insight: Dataset conversion should incorporate augmentation at source level - random instruction sampling per episode increases training diversity without requiring multiple dataset versions, directly impacting model generalization to instruction paraphrasing. This is a one-line change with major training impact
6. Robobrain Pi task completion binary classifier may learn trivial ‘always predict 0’ solution despite task_loss_weight=0.1 Solution: Root cause: severe class imbalance (only last 20 frames of ~200-frame episodes are positive = 9:1 negative:positive ratio) combined with task_pos_weight=1.0 (no balancing). Solution requires independent tuning of two hyperparameters: keep task_loss_weight=0.1-0.15 (prevent auxiliary task from overpowering 50-step action prediction), but increase task_pos_weight to 5.0 (give positive class sufficient gradient signal via per-class weighting)
Key Insight: For imbalanced auxiliary classification, per-class weighting (pos_weight) is more effective than overall loss scaling when the task shouldn’t dominate the main objective. loss_weight and pos_weight serve different purposes: loss_weight balances task importance, pos_weight balances class contributions within that task. Must tune independently
7. Node.js ccusage tool cannot fetch latest model pricing from LiteLLM (1.4MB JSON), falls back to cached 46 models instead of 2696 Solution: Diagnosed via layered testing: (1) curl through campus proxy takes 28 seconds for 1.4MB download, (2) Node.js fetch shows ‘getaddrinfo EBUSY’ hostname resolution failure, (3) discovered Node.js v24’s built-in fetch (undici) doesn’t respect HTTPS_PROXY environment variable by default unlike curl/axios. Solution: NODE_OPTIONS="–use-env-proxy" npx ccusage@latest
Key Insight: Node.js v24’s fetch differs fundamentally from traditional HTTP clients (curl/axios/requests) - it ignores environment proxy variables by default. This is a compatibility trap requiring explicit –use-env-proxy flag or global-agent library. Critical for restricted network environments
8. MIHD documentation over-proliferation: created 6 detailed reference guides (48.9KB) when user wanted minimal 4-doc structure Solution: User immediately requested re-simplification after 6-guide completion. Created new plan merging guides back into README (all features what), tutorial (how to use), plans (past/present/future), RESULTS (all experiments). Not yet executed
Key Insight: AI defaulted to ‘comprehensive classification’ approach instead of ‘minimal usage-oriented’ docs. Research code changes fast - detailed guides become stale quickly. Better to have thin integration docs + well-commented code. Should have confirmed user’s preferred granularity before creating detailed structure. User’s first instruction (25+ → 4) should have been constraint throughout
9. Kubernetes container nvidia-smi shows [Not Found] for all GPU processes despite GPU being actively used Solution: Root cause: PID namespace isolation. nvidia-smi queries kernel driver returning host PID (e.g., 3076643), but container PID namespace has different numbering (e.g., 990465). Solution: developed gpumon.py tool combining psutil (reads container-internal /proc for process info: CPU/memory/cmdline/runtime) with nvidia-smi (GPU hardware state: VRAM/utilization/temperature), visualized via rich TUI
Key Insight: Container namespace isolation breaks cross-namespace process ID mapping. Tools must collect information within their respective namespaces then aggregate. This is why standard monitoring tools fail in containerized environments - they assume unified PID space
10. Openpi-moe serve_policy.py crashes with ‘MUJOCO_EGL_DEVICE_ID must be between 0 and 0, got 6’ Solution: CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=6 restricts process to single GPU remapped as device 0 in CUDA context, but EGL renderer attempted to use original hardware index 6. Added os.environ.setdefault(‘MUJOCO_EGL_DEVICE_ID’, ‘0’) at script startup to align with CUDA remapping
Key Insight: CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES remaps GPU indices to 0-N range for isolated process; dependent libraries (MuJoCo EGL, rendering contexts) must use remapped indices not original hardware IDs. This is subtle but critical interaction between CUDA device isolation and rendering frameworks. Always set rendering device IDs to 0 when using CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES with single GPU
11. CALVIN calvin_agent module not found despite install.sh completing successfully Solution: Discovered pip install executed in different conda environment than active univla environment. Editable installs create .egg-info directories but package availability is scoped to Python environment executing the install. Reinstalled with pip install -e . –no-deps in correct environment to avoid overwriting PyTorch
Key Insight: Editable pip installs are environment-scoped - artifacts may exist but imports fail if installation ran elsewhere. Always verify ‘which python’ matches intended environment before troubleshooting missing modules. –no-deps flag allows installing package structure without touching existing dependencies
General Issues 12. Chinese characters render as underscores in tmux session, but other users on same server see correctly Solution: Traced issue through entire chain: server locale correct (C.UTF-8), tmux configured for UTF-8, but Windows client terminal not passing UTF-8 encoding through SSH. Root cause is client-side: Windows PowerShell SSH doesn’t properly negotiate UTF-8 encoding. Solution: Enable ‘Beta: Use Unicode UTF-8’ in Windows Region Settings or run chcp 65001 before SSH connection
Key Insight: Terminal encoding issues propagate through multiple layers (client terminal → SSH protocol → tmux → shell). When one user experiences problems but others don’t on same server, investigate client-side configuration first. Comparative observation (‘works for others’) immediately narrows problem domain from server to client
Human vs AI Approaches Strategic Level Error Recovery architecture redesign decision-making rhythm Role Approach Human User has clear vision (11 error types × D0/D1/D2, offline scan+quota, Error Skill modules) but thoughts still evolving, multiple times said ‘有新想法’ and rejected AI’s questions/confirmations. Needs exploratory flexibility without premature commitment AI AI attempted to converge quickly via AskUserQuestion and ExitPlanMode, trying to lock in decisions between ‘Injection+Natural’ vs ‘only 7 high-feasibility types’ options. Wanted to move from planning to execution phase Difference Analysis: User requires exploration flexibility while ideas mature - doesn’t want forced decisions before thinking is complete. AI seeks rapid convergence to executable plan. User rejected 3-4 consecutive AskUserQuestion/ExitPlanMode calls, clear signal AI pushed too early for implementation. Shows AI should wait for user to crystallize vision before seeking commitment
MIHD patch query visualization debugging Role Approach Human User reported ‘sometimes query patches don’t match current patch’ - empirical observation from visual inspection and interactive testing AI AI systematically traced data pipelines (cache creation path vs viz loading path, barcode ordering assumptions, coordinate systems) and found 3 distinct architectural bugs Difference Analysis: Human detected symptom through visual QA; AI performed architectural debugging to find root causes. Human insight necessary to trigger investigation, but AI’s systematic tracing across file I/O boundaries identified all failure points. Shows value of visual sanity checks even in automated pipelines
Documentation organization philosophy for research codebases Role Approach Human Minimal usage-oriented structure: 4 files answering ‘what are features’ (README), ‘how to use’ (tutorial), ‘what’s the plan’ (plans), ‘what are results’ (RESULTS). User explicitly stated ‘我只需要四个’ after AI created 6 guides. Values integration over separation, action-orientation over completeness AI Comprehensive classification approach: created 6 specialized guides (GENE_ENCODER_GUIDE, FUSION_STRATEGY_GUIDE, EVALUATION_GUIDE, etc.) organizing features by type with detailed API references, parameters, performance tables. Prioritizes logical categorization and completeness Difference Analysis: Human prioritizes minimal cognitive load and action-oriented docs (‘I need to use X, where do I look?’). AI prioritizes completeness and logical structure (‘all encoder info in one place’). Human’s iterative rejection shows research code needs thin docs that stay maintainable, not comprehensive manuals that become stale. Fast-changing codebases favor integration over classification
Robobrain Pi loss computation correctness diagnosis Role Approach Human User noticed loss oscillation and suspected dimension mismatch, specifically asked to verify if 14-dim actions → loss computation correct after post-processing. Intuited problem from training behavior patterns AI AI traced full data flow (RealInputs pad 14→32 → DeltaActions mask only [:14] → Normalization → loss on all 32 dims), quantified exact gradient dilution ratio (56% from padding), identified architectural root cause (loss not masked to real dims) Difference Analysis: Human pattern-recognized anomaly from empirical observation; AI performed systematic architectural analysis to quantify root cause. Complementary: human intuition triggers investigation, AI provides mathematical precision. Human correctly identified dimensional structure concern before analysis; AI needed prompt to reconsider tensor shapes and revise task_loss_weight recommendation from 0.5→0.1-0.15
tmux encoding root cause identification strategy Role Approach Human Observed other users on same server see Chinese correctly, and characters typed by user render properly in others’ terminals - immediately pivoted diagnosis to client-side issue based on comparative behavior AI Initially focused on server-side locale configuration (checking LANG, LC_ALL, tmux settings, shell environment) following systematic diagnostic path until human observation redirected investigation Difference Analysis: Human comparative observation (‘works for others, not for me’) immediately narrowed problem domain from server config to client setup. AI followed comprehensive server-side diagnostic path before crucial pivot. Demonstrates value of asking ‘does this work for anyone else?’ early in troubleshooting - differential diagnosis eliminates shared components
Bug fix strategy for missing STAIGTrainerE2E.py Role Approach Human Chose ‘cleanup all references’ option immediately without discussion - prefers aggressive removal of dead features rather than code archaeology to restore functionality AI Offered two balanced options: (1) restore file from git (preserve e2e functionality), (2) cleanup all references. AI recommended option 1 (‘推荐’) to maintain feature completeness Difference Analysis: Human treats missing file as signal feature should be removed (‘delete harder’). AI treats it as recoverable bug (‘restore to preserve capability’). Human’s decision suggests staig_fusion_e2e was intentionally abandoned, not accidentally deleted. Shows different risk tolerances: human willing to lose features for simplicity, AI defaults to preserving capabilities
RoboTwin instruction sampling pattern recognition Role Approach Human Identified reference implementation in convert_aloha_data_to_lerobot_robotwin.py and recognized np.random.choice pattern should be replicated - learning from existing codebase AI Located both files, compared implementations, applied mechanical code transformation following human-provided pattern Difference Analysis: Human provided domain knowledge (where to find reference and that it represents correct behavior); AI performed file location and pattern matching. Shows importance of learning from existing codebase patterns rather than inventing new approaches - codebase consistency matters
Iterative refinement vs upfront specification Role Approach Human Provided high-level requirement (‘25+ docs → 4 core’), let AI execute, then course-corrected when result (6 guides) didn’t match vision. Iterative rejection-based refinement style AI Interpreted ‘4 core docs’ as ‘consolidate 12 existing guides’ → created intermediate 6-guide structure → then planned 4-doc consolidation. Multi-step approach assuming more structure is safer Difference Analysis: Human has clear end-state vision but provides minimal specification, expecting AI to converge through feedback cycles. AI adds intermediate steps for safety but increases iteration count. Human’s frustration (‘docs too many’) indicates preference for direct paths over incremental consolidation
AI Limitations Critical Limitations Poor recognition of user rejection signals: During Error Recovery redesign, user rejected AskUserQuestion/ExitPlanMode 3-4 consecutive times, but AI continued similar questioning patterns (‘你想怎么处理?’, ‘请描述你的新想法’) instead of recognizing need to stop asking and wait for user to articulate vision autonomously Over-engineering documentation: Created 6 detailed reference guides (48.9KB) despite user’s explicit initial instruction to consolidate 25+ files into 4 core docs. Failed to maintain original constraint throughout multi-session workflow. Context loss across sessions: Session 4 created 6 guides after Session 1 archived 16 files to reach 4 docs - should have recognized contradiction Overuse of AskUserQuestion tool during exploratory phase: Tried to force user into binary decisions (‘7 high + 6 medium injection’ vs ‘only 7 high-feasibility types’) when user’s thinking was still evolving. Limited user’s thought space instead of providing information and waiting for autonomous decision Lack of confirmation before large execution: Did not ask ‘Should I create 6 separate guides or merge everything into 4 files?’ before Step 4 execution in project cleanup. User immediately rejected output, wasting implementation effort Multiple CLAUDE.md improvement misjudgments: User ran /init, AI concluded ‘already comprehensive, no changes needed’, attempted ExitPlanMode twice before user rejection. Only on third attempt found 4 missing make targets. Shows overly conservative assessment of ‘good enough’ Plan mode execution stalled repeatedly when user rejected ExitPlanMode tool calls - agent did not adapt by asking user to manually exit or finding alternative workflow, instead kept retrying same blocked tool call in loop Initial task_loss_weight recommendation (0.5) overlooked dimensional structure of action_loss (batch×50 tensor) until human prompted reconsideration - should have analyzed tensor shapes more carefully upfront before making recommendation General Limitations Insufficient integration of parallel agent results: Error Recovery exploration launched 3 parallel agents (framework, configs, full codebase) each returning 2000-3000 tokens, but AI didn’t efficiently synthesize into concise action items - instead pasted large exploration dumps Technical limitations: 403 ‘Request not allowed’ API errors at session start requiring /login; multiple ripgrep timeouts on large directories (20-second limits); file modification conflicts during Edit operations due to external linters/formatters Learnings Key Learnings VLA model temporal context dependency requires fundamental benchmark redesign: Cannot simply initialize episodes with error states - VLA needs behavioral history to make contextually appropriate recovery decisions. Solution: replay first N-1 frames to build context window, inject error at frame N, let VLA infer freely from N onward. This shifts error injection from ‘initial state perturbation’ to ’trajectory interruption’, matching real-world failure modes where errors emerge during execution Offline scan + quota scheduler architecture for balanced error datasets: Stage 0 collect clean trajectories (human + 10×VLA policy success runs) → Stage 1 offline scan opportunities per frame → Stage 2 quota allocation (100 per task×error category) → Stage 3 context replay. More efficient than online generation, guarantees dataset balance, enables reuse of expensive clean trajectory collection across multiple error injection experiments Error taxonomy deformation dimension classification: D0 (position only), D1 (rotation only), D2 (position+rotation) systematically describes spatial deformations across all error types. More principled than phase-based classification (reach/grasp/lift/transport/place), enables clearer validator logic by decomposing 6DOF changes into orthogonal components Zero-padding architectural compatibility trap: Padding for model compatibility (14→32 dims) can severely dilute gradient signals (56% waste) if included in loss computation. Always mask loss to cover only meaningful dimensions. In multi-dimensional outputs, this is subtle but critical - the model converges but learns inefficiently because majority of gradient signal optimizes trivial zero→zero mappings Independent hyperparameter tuning for imbalanced auxiliary tasks: loss_weight (balances task importance) and pos_weight (balances class contributions within task) serve different purposes and must be tuned independently. For auxiliary classification under severe imbalance: keep loss_weight low (0.1-0.15) to avoid overpowering main objective, increase pos_weight (e.g., 5.0) to address class imbalance. Overall scaling vs per-class weighting are orthogonal controls Spatial transcriptomics barcode-based alignment requirement: Explicit barcode mapping required at every pipeline junction (cache→load→render). Position-based matching breaks when adata reloaded from different sources or reordered by scanpy operations. Never assume index preservation across file I/O boundaries. Solution: maintain bc_to_cache_idx dictionaries throughout pipeline, use barcode lookup for all coordinate assignments MIHD architecture deep understanding: 3-stage pipeline (Encoders → Fusion → Clustering/Evaluation), 5 gene encoders (PCA 50d, MLP 128d random init, scGPT 512d, GCN 50d, none), 4 vision encoders (ResNet50, HIPT, UNI, UNI2), 10 fusion strategies (concat/mean/element_wise_sum + attention variants + STAIG/QFormer). Two-phase: Phase 1 extraction (cache to embeddings_cache/), Phase 2 evaluation (load + fuse + cluster + metrics) Dataset conversion augmentation at source level: Random instruction sampling per episode (np.random.choice from available paraphrases) increases training diversity without requiring multiple dataset versions. One-line change with major impact on language-conditioned policy generalization. Shows importance of pattern matching against reference implementations in same codebase CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES GPU index remapping: Restricting to subset remaps indices to 0-N range within process CUDA context. Dependent libraries (MuJoCo EGL, rendering frameworks) must use remapped indices not original hardware IDs. Always set rendering device IDs to 0 when using single-GPU isolation. Subtle but critical for multi-GPU setups where different jobs run on isolated device subsets Injection vs Natural Capture applicability: Physics-based injection (impulse/friction/pose_perturb) reliably generates 7 error types (DROP, COLLISION, POSITIONING, STALL partially). GRASP_FAILURE/SEQUENCING/TRAJECTORY/REGRESSION require policy temporal behavior - only obtainable via natural capture (VLA rollout + PolicyErrorMonitor detection). Hybrid approach needed for complete error coverage RM-IDEAL spatial niche matching algorithm: (1) Build spatial adjacency graph (k-NN, n_neighbors=6), (2) WWL message passing (K=3 iterations aggregate neighbor labels), (3) Compute Hamming distance cost matrix, (4) Wasserstein optimal transport, (5) RM = 1 - W ∈ [0,1]. Cross-section evaluation: bidirectional query, compare RM-IDEAL scores vs embedding cosine similarity via Spearman ρ, P@K, SL@K metrics Kubernetes PID namespace isolation breaks monitoring: nvidia-smi queries kernel driver returning host PIDs, but container has independent PID namespace. Solution: collect information within respective namespaces then aggregate. For GPU monitoring: psutil in container for process info (/proc parsing), nvidia-smi for hardware state, visualize via rich TUI. General principle: namespace isolation requires namespace-local data collection Documentation philosophy for research codebases: Users prefer ‘minimal usage-oriented’ structure (what/how/plan/results) over ‘comprehensive classification’ (detailed per-feature guides). Rationale: research code changes fast, detailed guides become stale quickly. Better to have thin integration docs + well-commented code than thick reference manuals. AI should default to minimal docs and ask before creating comprehensive structures Code-documentation consistency audit workflow: (1) Launch parallel exploration agents per module, (2) Cross-reference README claims against actual code (class names, dimensions, parameters, training procedures), (3) Categorize discrepancies (critical breaking / medium incorrect / low omission), (4) User chooses fix strategy (update docs vs fix code). Revealed 1 critical ImportError + 6 dimension mismatches in MIHD project AI behavior during exploratory phases: When user thinking still evolving (multiple ‘有新想法’ statements), avoid forcing decisions via AskUserQuestion. Provide information, analyze trade-offs, wait for autonomous decision. Consecutive rejected ExitPlanMode/AskUserQuestion calls are clear ‘pause’ signals. User needs exploration space, not premature commitment to implementation path Node.js v24 fetch proxy compatibility trap: Built-in fetch (undici) doesn’t respect HTTPS_PROXY/HTTP_PROXY environment variables unlike curl/axios/requests. Requires explicit NODE_OPTIONS="–use-env-proxy" or global-agent library. Critical for restricted network environments. Represents breaking behavior change from traditional HTTP clients Editable pip install environment scoping: Package availability scoped to Python environment executing install command. Artifacts (.egg-info) may exist but imports fail if installed elsewhere. Always verify ‘which python’ matches intended environment. Use –no-deps to install package structure without overwriting existing dependencies (critical for avoiding PyTorch version conflicts) MIHD clustering methods: KMeans (default, fast), mclust (R GMM via rpy2, used for STAIG, has zero-variance fallback → KMeans), Leiden (auto cluster estimation for HD data without ground truth). Spatial refinement via k-NN majority voting (refine_labels_spatial_majority) only for STAIG fusion. Selection logic based on fusion strategy and data characteristics Terminal encoding troubleshooting: Issues propagate through multiple layers (client terminal → SSH protocol → tmux → shell). When one user has problems but others don’t on same server, investigate client-side first. Comparative observation (‘works for others’) immediately narrows from shared components to user-specific config. For Windows: check code page (chcp 65001) or enable ‘Beta: Use Unicode UTF-8’ in Region Settings Conversation Summaries MIHD (Multi-modal Integration for High-Definition Spatial Omics) • Comprehensive documentation restructuring and cleanup 2026-03-10 | claude_code Five-session arc: (1) Archived 16 files, consolidated to 4 core docs (README/tutorial/plans/RESULTS). (2) Enhanced README with 145-line clustering & RM-IDEAL evaluation section (WWL message passing, Wasserstein transport, cross-section metrics). (3) Code-documentation consistency audit via 3 parallel agents - found 1 critical bug (STAIGTrainerE2E.py missing but referenced → ImportError) + 6 dimension discrepancies. (4) Project cleanup: archived 4 dead code files + 11 obsolete dirs + 34 summaries, cleaned pipeline_config.yaml, created 6 reference guides (48.9KB) - user immediately rejected as too detailed. (5) Began outputs/ reorganization (DLPFC/HD separation) but blocked by ExitPlanMode rejection loop. Also: removed staig_fusion_e2e feature from 14 files, fixed 3 critical patch query visualization bugs (barcode alignment, coordinate system mismatches). Overall: reduced documentation from 25+ files to 4, exposed critical ImportError risk, improved spatial visualization correctness, but revealed tension between AI’s comprehensive classification approach vs user’s minimal usage-oriented philosophy
RoboBrain Pi (Bimanual VLA Training) ✅ Training loss computation debugging and optimization 2026-03-10 | claude_code Three-part optimization: (1) Diagnosed loss oscillation - identified 56% MSE loss dilution from zero-padded action dimensions (14-dim real actions padded to 32-dim for model compatibility, but loss computed over all 32). Recommended masking to real dims. (2) Task completion classifier hyperparameter evaluation - found severe class imbalance (9:1 negative:positive ratio from last 20 frames of 200-frame episodes) with task_pos_weight=1.0 providing no balancing. Recommended task_loss_weight=0.1-0.15 (avoid overpowering 50-step action loss) + task_pos_weight=5.0 (address imbalance). (3) Environment troubleshooting: reinstalled uv package manager, set WandB offline mode, handled multi-user WandB account conflicts via WANDB_API_KEY env var, debugged Chinese terminal rendering (client-side UTF-8 encoding issue). Also verified task completion implementation mathematically correct (CLS token + MLP head, allow_partial=True for param mismatch). Impact: identified two critical training efficiency bugs (gradient dilution + class imbalance), provided concrete hyperparameter recommendations, resolved development environment usability issues
Error Recovery Benchmark 🔍 v5.0 architecture redesign and planning 2026-03-10 | claude_code Major architectural shift spanning multiple sessions: (1) Initial /init attempts to improve CLAUDE.md (multiple rejections before successfully adding 4 missing make targets). (2) User presented redesign vision: move from online physics-force injection to offline two-stage pipeline (collect clean trajectories → scan opportunities → quota injection → context replay). Defined 11 error types × 3 deformation dimensions (D0/D1/D2) = 26 subtypes. (3) Launched 3 parallel exploration agents to analyze v4 codebase - identified 60% reusable components (EnvWrapper, StateExtractor) vs complete rewrite needed (detectors/injectors/validators → Error Skill modules). (4) Provided detailed 24-type generation feasibility analysis: 7 high-feasibility via injection, 6 medium, 7 requiring natural capture. User indicated ’new ideas’ multiple times but rejected AI’s AskUserQuestion attempts to force decisions. Plan remains in exploratory phase - user needs flexibility while vision crystallizes. Key insight: VLA temporal context dependency requires errors to ‘grow from correct trajectories’ rather than being initial state perturbations. Offline scan+quota architecture more efficient and achieves better dataset balance
Infrastructure Tools ✅ GPU monitoring and Node.js tooling 2026-03-10 | claude_code Two infrastructure improvements: (1) Developed gpumon.py for Kubernetes containers - nvidia-smi shows [Not Found] for processes due to PID namespace isolation (host PID 3076643 ≠ container PID 990465). Solution combines psutil (container-internal process info: CPU/memory/runtime) + nvidia-smi (GPU hardware state: VRAM/utilization/temperature) with rich TUI visualization. User interrupted during layout optimization. (2) Fixed npx ccusage unable to fetch LiteLLM model pricing (fallback 46→2696 models). Root cause: Node.js v24’s fetch (undici) doesn’t respect HTTPS_PROXY by default, 1.4MB JSON takes 28s through campus proxy exceeding timeout. Solution: NODE_OPTIONS="–use-env-proxy". Impact: filled critical monitoring gap in containerized environments, restored token pricing tool functionality in restricted networks
Robot Learning Environments (CALVIN, Openpi-moe, RoboTwin) ✅ Multi-project environment setup and debugging 2026-03-10 | claude_code Systematic debugging across three projects: (1) CALVIN: fixed calvin_agent import failure - install.sh ran in wrong conda environment, reinstalled with pip install -e . –no-deps in univla environment. (2) Openpi-moe: diagnosed serve_policy.py crash - CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=6 remapped GPU to index 0, but MUJOCO_EGL_DEVICE_ID=6 exceeded remapped range. Fixed by setting EGL device to 0 at script startup. (3) RoboTwin: modified dataset conversion to randomly sample instructions (np.random.choice) instead of always using first instruction, matching reference implementation pattern for training diversity. Also: terminal UTF-8 encoding troubleshooting (Windows client-side code page issue), Claude Code API config file location query. Impact: unblocked three critical research pipelines, enabled robot learning experiments to proceed. All issues traced to subtle environment/configuration mismatches requiring careful layer-by-layer diagnosis
summary: Multi-project development day spanning documentation cleanup, spatial transcriptomics debugging, robot learning optimization, and environment troubleshooting across 6+ codebases
title: Bug Journal 2026-03-10

content:
Daily Report — 2026-03-09 Daily Overview What was done: 在三个设备上并行推进五个主要方向：(1)MIHD空间转录组项目核心算法开发（跨样本结构评估+跨模态增强架构），(2)VLA/机器人学习系统开发（文档、数据管道、理论分析），(3)gadget工具架构转型（CLI→MCP服务器），(4)跨项目集成（CalendarPro自动化工作流），(5)研究工具增强（多数据源+生产级日志） How it was done: 使用WWL图核建立空间结构评估基准、设计基于空间邻域的cross-attention机制（并解决CUDA硬件兼容性问题）、系统性文献调研foundation model性能、创建MCP协议包装层暴露工具、通过conda subprocess实现跨环境调用、扩展research_scout到三个学术数据源（arXiv/bioRxiv/PubMed）、建立生产级日志系统 Impact: 建立可复用的跨样本空间评估工具链和跨模态增强方法、揭示scGPT foundation model不如传统方法的关键发现、使gadget工具可被所有AI agent发现和使用、实现CalendarPro自动化每日研究和总结工作流、将研究覆盖范围从arXiv单源扩展到三大学术来源并建立完整的错误追踪机制 DCC What was done: MIHD空间转录组项目全流程工作：跨样本RM-IDEAL实验与可视化（151673-151508/151676）、CrossModalEnhancer设计与实现（含多轮CUDA调试）、CLAUDE.md维护、scGPT文献调研 How it was done: 在HPC环境运行大规模实验（3639 spots），使用conda环境管理+多轮CUDA错误调试（最终采用mini-batch workaround和CPU训练fallback）、执行并行web search和WebFetch获取benchmark数据、生成空间热力图可视化 Impact: 产出完整的跨样本评估报告（7张空间热力图+中文技术文档）、交付可工作的CrossModalEnhancer模块（已集成到Fusion.py）、获得scGPT性能的关键洞察（zero-shot弱于传统方法）、识别PyTorch CUDA版本兼容性问题并建立CPU训练fallback流程 TzJsDesktop What was done: 全栈集成与工具增强工作：创建workspace文档（5个项目）、gadget MCP服务器实现（9个工具）、CalendarPro-gadget集成桥接（13个测试）、research_scout增强（日志系统+多数据源，500+行新增代码）、文档维护更新 How it was done: 使用并行agent编排进行代码库探索、系统性逐文件实现并在每阶段验证、通过subprocess隔离实现跨repo集成、实现全面测试（import检查、CLI验证、日志文件验证）、使用urllib.request实现零依赖API客户端 Impact: 建立统一开发环境与清晰文档、使gadget工具可被工作区所有AI agent访问、实现CalendarPro自动化每日研究工作流、将research_scout从arXiv单源转变为三源系统并具备生产级可观测性、保持所有工具文档与代码同步 tianhe What was done: 多VLA/机器人项目推进：UniVLA文档创建、CALVIN环境故障排查、RoboTwin→LeRobot数据转换、Pi0 flow matching理论分析 How it was done: 创建全面的CLAUDE.md（build/test命令、架构概览）、诊断DNS解析失败（Kubernetes环境）、实现convert_robotwin_democlean_to_lerobot.py（14-DOF，50 episodes，11,459 frames）、详解条件flow matching（Beta(1.5,1)时间采样+Euler积分） Impact: 建立复杂VLA代码库的文档标准、支持demo_clean数据复用于pi0.5训练、阐明flow matching实现细节用于机器人动作生成 完成MIHD空间转录组核心功能（跨样本RM-IDEAL评估、CrossModalEnhancer跨模态增强、scGPT文献调研）、推进多个VLA/机器人项目（UniVLA文档、RoboTwin数据转换、flow matching分析）、实现gadget工具MCP服务器化与CalendarPro集成、增强research_scout多数据源支持
Tasks Architecture & Strategy ✅ Run 151673-151508 cross-sample RM-IDEAL benchmark — Execute benchmark comparing spatial niche structure similarity between two DLPFC sections using PCA+UNI2+STAIG_fusion embeddings. Generated detailed correlation metrics (Spearman ρ) for all 7 tissue layers in both directions. ✅ Implement CrossModalEnhancer module — Created models/CrossModalEnhancer.py (~350 lines) with CrossModalAttentionBlock and CrossModalEnhancer classes supporting 3 modes (gene→image, image→gene, bidirectional). Integrated into Fusion.py dispatcher. ✅ Debug CUDA/cuBLAS compatibility issues — Resolved PyTorch 2.9/CUDA 12.9 cuBLAS bug on RTX 2080 Ti causing crashes on large matrix operations. Final solution: mini-batch (512 samples) InfoNCE loss to avoid N×N full-matrix backward. ✅ Research scGPT clustering benchmark literature — Conducted systematic web search and literature extraction to evaluate scGPT and scGPT-spatial clustering performance. Found zero-shot scGPT underperforms traditional methods (PCA, scVI) and scGPT-spatial lacks independent SOTA comparisons. ✅ Create CLAUDE.md for UniVLA repository — Comprehensive documentation covering build commands (torchrun, accelerate), linting (black/ruff), training stages (LAM, VLA pretraining, finetuning), evaluation (LIBERO, CALVIN, R2R, SimplerEnv), and architecture overview (VLM→PrismaticVLM→OpenVLA, vision/LLM backbones, action tokenization, draccus config, RLDS pipeline) ✅ Refactor MCP tools to return content — Modified 5 MCP tools (summarize_export/merge, research_report, benchmark_run/report) to return full content (markdown/JSON) with optional save parameter, bypassing cmd_* wrappers to control file I/O ✅ Transform gadget tools into MCP server — Created mcp_server.py exposing 9 tools (summarize_export/merge/list_dates, research_search/report/list_projects, benchmark_run/report/system_info) with stdout capture and SystemExit handling ✅ Integrate gadget tools into CalendarPro — Built external tool integration layer (src/tools/) with async subprocess runner via conda run, implemented ResearchScoutTool and DailySummaryTool, created background services with cron scheduling, wrote 13 unit tests ✅ Add bioRxiv and PubMed support to research_scout — Implemented search_biorxiv() and search_pubmed() with urllib.request (zero new dependencies), added multi-source orchestration with unified caching, extended CLI with –source flag, generalized paper_id/arxiv_id handling with backward compatibility ✅ Generate RM-IDEAL spatial heatmap visualizations — Modified _viz_rm_ideal.py to generate 7 PNG files (one per niche label) showing ground truth, RM-IDEAL scores, and embedding similarity side-by-side for 151673↔151508. ✅ Write comprehensive cross-sample patch query documentation (Chinese) — Created docs/CROSS_SAMPLE_PATCH_QUERY_REPORT.md with complete methodology (WWL graph kernel, Wasserstein distance, two-path evaluation), code paths, data flow, results analysis, and reproduction guide. ❌ MIHD cross-sample patch visualization with STAIG fusion — Implement 151673→151676 patch query using UNI2+PCA+STAIG_fusion embeddings and visualization. Blocked by GPU CUDA compatibility issue (PyTorch 2.9.0+cu129 vs CUDA 13.1 driver incompatibility). CPU training started as workaround. ✅ Convert RoboTwin demo_clean to LeRobot format — Created convert_robotwin_democlean_to_lerobot.py script to convert 50 episodes (11,459 frames) from demo_clean (14-DOF HDF5 format) to LeRobot dataset structure at RoboTwin/data/place_dual_shoes_lerobot ✅ Add logging system to research_scout — Implemented RotatingFileHandler (5MB, 3 backups), migrated ~77 print statements to logger, added empty-field and zero-score warnings, enhanced error handling in API calls and cache operations ✅ Design CrossModalEnhancer architecture — Architected cross-modal enhancement module using spatial k-NN neighbors as K/V sequence to avoid single-token attention degradation. Designed symmetric InfoNCE loss and residual connections. ✅ Analyze Pi0 flow matching implementation — Explained conditional flow matching in pi0.py: linear interpolation path x_t = t·noise + (1-t)·actions, Beta(1.5,1) time sampling, MSE loss ‖v_t - u_t‖², Euler method sampling with 10 steps from t=1→0 ✅ Analyze CALVIN training data pipeline — Detailed explanation of how finetune_calvin.py uses DiskCalvinDataset to load auto_lang_ann.npy, episode_*.npz files, extract rgb_static/rel_actions/robot_obs, pass through LAM encoder and VLA with ActionDecoder Implementation & Fixes ✅ Create CALVIN dataset extraction script — Created extract_eval_files.py to extract only eval-needed files (validation/.hydra/merged_config.yaml, metadata) from full dataset, reducing size from 1.3GB to 600KB ✅ Create workspace-level CLAUDE.md — Analyzed 5 independent projects (CalendarPro, OCR, gadget, get-shit-done, openclaw) and created unified CLAUDE.md with quick reference commands and architecture highlights ✅ Update CLAUDE.md documentation for research_scout.py — Identified and documented previously undocumented features: author search (–author flag, search_arxiv_author), init –from-overview (LLM-based project creation), updated line count from ~2500 to ~2650, added author search cache pattern ✅ Update CLAUDE.md with cross-modal and RM-IDEAL content — Added CrossModalEnhancer to core modules, RM-IDEAL benchmark commands to common commands, cross-sample documentation references, and enriched utils descriptions (rm_ideal.py, niche_utils.py). ✅ Streamline CLAUDE.md module listings — Condensed Architecture section from ~30 lines of per-module function lists to ~10 lines focusing on non-obvious module relationships. Preserved all critical architectural patterns. ❌ Troubleshoot CALVIN environment installation — Diagnosed DNS resolution failure (Errno -3) preventing pip from reaching pypi.tuna.tsinghua.edu.cn or pypi.org in Kubernetes environment. Identified proxy config (http_proxy=localhost:9997) but didn’t resolve issue. Problems & Solutions Critical Issues 1. PyTorch 2.9/CUDA 12.9 cuBLAS bug on RTX 2080 Ti causes crashes during backward pass of large matrix operations (3639×3639 logits for contrastive loss). Multiple architecture rewrites attempted before identifying root cause. Solution: Redesigned contrastive loss to use mini-batch sampling (512 samples per batch) to avoid full N×N matrix computation. Added gradient clipping and LayerNorm for stability. For cross-sample visualization task, switched to CPU training as fallback when GPU incompatibility confirmed (PyTorch 2.9.0+cu129 vs CUDA 13.1 driver).
Key Insight: GPU hardware/driver compatibility issues cannot be solved through model architecture alone—algorithmic workarounds at the loss function level are necessary. Version mismatches between PyTorch CUDA build and driver version cause sporadic ‘illegal memory access’ errors that pass basic tests but fail in complex training loops. CPU fallback is valid strategy for medium-scale training (3460 spots).
2. MCP server stdout corruption: gadget tools use print() extensively, but MCP uses stdout as JSON-RPC transport Solution: Created capture_stdout() context manager to redirect print() output, wrapped all tool invocations with _safe_call() that captures stdout/stderr and converts SystemExit to RuntimeError, returned captured logs in JSON response’s _logs field
Key Insight: When integrating existing CLI tools into protocol-based systems, stdout isolation is critical. Context managers provide clean, reversible redirection without modifying original code
3. research_scout hardcoded arxiv_id throughout codebase, blocking multi-source expansion Solution: Introduced paper_id as canonical ID with source field, kept arxiv_id as backward-compatible alias, created _paper_id()/_paper_url() helpers, updated prompts to use paper_id, made merge logic check both fields when matching LLM responses
Key Insight: When generalizing from single-source to multi-source, introduce new abstraction while maintaining old field as alias. Helper functions centralize ID/URL logic, making future source additions trivial
4. RM-IDEAL correlation low for Layer_3 (ρ=-0.21/-0.36) in 151673-151508 cross-sample query, while Layer_1/4/5 show high positive correlation (ρ>0.4) Solution: Analysis revealed Layer_3 is the largest DLPFC layer with high internal heterogeneity, making embeddings unable to capture unified spatial structure. Smaller, unique layers with clear boundaries (Layer_1: ρ=0.62, Layer_4: ρ=0.66) perform best.
Key Insight: Tissue layer size and internal heterogeneity critically affect embedding quality for spatial structure capture. Distinct, boundary-clear layers yield better cross-sample matching than large heterogeneous regions.
5. Single-token cross-attention degenerates to linear projection when each spot has only one gene vector and one image vector Solution: Used spatial k-NN neighbors to construct K/V sequences, allowing cross-attention to operate on neighborhood context. Reused QFormerFusion’s batched neighbor construction infrastructure.
Key Insight: Spatial transcriptomics data provides natural sequential context through neighborhood structure. Leveraging spatial adjacency enables meaningful attention even without temporal or batch dimensions.
6. RoboTwin demo_clean data (14-DOF, directory-based) incompatible with existing LeRobot conversion script (16-DOF, zip-based) Solution: Created new script convert_robotwin_democlean_to_lerobot.py: read directly from data/instructions directories, change ACTION_DIM/STATE_DIM to 14, use LeRobotDataset.create() with root parameter instead of HF_LEROBOT_HOME env var hack
Key Insight: Robot data format variations (DOF count, storage format) require tailored conversion scripts. Directory structure and HDF5 key names are project-specific.
7. Empty motivation/innovation_point fields in research_scout screening stage due to silent LLM JSON parsing failures Solution: Added empty-field detection after _screen_papers() and _screen_papers_chunked(), logged warnings with count and DEBUG-level per-paper details, enhanced _try_repair_result() to log raw response preview when repair fails
Key Insight: Silent .get() fallbacks mask data quality issues. Explicit validation and logging at aggregation points (after batch processing) provides actionable debugging information without cluttering per-item logic
8. Initial CrossModalEnhancer design outputting original modality dimension (1536d for image) triggered cuBLAS GEMM failure on 128→1536 projection Solution: Revised architecture to output in hidden_dim space (128d) instead, avoiding problematic large-matrix projections. Used projection heads for contrastive loss while keeping core output compact.
Key Insight: Engineering constraints (GPU bugs) can force architectural decisions that differ from ideal semantic design. Practical systems must balance theoretical elegance with implementation robustness.
General Issues 9. FastMCP constructor rejected ‘version’ parameter on user’s conda environment Solution: Used inspect.signature() to check actual constructor, removed version parameter and passed only ’name’ and ‘instructions’, let mcp library handle versioning internally
Key Insight: SDK APIs evolve. When integration fails, inspect actual signatures rather than relying on documentation or assumptions. The mcp library’s FastMCP API changed between versions
10. CLAUDE.md documentation was outdated - missing author search feature, init –from-overview capability, and incorrect line count Solution: Systematically explored codebase using Grep to find undocumented functions (search_arxiv_author, create_project_from_overview), verified line count via wc -l, then made 6 targeted edits to update documentation sections (Usage, Code Organization, Key Implementation Details, File Layout)
Key Insight: Documentation drift is inevitable in actively developed tools; systematic code exploration (grep for patterns like –author, –from-overview) is more reliable than manual review
11. DNS resolution failure in Kubernetes pod preventing pip from accessing PyPI mirrors Solution: Diagnosed issue (nameserver 10.96.0.10, proxy localhost:9997), but didn’t resolve - likely cluster-level networking config issue
Key Insight: Kubernetes cluster DNS and egress proxy configurations can break standard pip workflows. May need cluster admin intervention or use of cached wheels.
12. conda run with –no-banner flag failed on user’s conda version with ‘unrecognized arguments’ error Solution: Removed –no-banner flag entirely. Conda run works without it, and banner output goes to stderr (doesn’t corrupt stdout). Tested with conda run -n calendarpro python -c ‘print(test)’ successfully
Key Insight: Optional flags that improve output cleanliness but aren’t functionally required should be omitted if they risk compatibility issues. Banner in stderr doesn’t affect stdout-based protocols
Human vs AI Approaches Strategic Level MCP tool design philosophy Role Approach Human Insight that summarize/research are ‘Claude Code enhancement skills’ - should return content, not just write files. Explicitly framed as architectural principle. AI Initially implemented tools as file-writing scripts with metadata returns, not recognizing the in-context usage pattern Difference Analysis: Human user understood the tool-assistant interaction model and desired workflow. AI implemented based on existing script patterns without considering integration context.
CalendarPro-gadget integration approach: MCP vs. subprocess vs. shared code Role Approach Human User explicitly chose subprocess + conda run despite AI’s MCP recommendation. User understood that gadget tools are monolithic CLI scripts (2800+ lines) and preferred zero gadget code modification with clear separation via subprocess boundaries AI AI initially recommended MCP server as primary approach, viewing it as ‘cleanest’ due to native tool discovery. Only after user clarification did AI fully commit to subprocess approach with detailed ToolRunner design Difference Analysis: Human prioritized maintainability and separation of concerns (zero cross-repo dependencies) over ‘cleanliness’ of protocol. AI initially favored protocol elegance. Human’s choice reflects pragmatic systems thinking about long-term evolution of independent codebases
GPU vs CPU training decision after CUDA errors Role Approach Human Immediately questioned ‘Why use CPU training? Can run on GPU!’ - prioritized using faster hardware and debugging GPU issues AI After encountering CUDA errors, conservatively switched to CPU training to avoid failures without deeply investigating GPU problem root cause Difference Analysis: Human strongly prefers optimal performance (GPU) and pushes to debug issues; AI tends toward error avoidance with safer fallback (CPU). Human intuition that GPU should work outweighs initial failure signals.
CrossModalEnhancer output dimension design Role Approach Human Original plan specified output dimension matching original modality (1536d for image) to preserve semantic space, prioritizing theoretical soundness AI After encountering cuBLAS bug on large matrix projection, pragmatically revised to output in hidden_dim space (128d) to avoid hardware constraints Difference Analysis: Human maintains ideal architectural design from theoretical perspective. AI adapts architecture based on discovered implementation constraints, prioritizing ‘working code’ over ’elegant design’ when they conflict.
Data file relocation strategy Role Approach Human Directly suggested ‘你直接把…剪切过来就好了’ (just move it over) - identified that correct data already exists, use filesystem move AI Planned to delete old data and regenerate from scratch - more conservative but wasteful when correct data already available Difference Analysis: Human quickly recognized existing data validity and chose direct filesystem operation; AI defaulted to regeneration for correctness guarantee. Human efficiency vs AI caution.
Implementation Level scGPT benchmark research methodology Role Approach Human Posed broad question: ‘search for existing papers’ views on scGPT and clustering benchmarking’, expecting comprehensive synthesis AI Decomposed into multiple targeted keyword searches (zero-shot, ARI/NMI metrics, specific comparisons), executed parallel WebSearch and WebFetch, then synthesized findings with structured summary Difference Analysis: Human frames questions at conceptual level (‘what do people think?’). AI translates to executable search strategies with specific metrics, datasets, and comparison baselines, then reconstructs the conceptual answer.
RM-IDEAL visualization requirement Role Approach Human Immediately asked ‘where is your visualization?’ when noticing benchmark script only produced numerical CSV outputs, expecting visual outputs as standard deliverable AI Initially ran benchmark and reported numerical results (Spearman ρ, Precision@K) without generating visualizations, prioritizing quantitative metrics over visual outputs Difference Analysis: Human emphasizes visual validation and presentation-ready outputs as integral to analysis completion. AI defaults to numerical correctness and assumes visualization is a separate optional step.
AI Limitations Critical Limitations Insufficient CUDA debugging experience led to 4 architecture rewrites (gather neighbors→project, project→gather, output in hidden_dim, mini-batch loss) before identifying cuBLAS large-matrix bug as root cause. Failed to recognize known GPU driver compatibility issues upfront. Originally designed MCP tools as file-writing scripts returning metadata, not recognizing the pattern that tools should return content for Claude Code to consume directly When encountering CUDA errors, conservatively switched to CPU training without sufficient investigation of GPU compatibility issues. Multiple failed attempts before recognizing PyTorch CUDA build vs driver version mismatch. Initially didn’t recognize that torch.use_deterministic_algorithms(True) conflicts with PyG scatter operations on CUDA, attempting multiple workarounds before identifying the core issue Failed to anticipate stdout/stderr isolation requirement for MCP server until seeing the architecture context. Only recognized the issue when designing the wrapper layer, not when initially exploring gadget CLI tools General Limitations When user proposed large refactoring task (‘organize all docs, merge duplicate functions, archive unused code’), launched 3 parallel Explore agents but session was interrupted before agents returned, demonstrating poor handling of long-running multi-agent workflows across session boundaries. WebFetch encountered 403 errors on multiple key papers (bioRxiv scGPT-spatial preprint, ResearchGate links) without attempting fallback strategies like archive.org snapshots or requesting user to manually download PDFs, limiting depth of literature analysis. Did not proactively generate visualizations when running RM-IDEAL benchmark, requiring user to explicitly ask ‘where is the visualization?’. Failed to anticipate that spatial analysis naturally demands visual validation. When implementing bioRxiv/PubMed support, did not proactively question whether user wanted to modify 2600-line monolithic file vs. refactor into modular package. Directly implemented as requested without offering architectural alternatives DNS troubleshooting session ended without resolution - didn’t fully explore cluster networking configs or suggest alternative wheel distribution methods Learnings Key Learnings RM-IDEAL uses WWL (Wasserstein Weisfeiler-Lehman) graph kernel to compute spatial niche matching: iterative neighbor label hashing → node feature distributions → optimal transport (Wasserstein distance) between subgraphs. Provides topology-based ground truth for evaluating embedding quality. Cross-attention on single tokens degenerates to linear projection. Spatial transcriptomics provides natural solution: k-NN neighbors form K/V sequences that encode neighborhood context. This pattern (spatial neighbors as attention context) is broadly applicable to spatial omics architectures. PyTorch 2.9 + CUDA 12.9 on RTX 2080 Ti has cuBLAS bug causing illegal memory access on large matrix operations (gemm failures at specific shapes like 128→1536, backward on N×N where N>3500). Workaround: mini-batch processing to keep matrices small. PyTorch CUDA build version (e.g., cu129) must match GPU driver CUDA version. scGPT foundation model underperforms traditional methods (PCA, scVI, Harmony) in zero-shot clustering benchmarks (Genome Biology 2025). AvgBIO scores on Pancreas/PBMC datasets show scGPT ~0.52-0.60 vs. HVG baseline ~0.60-0.77. scGPT-spatial requires fine-tuning and only compares to weak baselines (SpaGCN, stLearn), not SOTA (GraphST, STAGATE). Independent benchmarks more reliable than original paper claims. Conditional Flow Matching for robot action generation: learns vector field u_t from noise to actions using linear interpolation path x_t = t·noise + (1-t)·actions, Beta(1.5,1) time sampling for stability near noise endpoint, Euler integration with 10 steps for inference. Key insight: time conditioning via sinusoidal embeddings + MLP (or adaRMS for Pi0.5). MCP tool design principle: tools for Claude Code should return content by default (markdown reports, JSON data), with optional file persistence. This enables in-context use without filesystem round-trips. Summarize/research are ’enhancement skills’, not just file generators. When wrapping existing CLI tools for protocol-based systems (MCP, JSON-RPC), stdout/stderr isolation is a first-class architectural concern, not an afterthought. Use context managers for clean, reversible redirection without modifying original code When generalizing single-source systems to multi-source, introduce new abstraction (paper_id) while keeping old field (arxiv_id) as alias.
