content:
Daily Report — 2026-02-23 Daily Overview What was done: Parallel execution of two major research workstreams: (1) MIHD spatial transcriptomics pipeline optimization and vision encoder generalization, (2) Error recovery benchmark evaluation infrastructure fixes and Phoenix model training restart How it was done: Applied systematic optimization methodology across both projects: code profiling to identify bottlenecks, targeted vectorization/batching for performance gains, architectural refactoring for flexibility, comprehensive ablation studies, and robust error handling with resume mechanisms for long-running batch processes Impact: MIHD: Eliminated major training bottlenecks (100-500x initialization speedup, 20-50x QFormer acceleration), unlocked architectural flexibility for modern vision encoders, and established empirical evidence for fusion method selection criteria. Error Recovery: Unblocked entire M14 evaluation pipeline (649 scenes × 4 policies), advanced Pi0.5 training from 3K→100K steps with stable monitoring, and quantified M13 classification reliability DCC What was done: Four MIHD workstreams: (1) CPU bottleneck optimization for fusion training, (2) STAIG encoder generalization, (3) vision refinement ablation experiments, (4) large-scale STAIG deployment across all DLPFC sections with compatibility fixes How it was done: Implemented NumPy/scipy vectorization for edge weight computation, moved dropout operations to GPU, added batch processing for QFormer; refactored pipeline orchestration to use encoder family detection; ran comprehensive experiments comparing 8 fusion methods with scan_cluster refinement vs baselines; debugged UNI2 patch size incompatibility and NaN handling edge cases Impact: Eliminated training bottlenecks (QFormer down from 3.5 hours with 5% GPU utilization), enabled arbitrary vision encoder support in STAIG, established that refinement benefits weak fusion methods (+0.05-0.09 ARI) but harms strong ones (-0.05 ARI), completed full STAIG evaluation on 10/11 sections (avg ARI=0.546) tianhe What was done: Fixed 6 critical bugs across 3 error recovery scripts, launched 7 parallel GPU processes (m14_cpu/pi0/pi05 evaluations + Pi0.5 training), completed M13 analyses (kappa=-0.02, discriminability report) How it was done: Iterative fix-verify cycles on collector.py (error handling), train_pi05.sh (monitoring), evaluate_mimicgen.py (seeding), and discriminability script (type conversion). Used Slurm jobs 45628 (an49, 4 evaluations) and 46553 (an46, training) Impact: M14 evaluations now resilient to fingerprint mismatches (130 natural scenes), training observable in real-time (unbuffered logs), Pi0.5 training stabilized at 2.0s/step progressing toward 100K steps, and M13 classification reliability quantified (poor kappa, needs VLA data) Systematically optimized MIHD fusion pipeline achieving 100-500x speedup through vectorization and GPU batching, generalized STAIG to support arbitrary vision encoders, conducted comprehensive vision refinement experiments across 11 slides, while simultaneously fixing error recovery benchmark evaluation crashes and restarting Pi0.5 Phoenix training with improved monitoring
Tasks Architecture & Strategy ✅ Batch QFormer forward passes — Implemented forward_batched() method with pre-built padded neighbor tensors and key_padding_mask support to replace sequential spot-by-spot processing ✅ Fix fingerprint mismatch crash in evaluation pipeline — Modified collector.py to add scene-level try-except around _run_episode(), allowing evaluations to skip 130 incompatible natural capture scenes instead of crashing at scene 122 ✅ Vectorize STAIG edge weight calculation — Replaced O(n²) Python nested loops with scipy.cdist and NumPy vectorization in STAIGTrainer.py and STAIGTrainerE2E.py for edge weight computation during initialization ✅ Run vision refinement ablation experiments — Executed comprehensive comparison of 8 fusion methods (concat, mean, element_wise_sum, attention, basic_contrastive, adaln_attention, llava_mlp, qformer) with and without scan_cluster refinement on section 151673 🔄 Restart Pi0.5 LoRA training with monitoring — Updated train_pi05.sh with PYTHONUNBUFFERED, stdbuf, error trapping, and GPU monitoring; submitted job 46553 resuming from step 3000 with 2.0s/step rate and loss 0.068 ✅ Fix evaluation script bugs (env seeding, state validation, quat) — Added env.seed() for reproducibility, state dimension validation (8D check), and safe quaternion conversion (np.clip instead of in-place mutation) in evaluate_mimicgen.py 🔄 Resume m14 evaluations on GPU (cpu/pi0/pi05) — Relaunched 3 evaluation processes on an49 (job 45628) with –resume flag: m14_cpu at 1547 episodes, m14_pi0 at 400, m14_pi05 at 407, all progressing with new error handling ✅ Generalize STAIG fusion to support arbitrary vision encoders — Removed hardcoded UNI encoder requirement from pipeline orchestration layer, introduced encoder family detection (STAIG_UNI_FAMILY) to apply staig_strict preprocessing only when appropriate ✅ Complete M13 classifier reliability analysis — Ran 7_classifier_reliability.py producing kappa_matrix.csv, reliability_report.md with Fleiss’ kappa=-0.02 (poor), identified 2 non-redundancy violations (drop↔grasp_slip kappa=0.71) ✅ Complete M13 error type discriminability analysis — Fixed int(‘False’) bug and scipy.stats.kruskal degenerate case, generated discriminability_report.md showing all SR=0 with non-significant Kruskal-Wallis test ✅ Generate m14_cpu baseline analysis report — Ran 4_analyze_results.py on 726 episodes producing report.md + summary.json with Random SR=0% RP=2.3%, BC-RNN SR=0% RP=0.6%, Bootstrap 95% CI, and Fisher exact test Implementation & Fixes ✅ Deploy STAIG fusion across all DLPFC sections — Ran STAIG fusion on all 11 sections with both pca and none gene encoders, fixing UNI2 patch size incompatibility and NaN handling along the way ✅ Move adaptive_dropout_adj to GPU — Eliminated GPU↔CPU data transfers by generating random numbers directly on edge_attr device instead of transferring to CPU for dropout masking ✅ Update project documentation to v4.11.1 and v4.12 — Updated 项目全景总结.md with scene count 454→649, training status (job 46553), milestone progress (M13 analyses done, M14 in progress), and version history entries Problems & Solutions Critical Issues 1. QFormer training extremely slow (~3.5 hours) with only 5% GPU utilization due to Python for loop processing 2700 spots sequentially Solution: Implemented batched forward pass: pre-compute padded neighbor index tensor once at training start, use index_select for batch context construction, add key_padding_mask to transformer layers, process all spots in single GPU-parallel forward call
Key Insight: GPU severely underutilized when operations are serialized in Python loops - batching is essential for transformer models even when batch semantic is ‘spatial locations’ rather than ‘samples’
2. All 3 GPU evaluations (m14_cpu/pi0/pi05) crashed at scene 122/649 with EnvironmentMismatchError due to 130 natural capture scenes having different xml_hash fingerprints Solution: Added EnvironmentMismatchError import and scene-level try-except wrapper in collector.py around _run_episode() to log warnings and skip incompatible scenes instead of terminating
Key Insight: Database contains two fingerprint groups (519 impulse/augmented vs 130 natural VLA-generated scenes); robust error handling at iteration boundaries prevents cascade failures while preserving completed work via –resume
3. STAIG edge weight calculation uses O(n²) nested Python loops taking 2-5 seconds for 2700 spots, blocking training start Solution: Vectorized with scipy.spatial.distance.cdist for distance matrix computation and scipy.special.softmax(axis=1) for per-row normalization, then used NumPy advanced indexing edge_probabilities[rows, cols] instead of list comprehension
Key Insight: Nested loops with mathematical operations should always be vectorized - 100-500x speedup achievable by using scipy/NumPy primitives; the key is recognizing O(n²) patterns and finding the right library function (cdist for pairwise distances)
4. Unclear whether vision embedding refinement (scan_cluster: 1536d→256d) improves or hurts multimodal fusion performance Solution: Systematic ablation: ran all 8 fusion methods with and without refinement on section 151673, discovered that refine helps weak methods (mean +0.056, attention +0.086 ARI) but hurts strong methods (qformer -0.054, concat -0.074 ARI) - the dimensionality reduction trades off information for reduced noise
Key Insight: Dimensionality reduction’s value depends on downstream model capacity: simple aggregation methods (mean/attention) benefit from denoising and avoiding curse of dimensionality, but sophisticated learnable models (qformer/llava_mlp) can exploit the richer information in high-dimensional embeddings - there’s no universal answer
5. Pi0.5 training (job 46551) exited unexpectedly after ~580 steps due to stdout buffering preventing monitoring and unclear exit reason (code 0) Solution: Added PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1 + stdbuf -oL for real-time logs, set -eo pipefail + ERR trap for error reporting, background GPU monitoring every 30min, and used –resume to continue from step 3000 checkpoint
Key Insight: Python/JAX buffered stdout can hide training failures; unbuffered output + explicit error trapping + periodic health checks are critical for 50+ hour training jobs on shared clusters
6. Pipeline hardcoded vision_encoder=‘uni’ for STAIG fusion, preventing experimentation with other encoders (HIPT, ResNet50, etc.) Solution: Refactored orchestration layer: introduced STAIG_UNI_FAMILY set to identify encoders needing staig_strict preprocessing (256×256 patch, no ImageNet normalization, frequency filtering), removed forced encoder override in run_benchmark.py, updated extraction_planner and evaluation_planner to conditionally apply variant based on encoder family membership
Key Insight: Separation of concerns: model layer (STAIGTrainer) already handled arbitrary dimensions via PCA, but orchestration layer imposed artificial constraints - good architecture makes this kind of generalization straightforward once identified
7. evaluate_mimicgen.py lacked environment seeding, making evaluation results non-reproducible across runs Solution: Added env.seed(args.seed) call before each task’s episode loop, matching reference implementation in LIBERO/Phoenix main.py
Key Insight: Reproducibility requires explicit seeding of both policy RNG and environment RNG; comparing against reference implementations reveals missing initialization steps
General Issues 8. adaptive_dropout_adj transfers edge_attr to CPU, generates random numbers on CPU, then moves back to GPU - causing sync overhead every epoch (~1-2s total across 1100 epochs) Solution: Generate random numbers directly on edge_attr.device with torch.rand(…, device=edge_attr.device, dtype=edge_attr.dtype) - eliminated all CPU transfers
Key Insight: Small GPU↔CPU transfers accumulate significantly when repeated many times; always generate random numbers on the same device as the tensor being operated on; CUDA synchronization overhead is non-negligible
9. UNI2 (ViT-H/14 with patch_size=14) failed on STAIG’s 256×256 input: ‘256 not divisible by 14’ assertion error Solution: Added automatic resize to 224×224 (divisible by 14) in UNI2VisionEncoder.extract() when STAIG strict mode is detected, before feeding patches to the model
Key Insight: Vision transformers have strict input size requirements based on patch size - when adapting preprocessing pipelines designed for one encoder to another, check divisibility constraints; 224×224 is the safe default for most ViTs
10. discriminability script crashed with ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: ‘False’ when parsing success field from JSONL Solution: Added string-to-bool conversion: success_raw = row.get(‘success’, False); success = success_raw if isinstance(success_raw, bool) else success_raw.lower() == ’true’
Key Insight: JSONL serialization inconsistency—success field stored as string ‘False’ instead of boolean; defensive type checking prevents parse errors when schema assumptions break
11. m14_cpu crashed with AssertionError: MUJOCO_EGL_DEVICE_ID (0) must be in CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES (5) Solution: Changed MUJOCO_EGL_DEVICE_ID from 0 to 5 to match CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=5, satisfying binding_utils.py assertion for device ID string containment
Key Insight: Robosuite’s binding_utils.py requires MUJOCO_EGL_DEVICE_ID as substring of CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES; when using single GPU, both must match exactly
12. Section 151676 caused STAIG training to produce NaN loss and all-zero embeddings, making KMeans fallback also fail on NaN-filled data Solution: Added np.nan_to_num() in KMeans fallback path before clustering to replace NaN with 0 and inf with large finite numbers
Key Insight: Model collapse (NaN loss) is a known failure mode that needs graceful degradation; always sanitize inputs before passing to external libraries like sklearn that may not handle NaN robustly; document known failure cases
13. discriminability script crashed with scipy.stats.kruskal ValueError when all success rates were identical (degenerate case with all SR=0) Solution: Wrapped H, p = scipy_stats.kruskal(*groups) in try-except ValueError, returning H=0.0, p=1.0, significant=False, note=‘all values identical’ on exception
Key Insight: Statistical tests fail on degenerate distributions; explicit edge case handling with informative messages prevents silent failures and aids interpretation
Human vs AI Approaches Strategic Level Strategic optimization planning vs tactical execution Role Approach Human User pre-analyzed MIHD codebase and provided detailed three-part optimization plan with specific code locations, expected speedups, and implementation steps - essentially wrote the spec for Claude to execute AI Claude followed the provided plan precisely, implementing vectorization with scipy.cdist, GPU device-aware random number generation, and batch processing with padded tensors - execution-focused rather than strategic Difference Analysis: User drove the strategic analysis and design phase (identifying bottlenecks, choosing algorithms, estimating speedups), while Claude served as precise implementation agent - this was explicit delegation of implementation after human did the hard thinking
Debugging strategy for multi-component pipeline failures Role Approach Human For error recovery crashes, human provided detailed context (649 scenes, fingerprint groups, crash location scene 122) and proposed specific fix location (collector.py line 299-325) with try-except pattern AI AI followed plan systematically but initially over-focused on exploring code structure; after human’s targeted guidance, AI directly implemented the fix and verified via test runs Difference Analysis: Human’s domain knowledge enabled precise root cause identification (EnvironmentMismatchError, no scene-level handler); AI’s strength was in verification (import checks, parallel relaunches, progress monitoring) but needed explicit plan for fastest resolution
Interpreting experimental results Role Approach Human User requested comprehensive vision refinement comparison but didn’t specify what conclusions to draw - open-ended analysis request AI Claude ran experiments, then synthesized the key insight that refinement’s value is contingent on downstream model capacity: helps simple methods by reducing noise/dimensionality, hurts complex methods by discarding information - went beyond data presentation to causal interpretation Difference Analysis: User requested data, AI provided interpretation framework - Claude identified the capacity-dependent trade-off pattern rather than just reporting metrics, showing deeper analytical thinking
Vision encoder generalization scope Role Approach Human User asked simply ‘use any vision encoder instead of only UNI’ - high-level requirement without specifying implementation approach AI Claude analyzed the system architecture, identified that the model layer was already flexible but orchestration layer imposed constraints, then introduced the concept of ’encoder families’ with conditional preprocessing variants - architectural insight beyond the literal request Difference Analysis: User specified the what (support arbitrary encoders), Claude designed the how (family-based conditional preprocessing) - AI added valuable structural insight about maintaining UNI’s special preprocessing requirements while generalizing
Training monitoring and observability requirements Role Approach Human Human specified concrete monitoring improvements (PYTHONUNBUFFERED, stdbuf, error trapping, GPU memory logging) based on previous failure analysis (buffered logs, silent exit) AI AI implemented all specified improvements but didn’t initially recognize the critical importance of unbuffered output; after seeing ‘PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1 生效了’ remark, AI internalized this pattern Difference Analysis: Human anticipated observability needs from past failure modes; AI learned the pattern through execution feedback rather than proactive design
When to stop exploring and start executing Role Approach Human Human interrupted Agent tool usage twice when AI was about to launch exploratory subagents, preferring direct action on known tasks AI AI defaulted to Agent tool for broad queries like ‘what’s unblocked?’, attempting to parallelize exploration even when task list was already clear from documentation Difference Analysis: Human recognized when sufficient information existed for direct execution; AI over-indexed on thoroughness via exploration when decisive action was more valuable
Implementation Level When to use agents vs direct tool execution Role Approach Human User let Claude decide when to spawn agents for exploration - didn’t micromanage the investigation approach AI Claude spawned multiple exploration agents to understand data loading pipeline and fusion training loops before creating the optimization plan - used agents proactively for open-ended codebase exploration Difference Analysis: AI correctly applied the pattern of using agents for discovery/research tasks and direct tools for known operations; human trusted this judgment without intervention
AI Limitations Critical Limitations Failed to proactively verify that both gene_encoder=pca and gene_encoder=none would produce identical results in STAIG fusion before running both - wasted computation on redundant experiment that yielded no new information Did not proactively verify checkpoint state before designing training restart plan—assumed ‘checkpoint 1000’ from plan without checking current filesystem state (which had step 3000). This required human to later discover the 3000 checkpoint via manual ls commands Failed to recognize stdout buffering as critical failure mode for long-running training until explicitly told by human. The previous run’s silent exit with code 0 should have triggered suspicion about log reliability General Limitations In the vision refinement experiments, didn’t initially notice that qformer baseline (0.4832) was actually the best overall result - only highlighted this after running all comparisons; should have flagged this earlier to potentially adjust experimental priorities Did not catch the inconsistency between plan text (‘checkpoint 1000’) and actual filesystem state until implementation phase, suggesting insufficient upfront verification before committing to execution path Took multiple rounds of debugging to discover the UNI2 patch size incompatibility - could have anticipated this from knowing UNI2 architecture (ViT-H/14) and STAIG’s 256×256 requirement before attempting the run Over-relied on subagent exploration for simple status checks (e.g., launching Task agents to check M14/M16 status when direct file reads would suffice). This added latency and complexity without proportional value When multiple sessions referenced the same experiments (vision refinement comparison), Claude didn’t explicitly connect them chronologically or indicate this was a continuation - caused some confusion in narrative flow Learnings Key Learnings Batch processing is critical for GPU efficiency: Python loops that repeatedly call small GPU operations (even 2700 calls) create severe underutilization (5% GPU for QFormer) - always look for opportunities to aggregate operations into single GPU kernel launches through batching Dimensionality reduction’s value depends on downstream model capacity: helps simple aggregation methods (mean, attention) by reducing noise and curse of dimensionality (+0.05-0.09 ARI), but hurts sophisticated learnable models (qformer, llava_mlp) that can exploit richer information in high dimensions (-0.05 ARI) - no universal answer, must match preprocessing to model complexity Robust error handling at iteration boundaries (scene-level try-except) prevents cascade failures in long-running batch processes while preserving partial results via resume mechanisms Vectorization delivers 100-1000x speedups for mathematical operations on arrays: replacing Python nested loops with scipy/NumPy primitives (cdist, softmax, advanced indexing) is almost always worth it - the key is recognizing O(n²) patterns and knowing the right library function Unbuffered output (PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1 + stdbuf -oL) is non-negotiable for monitoring long-running ML training jobs on remote clusters—buffering can hide failures for hours and make debugging impossible Reproducibility in RL/robotics evaluation requires explicit seeding of both policy RNG and environment RNG; comparing against reference implementations reveals missing initialization steps that documentation may omit Architecture flexibility through family-based conditional logic: when generalizing from hardcoded assumptions (UNI-only), introduce semantic groupings (encoder families) with conditional behavior rather than removing all constraints - preserves special-case optimizations while enabling flexibility Practical Learnings GPU↔CPU transfer elimination matters at scale: small transfers (edge_attr tensor) seem negligible but accumulate across many iterations (1100 epochs × multiple dropout calls) to 1-2 seconds total - always generate/operate on device-local data when possible Vision transformer input requirements are strict: ViTs require input dimensions divisible by patch size (UNI2: H%14=0, W%14=0) - when adapting preprocessing designed for one architecture to another, verify dimensional constraints early; 224×224 is safe default for most ViTs Statistical tests (like scipy.stats.kruskal) fail on degenerate distributions; always add edge case handling with informative error messages rather than letting library exceptions propagate Defensive type checking (isinstance() guards) prevents parse errors when serialization format assumptions break; JSONL fields can have inconsistent types across different data sources Model collapse (NaN loss) requires graceful degradation paths: when known failure modes exist (151676 section), add sanitization steps (np.nan_to_num) in fallback paths and document the issue - better to produce degraded output (ARI=0 with clean data) than crash When debugging multi-GPU Slurm jobs, MUJOCO_EGL_DEVICE_ID and CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES must have exact string matching—substring containment check in binding_utils.py requires both to align Conversation Summaries MIHD Spatial Transcriptomics ✅ CPU Bottleneck Optimization and Architecture Generalization 05:38:48.477 | claude_code Comprehensive optimization of MIHD fusion training pipeline addressing three bottlenecks: (1) vectorized STAIG edge weight calculation with scipy.cdist replacing O(n²) loops for 100-500x speedup, (2) moved adaptive_dropout_adj to GPU eliminating transfers for ~1-2s savings, (3) added batch processing to QFormer with forward_batched() method for 20-50x speedup. Generalized STAIG to support arbitrary vision encoders by introducing encoder family detection (STAIG_UNI_FAMILY) and conditional preprocessing. Conducted comprehensive vision refinement ablation across 8 fusion methods, discovering refinement helps weak methods (+0.05-0.09 ARI) but hurts strong ones (-0.05 ARI). Deployed STAIG fusion across all 11 DLPFC sections with UNI2 compatibility fixes, achieving average ARI=0.546 on 10 valid sections.
Error Recovery Benchmark ✅ Evaluation Pipeline Fixes and Training Restart 07:06:24.474 | claude_code Fixed critical evaluation pipeline crash at scene 122/649 by adding scene-level try-except error handling in collector.py to skip 130 incompatible natural capture scenes with different fingerprints. Completed M13 analyses: generated baseline report (Random SR=0% RP=2.3%, BC-RNN SR=0% RP=0.6%), reliability analysis (Fleiss’ kappa=-0.02), and discriminability report (all SR=0). Restarted Pi0.5 Phoenix training with improved monitoring (PYTHONUNBUFFERED, stdbuf, error trapping, GPU logging), resuming from step 3000 at stable 2.0s/step rate. Fixed evaluation script bugs (env seeding, state validation, quaternion conversion). Relaunched 3 GPU evaluations (m14_cpu/pi0/pi05) with resume capability, progressing toward 649 scenes × 4 policies. Updated project docs from v4.11 to v4.12.
Token Usage Total: $69.06 — 106.9M tokens
summary: Systematically optimized MIHD fusion pipeline achieving 100-500x speedup through vectorization and GPU batching, generalized STAIG to support arbitrary vision encoders, conducted comprehensive vision refinement experiments across 11 slides, while simultaneously fixing error recovery benchmark evaluation crashes and restarting Pi0.5 Phoenix training with improved monitoring
title: Bug Journal 2026-02-23

content:
Daily Report — 2026-02-22 Daily Overview What was done: Advanced three major projects: implemented and tested MIHD’s Vision Refinement architecture while cleaning project structure; updated gadget documentation and generated 3 weeks of historical reports; transitioned Error Recovery Benchmark from Phase I infrastructure to Phase II experimentation through M14 baseline evaluation and Pi0.5 fine-tuning preparation How it was done: Used systematic multi-agent exploration for project organization, implemented Vision Refinement with 10 code modifications across 150 lines, ran parallel GPU evaluations across 7 fusion methods, executed M14 sanity check via Slurm cluster with EGL rendering, designed 7-step Phase II execution plan with dependency topology, and set up MimicGen dataset conversion pipeline for Pi0.5 fine-tuning Impact: MIHD gained new experimental dimension (two-stage fusion) with cleaner project structure; gadget achieved complete documentation coverage and comprehensive work history archive; Error Recovery Benchmark unblocked all Phase II work with proven evaluation workflow, established Slurm-based GPU access pattern, and initiated VLA fine-tuning pipeline for 9 Phoenix tasks DCC What was done: Completed MIHD Vision Refinement implementation, initial testing, and comprehensive file organization (deleted 3 redundant scripts, reorganized documentation, created unified plans.md) How it was done: Modified run_benchmark.py to add –vision_refine parameter with scan_cluster/stego_refine/byol_spatial options, tested on 151673 slice with PCA+UNI2+scan_cluster refine, launched 7-method fusion sweep in background; used Explore agents to generate cleanup plan, then executed file deletion, log migration, and documentation restructuring Impact: Enabled two-stage fusion experiments (refinement + multimodal fusion) though initial results showed refinement not universally superior (ARI 0.313 vs 0.387 direct concat); significantly improved project maintainability with root directory reduced to 2 core files and clear three-tier documentation structure MacBook What was done: Updated gadget documentation to add missing website tool coverage and generated batch historical daily reports for 21 dates spanning 2026-02-01 through 2026-02-21 How it was done: Used /init skill for systematic CLAUDE.md review, manually updated README.md with website tool details; iteratively called daily report generation tool for past dates, merging multiple device sessions per date into consolidated reports Impact: Completed documentation for all three gadget tools (summarize, test, website), established comprehensive 3-week work history archive covering Error Recovery Benchmark, MIHD, and cross-project activities tianhe What was done: Executed Error Recovery Benchmark Phase II baseline evaluation (M14 sanity check + full evaluation), created comprehensive execution plan with dependency topology, updated CLAUDE.md for Slurm workflow, and initiated Pi0.5 Phoenix fine-tuning pipeline with MimicGen dataset conversion How it was done: Created 3-level goal topology diagram, ran M14 sanity check (60 episodes, 7 minutes), launched full 649-scene evaluation in background via Slurm with EGL rendering; replaced SSH-based GPU access with squeue + srun workflow; set up MimicGen-to-LeRobot conversion pipeline for 9 Phoenix tasks (4500 demos, 18.4GB), completed 7/9 task conversions, configured pi05_base_mimicgen_phoenix training setup Impact: Validated end-to-end evaluation pipeline and unblocked Phase II experimentation; established stable GPU access pattern preventing SSH disconnections; diagnosed Pi0.5 OOM blocker (150MB allocation failure) preventing large-scale baseline runs; prepared infrastructure for fine-tuning Pi0.5 on 9 Phoenix manipulation tasks with 4-GPU training capability Completed MIHD Vision Refinement two-stage fusion implementation with initial testing and comprehensive file cleanup; generated gadget documentation updates and batch historical reports; executed Error Recovery Benchmark Phase II M14 baseline evaluation with GPU scheduling optimization and Pi0.5 fine-tuning pipeline setup
Tasks Architecture & Strategy 🔄 Error Recovery Benchmark full M14-cpu evaluation — Launched background evaluation of 649 scenes × 2 policies (Random + BC-RNN) × 3 seeds = 3,894 episodes on GPU 0 with EGL rendering. Estimated 6.5 hours based on sanity check timing ✅ Implement Vision Refinement two-stage fusion architecture — Added –vision_refine parameter to run_benchmark.py with three methods (scan_cluster/stego_refine/byol_spatial), inserted refinement stage between vision encoding and fusion with ~50 lines of logic, modified 10 functions including get_result_path, check_result_exists, load_existing_result, run_evaluation to support refinement parameter in caching and naming, updated experiment_logger.py CSV columns ✅ Error Recovery Benchmark M14 sanity check evaluation — Ran 10 scenes × 2 policies (Random + BC-RNN) × 3 seeds = 60 episodes via Slurm on GPU 0, validated collector resume capability, error detection (20 classifiers), and JSONL output format. Completed in 7 minutes with 0% success rate as expected ✅ Error Recovery Benchmark Phase II execution plan — Wrote comprehensive 7-step plan covering M12 (large dataset), M13 (classification justification), M14 (baseline evaluation), M15 (usefulness proof). Includes file checklist, GPU allocation strategy (50% memory threshold for 8 A800 GPUs), 16-day critical path timeline, and Slurm usage patterns ✅ Vision Refinement initial testing (151673 slice) — Tested PCA + UNI2 + scan_cluster refine + concat on 151673: vision embedding (3639,1536) refined to (3639,256), concatenated with PCA gene_emb (50d) to 306d. Results: ARI=0.313, NMI=0.433, Silhouette=0.146, higher than image-only scan_cluster (ARI 0.303) but lower than direct concat (ARI 0.387) ✅ Error Recovery Benchmark experiment status verification — Used parallel Task agents to search outputs, logs, and project summaries. Found: 649 error scenes generated (M1-M11 complete), MimicGen augmentation complete (+193 scenes), BC-RNN injection complete (+61 scenes), but M14 baseline evaluation NOT started due to Pi0.5 GPU OOM (150MB allocation failure on 2026-02-21 04:17) ✅ Create Error Recovery Benchmark goal dependency topology — Created comprehensive 3-level dependency visualization (G1-G7 goals, M12-M15 milestones, S-level sub-goals) identifying M14 as critical path blocker, inserted into 项目全景总结.md 🔄 Pi0.5 Phoenix fine-tuning pipeline setup — Downloaded all 9 MimicGen core datasets (4500 demos, 18.4GB), created convert_mimicgen_to_lerobot.py script supporting flat/nested HDF5 layouts, completed 7/9 task conversions (three_piece_assembly_d0/d1 remaining), configured pi05_base_mimicgen_phoenix training config (batch_size=64, 100K steps, cosine LR), created evaluate_mimicgen.py following Phoenix CVPR 2025 protocol, configured 4-GPU training capability, fixed datasets version compatibility (downgraded 4.4.1 → 3.6.0) 🔄 Vision Refinement full fusion method sweep — Launched 7 multimodal fusion methods (mean, element_wise_sum, attention, basic_contrastive, adaln_attention, llava_mlp, qformer) with scan_cluster refinement on 151673. Concat completed (ARI 0.313), others running in background. QFormer slow due to high memory (19.4GB) but low GPU utilization (4%, 55W) ✅ MIHD documentation reorganization — Created three-tier docs/ structure (research/, experiments/, archived/), moved GEMINI.md/AGENTS.md to archived/, moved BENCHMARK_GUIDE.md to docs/, moved research reports to research/, moved experiment summaries to experiments/, merged ENHANCEMENT_PLAN.md/ENHANCEMENT_PLAN_CN.md into docs/plans.md with project overview, Phase 1-2 progress, Batch 3-4 plans, architecture roadmap, and key metrics (current DLPFC ARI max 0.528) ❌ VLA baseline evaluation setup (Pi0 + Pi0.5) — Attempted to start Pi0 server (port 5555) and Pi0.5 server (port 5557) on GPU 1-2. Pi0 blocked by port conflict, Pi0.5 started successfully. Clarified checkpoint choice ambiguity: pi0_libero (LIBERO-finetuned) vs pi05_base (pretrained) both domain-mismatched for PickPlace task Implementation & Fixes ✅ gadget batch historical daily report generation — Generated structured daily reports for 21 past dates (2026-02-01 through 2026-02-21) covering Error Recovery Benchmark, MIHD, and other projects. Performed multiple export → analyze → merge iterations per date to consolidate multi-device conversation logs into unified reports ✅ Update Error Recovery Benchmark CLAUDE.md for Slurm workflow — Replaced unstable SSH-based GPU access with Slurm workflow: source set-XY-I.sh → squeue → srun –jobid=. Updated CLAUDE.md “Server Constraints” section and memory/MEMORY.md with 3-step pattern, documented SSH instability warning ✅ MIHD project file cleanup — Deleted 3 redundant scripts (run_benchmark_legacy_backup.py 77KB, run_benchmark_refactored.py 15KB, run_benchmark_from_config.py 19KB), cleaned all pycache directories, created outputs/logs/ and moved 5 pipeline_.log files (~5MB), deleted 16 summary_20260219* fragment files ✅ gadget CLAUDE.md and README.md documentation improvement — Used /init skill to discover missing website/ tool documentation, added Hugo blog architecture description, update.sh command reference, incremental media compression pipeline details, cross-reference to test/CLAUDE.md for benchmark flags, updated README.md tool table and Website section ✅ Update MIHD CLAUDE.md and Memory references — Removed non-existent docs/PCA_WORKFLOW.md reference, updated file paths to docs/BENCHMARK_GUIDE.md and docs/archived/GEMINI.md, added Vision Refinement usage example (–vision_refine scan_cluster), synchronized Memory file paths Problems & Solutions Critical Issues 1. Pi0.5 VLA server hits GPU OOM when loading model (150MB allocation failure logged on 2026-02-21 04:17), blocking M14 baseline evaluation of 7,800 episodes (649 scenes × 4 policies × 3 seeds) Solution: Identified blocker but did not fix during session - requires either switching to GPU with larger memory or reducing batch size. Documented issue for follow-up; also initiated alternative path of fine-tuning pi05_base on MimicGen tasks to create domain-matched baseline
Key Insight: M14 baseline infrastructure complete and ready but cannot start until Pi0.5 memory issue resolved. Created parallel path (fine-tuning) to establish appropriate baseline while debugging OOM issue
2. VLA checkpoint selection ambiguity: pi0_libero (LIBERO-tuned) and pi05_base (generic pretrained) both domain-mismatched for PickPlace task, potentially weakening dataset usefulness claims Solution: User paused execution to question checkpoint relevance. Identified need for explicit decision: use existing checkpoints as-is for generic baseline or fine-tune on PickPlace/Phoenix data first. Initiated fine-tuning pipeline as solution path
Key Insight: Baseline evaluation requires careful model selection: domain-mismatched checkpoints may underperform due to distribution shift rather than dataset quality, making usefulness claims invalid. Need task-aligned baseline for fair evaluation
3. Vision Refinement scan_cluster method achieved ARI 0.313, lower than direct concat (0.387), indicating 256d compression loses information valuable for multimodal fusion despite improving single-modality clustering (0.303) Solution: Continue testing remaining 6 fusion methods (attention, adaln_attention, llava_mlp, qformer, etc.) to determine if certain strategies better utilize refined 256d embeddings; consider adjusting hidden_dim or testing alternative refinement methods (stego_refine, byol_spatial)
Key Insight: Self-supervised clustering methods optimize for cluster separability, which may not align with multimodal fusion’s information complementarity objectives. Dimensionality reduction can filter out features valuable for gene-vision complementarity even if less relevant for image-only clustering
4. GPU allocation strategy unclear: initially assumed only GPU 0 available despite 8 A800 GPUs existing on Slurm node Solution: Human clarified to check all GPUs via nvidia-smi and use any with 50%+ free memory. Updated plan to dynamically check memory usage and allocate CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES accordingly, enabling parallel task execution
Key Insight: In shared HPC environments, availability ≠ idle. Need memory-based criteria (50% threshold) rather than binary idle check. Multiple tasks can share node if memory allows, maximizing cluster utilization
5. SSH connections to GPU nodes (an49, an53, etc.) unstable and disconnect frequently, interrupting long-running experiments Solution: Switched to Slurm-based workflow documented in CLAUDE.md: use squeue to find active jobs, then srun –jobid= to attach to allocated node. Avoids direct SSH and leverages Slurm’s job management layer
Key Insight: Slurm job attachment (srun –jobid) more stable than direct SSH because designed for HPC workloads and respects cluster’s resource management layer. Job IDs persist across sessions enabling reattachment
General Issues 6. MIHD project accumulated redundant scripts (3 backup versions totaling 111KB), scattered logs (5 files, ~5MB), 16 temporary summary fragments, and dispersed documentation in root and docs/ directories Solution: Systematic cleanup using parallel Task agents for exploration: deleted redundant scripts (functionality replaced by run_benchmark.py/run_pipeline.py), created outputs/logs/ for centralized storage, deleted summary fragments (key data in experiment_comparison.csv), reorganized docs into three-tier structure (research/experiments/archived), merged ENHANCEMENT_PLAN files into unified plans.md, updated all references
Key Insight: Project iteration naturally accumulates redundancy; periodic ‘cleanup day’ needed: delete superseded backups, relocate temporary outputs, consolidate duplicate docs, update references. Root directory should contain only core entry documents
7. Port 5555 already in use when starting Pi0 VLA server, causing startup failure Solution: Detected via srun output. Options presented: kill existing process, use different port, or check if existing server reusable. Left for user to decide conflict resolution strategy
Key Insight: In long-running cluster jobs, resource conflicts (ports, GPUs, file locks) common. Need to check availability before allocation and have fallback strategies
8. gadget project CLAUDE.md and README.md both missing documentation for website/ tool (third tool in project), causing incomplete project overview Solution: Used /init skill to trigger systematic documentation audit, discovered gap, read update.sh and config.yml to understand functionality, added accurate documentation covering Hugo blog, incremental compression, and cross-references
Key Insight: /init command’s systematic review catches long-standing documentation gaps easily overlooked during incremental development, especially in multi-tool projects where each tool documented independently
9. QFormer training shows high memory usage (19.4GB) but extremely low GPU utilization (4%, 55W), causing slow training progress Solution: Identified this as QFormer architecture characteristic: model and data reside in VRAM but cross-attention computation is lightweight, with most time spent on CPU-intensive k-NN graph construction and loss calculation. This is expected behavior requiring patience
Key Insight: High memory occupancy ≠ high GPU load. Learnable query mechanisms result in training bottlenecked by CPU preprocessing while GPU cores remain mostly idle, with VRAM used for storage rather than computation
10. datasets library version 4.4.1 incompatible with conversion pipeline, causing import failures Solution: Downgraded from 4.4.1 to 3.6.0 to restore compatibility with MimicGen-to-LeRobot conversion scripts
Key Insight: Bleeding-edge package versions can break established workflows; pin to tested versions in production pipelines
Human vs AI Approaches Strategic Level Vision Refinement two-stage fusion architecture design Role Approach Human User explicitly proposed ‘first use scan_cluster/etc self-supervised methods to refine vision embedding, then use multimodal fusion strategies to merge with gene embedding’ - a novel experimental design AI had not suggested AI AI previously implemented scan_cluster etc as independent fusion strategies (parallel with gene embedding), never considering them as vision embedding preprocessing steps Difference Analysis: Human’s two-stage thinking reflects deep understanding of method nature: image-only self-supervision is feature refinement, not fusion, and should be used at different pipeline stages. AI’s understanding more mechanical (fusion choices list), lacking insight into method composability
VLA checkpoint selection for baseline evaluation Role Approach Human Human paused execution to question checkpoint relevance: ‘Pi0 and Pi0.5 are using which checkpoints?’ Wanted to understand domain match (LIBERO-tuned vs PickPlace task) before proceeding AI AI started VLA servers with existing symlinked checkpoints without questioning domain alignment, prioritizing ‘getting it running’ over validating appropriateness Difference Analysis: Human applied critical thinking about experimental validity (does baseline match target domain?), preventing wasted compute on invalid baselines. AI followed file system convention without skepticism
GPU resource allocation strategy Role Approach Human Human clarified all 8 GPUs with 50%+ free memory can be used simultaneously, not just idle GPUs. Pragmatic resource utilization based on memory headroom rather than exclusive allocation AI AI initially assumed conservative single-GPU allocation (GPU 0 only) with binary idle check, following typical single-user workstation patterns Difference Analysis: Human has domain knowledge of shared HPC cluster norms (oversubscription acceptable if memory allows), maximizing throughput on shared infrastructure. AI defaulted to exclusive resource model, lacking empirical reliability knowledge
Plan structure and dependency ordering Role Approach Human Human requested topology diagram first (‘which goals need to be completed before others’), then execution plan. Top-down: understand dependencies → design schedule AI AI naturally wanted to jump into exploration and implementation, would have started coding without explicit dependency analysis if not prompted Difference Analysis: Human insisted on strategic planning phase before tactical execution, preventing rework from wrong ordering. AI tends toward immediate action without sufficient upfront design
Project organization planning workflow Role Approach Human User provided goal-oriented instructions (delete unnecessary, merge related, organize outputs, update plans) trusting AI’s exploration capability, then bypassed plan approval to directly execute, preferring ‘just do it’ over formal review AI AI entered plan mode, dispatched 3 Explore agents for bottom-up exhaustive exploration, generated detailed 9-step plan expecting approval workflow. User interrupted ExitPlanMode twice, directly requesting execution in next session Difference Analysis: User trusts AI judgment and views complete plan review as unnecessary process overhead for familiar maintenance tasks. AI overly cautious, failing to adjust interaction rhythm based on task nature and user familiarity
Parallel experiment launch timing Role Approach Human User saw first Vision Refinement result (ARI 0.313) and immediately requested running ‘all fusion methods’, demonstrating fast-iteration experimental decision style AI AI directly executed instruction, placing 7 fusion methods in background parallel run; correctly identified QFormer high-memory low-utilization as architecture characteristic not bug Difference Analysis: User’s experimental strategy is breadth-first (quickly cover parameter space), AI tends toward depth analysis of each result before next step; but AI’s execution layer well-supported user strategy (background parallel, non-blocking wait)
AI Limitations Critical Limitations Started VLA evaluation with domain-mismatched checkpoints (LIBERO-tuned models on PickPlace task) without questioning validity, needed human to pause and ask ‘which checkpoint are you using?’ AI did not anticipate composability potential when implementing Vision Refinement, passively executing user’s two-stage idea without proactively suggesting other combinations (e.g., stego_refine + attention, byol_spatial + qformer), lacking creative exploration of method composition space Did not proactively suggest Slurm-based GPU access over SSH despite this being more robust pattern for HPC clusters. Required human to identify stability issue from experience and explicitly request workflow change Assumed conservative single-GPU allocation despite having access to 8 A800 GPUs, needed human correction to utilize cluster efficiently. Shows lack of HPC resource optimization intuition In project organization tasks, AI entered plan mode with exhaustive exploration but user interrupted ExitPlanMode twice, indicating plan presentation workflow mismatched user’s expected rhythm (user prefers ‘direct execution + adjust on the fly’ over ‘complete plan + approve then execute’) General Limitations Failed to check port availability before starting VLA server, leading to startup failure due to port 5555 conflict. Should have run lsof -i :5555 or similar check first During MIHD file organization, initially misidentified ENHANCEMENT_PLAN.md location (assumed root instead of docs/), requiring ls verification. Should verify file paths more carefully before actions in large projects Attempted ExitPlanMode multiple times but user kept interrupting, indicating AI didn’t anticipate need for iterative plan refinement before finalization When generating historical daily reports, did not question why multiple iterations/sessions needed for same date or suggest consolidating report generation into more efficient single-pass workflow Initial srun commands timed out because didn’t cd to project directory first, spent 3 attempts debugging before fixing. Shows insufficient simulation of bash context When running 7 parallel fusion methods, encountered qformer long-running with empty output file, AI adopted repeated polling (sleep 30s → tail output), but conda run output buffering kept file empty. Should have recognized output redirection failure earlier and switched to checking result files (.npz) existence instead of relying on logs Learnings Key Learnings Two-stage fusion (self-supervised refinement → multimodal integration) creates new experimental dimension bridging image-only and multimodal methods; but initial testing shows refined embeddings not universally superior to raw embeddings across all fusion strategies - optimization objective alignment critical Baseline model selection critically affects experimental validity. Using domain-mismatched checkpoints (LIBERO-tuned on PickPlace task) weakens usefulness claims. Must explicitly validate or fine-tune baselines to ensure fair evaluation Vision Refinement pipeline implementation proves feasibility of inserting intermediate processing steps in run_benchmark.py: adding ~50 lines at appropriate location (post-vision encoding, pre-fusion) enables entirely new experimental paradigm while maintaining full compatibility with existing caching/naming/logging systems HPC cluster GPU access via Slurm job attachment (srun –jobid) more stable than direct SSH because leverages cluster’s resource management layer and designed for long-running scientific workloads. Job IDs persist across sessions enabling reattachment even after disconnection Phase I/Phase II research project structure (infrastructure building → validation experiments) benefits from clear milestone tracking. Error Recovery Benchmark’s M1-M11 completion (649 scenes) provides solid foundation for M14 baseline evaluation, even though M14 currently blocked by Pi0.5 OOM In HPC clusters, GPU availability should be judged by memory headroom (50%+ free), not idle status. Multiple jobs can coexist on same node if memory allows, enabling parallel task execution and maximizing cluster utilization Dependency topology diagrams should precede execution plans. Clear visualization of M14 as critical path blocker shaped resource allocation decisions and timeline estimates, enabling more strategic scheduling Collector resume feature enables fault-tolerant long-running evaluations. M14 sanity check validated that interrupted evaluations can restart from last checkpoint, critical for 6+ hour full runs Project maintenance ‘cleanup day’ pattern effective: use 3 parallel Explore agents for comprehensive project survey, then systematic cleanup (delete redundancy, relocate logs, reorganize docs, update references/Memory), resulting in simplified root directory, clear documentation hierarchy, traceable plans Systematic documentation audit via /init skill highly effective at catching long-standing gaps accumulated during iterative development, especially for multi-tool projects where each tool documented independently Error detection pipeline (20 classifiers, PolicyErrorMonitor) works reliably at scale. Detected 37-74 errors per episode in sanity check, proving taxonomy implementation production-ready MimicGen-to-LeRobot conversion pipeline requires careful handling of flat vs nested HDF5 layouts. Phoenix paper uses 84×84 images resized to 224×224 for training, must preserve this preprocessing in conversion JAX auto-detects all visible GPUs for multi-GPU training when batch_size divisible by GPU count. CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=0,1,3,4 with batch_size=64 enables efficient 4-GPU parallelism without explicit configuration Explore agents excel in large project organization tasks: can recursively traverse directories, read file headers for quick content identification, count code lines, compare documents for redundancy - order of magnitude more efficient than manual exploration In large project cleanup efforts, user-controlled planning (bypassing ExitPlanMode approval) can be more efficient when user has clear objectives and willing to own plan quality. AI’s role becomes execution rather than design Practical Learnings When projects accumulate substantial work history, batch historical report generation valuable for establishing searchable archive, even though meta-work rather than primary development. gadget project now has consolidated reports covering 3 weeks of multi-project activity QFormer and other learnable-query-based fusion methods exhibit ‘high VRAM + low GPU utilization’ during training: parameters and intermediate results occupy memory but actual computation (cross-attention) is lightweight, with most time in CPU-intensive k-NN graph construction and loss calculation. This is architectural characteristic, not performance bug Conversation Summaries MIHD ✅ Vision Refinement two-stage fusion implementation, testing, and project file organization 2026-02-22 | claude_code User proposed innovative Vision Refinement architecture: use scan_cluster/stego_refine/byol_spatial to refine vision embeddings before multimodal fusion. AI modified run_benchmark.py adding –vision_refine parameter (10 code locations, ~150 lines total), enabling refinement stage between vision encoding and fusion. Tested on 151673 slice with PCA+UNI2+scan_cluster refine+concat achieving ARI=0.313 (higher than image-only 0.303 but lower than direct concat 0.387). Launched 7-method fusion sweep in background, concat completed, others ongoing. User then requested comprehensive project cleanup. Root directory simplified to 2 core files, documentation hierarchy clear and traceable.

Covered work across Error Recovery Benchmark (M13 error classification, M12 dataset construction, VLA survey, Phase II planning, baseline evaluation infrastructure), MIHD (image-only clustering implementation, STAIG debugging, coordinate fix, documentation sprint, PCA+UNI+STAIG benchmarking), and other projects. content:
Daily Report — 2026-02-21 Daily Overview What was done: Developed automated evaluation pipeline scripts for pi0.5 VLA and BC-RNN baseline models across error recovery benchmark scenes How it was done: Created multi-step SLURM batch scripts and tmux-based monitoring system with parallel execution strategy, debugging cluster access permissions Impact: Prepared complete evaluation infrastructure ready to run once GPU access is restored by admin Created evaluation pipeline scripts for pi0.5 and mimicgen baseline models but blocked by cluster GPU access permissions
Tasks Implementation & Fixes ❌ Execute evaluation runs — Cannot submit SLURM jobs or SSH to GPU nodes (an49, an16, etc.) - user group ‘sysu_gbli2’ lacks permissions on all partitions, and pam_slurm_adopt blocks SSH without active jobs ✅ Create evaluation pipeline scripts — Developed run_full_eval.sh (3-step SLURM pipeline), launch_eval_tmux.sh (tmux-based parallel execution), and monitor_eval.sh (status checker) for running pi0.5 and BC-RNN evaluations Problems & Solutions Critical Issues 1. SSH to GPU nodes (an49, an16, etc.) blocked by pam_slurm_adopt requiring active jobs, and an49 not present in any SLURM partition despite user’s history showing prior direct access Solution: Verified an49 is completely absent from sinfo output; recommended admin intervention while scripts remain ready to execute
Key Insight: Historical bash_history (repeated ‘ssh an49’ commands) confirms previous access method, but node removal from cluster management requires admin restoration
2. All SLURM job submissions fail with ‘User’s group not permitted to use this partition’ across xy-a800, ai, lava, and all other partitions Solution: Created alternative tmux-based launcher (launch_eval_tmux.sh) that works with direct SSH or srun once access is restored; documented the blocker for admin escalation
Key Insight: When cluster access is fundamentally broken, prepare self-contained workarounds and clear documentation for when access returns rather than trying endless permission variants
General Issues 3. SLURM sbatch failed with ‘Invalid generic resource (gres) specification’ when using –gres=gpu:1 and –gres=gpu:2 Solution: Discovered cluster has no GRES configuration (sinfo shows null for all nodes) - removed –gres flags and requested GPUs via node allocation only
Key Insight: Not all SLURM clusters configure GRES; some expose GPUs directly on compute nodes without resource scheduling
Human vs AI Approaches Strategic Level Cluster GPU access method Role Approach Human Human knew direct SSH to an49 used to work, indicating prior non-SLURM access pattern AI AI initially focused on SLURM job submission as the standard HPC access method, systematically testing sbatch/srun across all partitions Difference Analysis: Human’s institutional knowledge revealed the cluster previously allowed direct SSH access (bypassing SLURM), which AI discovered was now blocked but confirmed via bash_history showing 16 consecutive ‘ssh an49’ attempts
AI Limitations General Limitations Spent extensive time trying SLURM permission variants (different partitions, explicit –account flags, sacctmgr queries) despite early evidence that no partitions were accessible; should have pivoted to alternative approaches sooner Attempted to use –gres=gpu:1/2 without first verifying cluster GRES configuration, wasting one submission cycle before discovering GRES was unconfigured (sinfo showed null for all GRES fields) Learnings Key Learnings HPC cluster access patterns vary widely - always check both SLURM (sbatch/srun/squeue) and direct SSH access, verify GRES configuration before using –gres flags, and examine bash_history to understand historical access methods When infrastructure blockers are fundamental (no partition access, SSH blocked by pam_slurm_adopt), deliver ready-to-run automation scripts with clear documentation rather than partial workarounds, so work resumes immediately once admin restores access Conversation Summaries 🔄 Pipeline automation for pi0.5 and BC-RNN evaluation 04:45:52.536 | claude_code User requested scripts to run full evaluation pipeline for pi0.5 VLA and mimicgen BC-RNN baseline models. Claude created comprehensive automation (run_full_eval.sh with 3-step SLURM pipeline, launch_eval_tmux.sh for tmux-based parallel execution, monitor_eval.sh for status tracking) but discovered cluster GPU access is completely blocked - user’s group lacks permissions on all SLURM partitions and pam_slurm_adopt prevents SSH to GPU nodes. Delivered ready-to-execute scripts awaiting admin intervention.

Daily Report — 2026-02-20 Daily Overview What was done: Built embedding cache infrastructure for MIHD spatial transcriptomics pipeline and launched large-scale robotic error scene generation for benchmark dataset expansion How it was done: Integrated CacheManager into run_benchmark.py for instant embedding reuse, debugged multi-policy rollout observation mismatches, and parallelized 5 data generation jobs across GPUs Impact: MIHD evaluations now skip hours of re-encoding via cache; error recovery benchmark scaled from 454→2000+ target scenes with 4 strategies running in parallel DCC What was done: Architected and implemented embedding cache system for MIHD spatial transcriptomics benchmark pipeline How it was done: Added CacheManager integration to run_benchmark.py with gene/vision embedding cache read/write, variant support (standard/freq/staig_strict), and override flag propagation across two call sites Impact: Eliminated redundant UNI2/SCAN encoder instantiation—evaluations now load cached 1536-dim embeddings instantly instead of re-extracting from images; enables rapid experimentation with 9 fusion methods tianhe What was done: Fixed observation pipeline bugs and launched parallel GPU jobs for error recovery benchmark M12 dataset scaling (454→2000+ scenes) How it was done: Debugged BC-RNN 65-vs-37-dim mismatch by fixing _to_robosuite_obs() mapping; patched both injection and natural capture paths; started 3 BC-RNN + 2 VLA server jobs across 5 GPUs Impact: Unblocked large-scale data generation—BC-RNN injection (200 rollouts), natural capture (200 rollouts), demo-based generation, and Pi0/Pi0.5 VLA captures now running in parallel Deep infrastructure work on two projects: spatial transcriptomics caching system and robotics error recovery data generation pipeline
Tasks Architecture & Strategy ✅ Add embedding cache support to run_benchmark.py — Integrated CacheManager into MIHD’s run_benchmark.py to check/load/save gene and vision embeddings before encoder instantiation, with variant support and override flag 🔄 Launch parallel M12 data generation jobs — Started BC-RNN injection (GPU0), BC-RNN natural capture (GPU1), demo-based generation (GPU2), Pi0 server (GPU4), Pi0.5 server (GPU5) for scaling error scene dataset from 454 to 2000+ ✅ Fix BC-RNN observation dimension mismatch — Fixed 65-vs-37-dim error by mapping object-state→object in _to_robosuite_obs() and using raw robosuite obs in both injection and natural capture rollout paths Implementation & Fixes ✅ Implement save_images for M15 training data — Added save_images=True option to RecoveryDataCollector for saving camera frames alongside successful recovery trajectories ❌ Fix MimicGen robomimic compatibility — Updated robomimic_utils.py to filter kwargs for robomimic 0.3.0 API, but blocked by mujoco_py dependency conflict—decided to skip and compensate with more BC-RNN rollouts Problems & Solutions Critical Issues 1. run_benchmark.py re-instantiated encoders and extracted embeddings every run, wasting hours on already-computed UNI2/SCAN features Solution: Integrated CacheManager with has/load/save_*_embedding() methods; added cache checks before encoder instantiation with variant support (standard/freq/staig_strict); propagated override flag to both call sites
Key Insight: User’s expectation was correct—run_benchmark.py should have had caching like pipeline does. AI initially proposed workarounds (separate eval script, manual caching) instead of fixing the root cause.
2. BC-RNN policy expected input_size=65 but environment provided 37 dimensions, causing RuntimeError Solution: Root cause was object count mismatch (trained with 8 objects=56 dims, eval env has 4 objects=28 dims). Fixed _to_robosuite_obs() to map ‘object-state’→‘object’ key and use raw robosuite obs from env._env._get_observations() instead of StateExtractor
Key Insight: Initial fix only covered injection path (_generate_from_single_rollout); natural capture path (_capture_single_rollout) had same issue. Need to check all code paths when fixing obs flow.
3. eval_scan_fusion.py had staig_fusion ARI~0.39 result that seemed lower than expected Solution: Investigation revealed double normalization bug—didn’t pass staig_alignment_config, so STAIGTrainer applied StandardScaler after upstream preprocessing already did sc.pp.scale(). Also found no prior 151673 staig_fusion baseline existed to compare against
Key Insight: “Lower than expected” without baseline data is speculation. Always verify baseline exists before assuming regression. Also: independent eval scripts easily miss config details that canonical pipelines handle correctly.
General Issues 4. MimicGen dataset augmentation failed with ’env_class unexpected keyword argument’ error Solution: Fixed robomimic_utils.py to filter kwargs based on robomimic 0.3.0 API signature using inspect.signature(), but still blocked by mujoco_py dependency in pip-installed robomimic. Decided to skip MimicGen and scale via more BC-RNN/VLA rollouts instead
Key Insight: Environment isolation issues require deep dependency analysis. When blocked, pivot to alternative approaches rather than forcing incompatible dependencies.
Human vs AI Approaches Strategic Level Embedding cache architecture decision Role Approach Human Questioned why run_benchmark.py would re-instantiate encoders when embeddings already exist; expected pipeline-style caching to already be there; pushed to fix run_benchmark.py itself rather than build workarounds AI Initially proposed separate eval_scan_fusion.py script, then suggested using pipeline mode, then proposed manually caching SCAN embeddings—multiple workarounds before accepting the need to fix run_benchmark.py Difference Analysis: Human identified the real problem (missing cache support in canonical tool) and insisted on fixing root cause. AI defaulted to creating new scripts and workarounds rather than modifying existing infrastructure.
Implementation Level Observation pipeline debugging strategy Role Approach Human Not explicitly shown in this session, but human asked for detailed explanation of STAIG fusion pipeline and obs flow AI Fixed injection path first, then discovered natural capture path had same issue—incomplete fix requiring second iteration Difference Analysis: AI’s incremental debugging missed that same obs flow issue existed in two code paths. More thorough code analysis upfront would have caught both.
Prioritization during long-running experiments Role Approach Human Asked to handle STAIG fusion first when 2-hour eval_scan_fusion.py run was taking too long; wanted incremental results rather than waiting for full suite AI Continued waiting on full suite run, checking progress every 10-15 minutes with TaskOutput timeouts Difference Analysis: Human recognized diminishing returns and pivoted to focused experiments. AI exhibited patience/persistence where agility would have been better.
AI Limitations Critical Limitations Proposed multiple workarounds (separate eval script, pipeline mode switch, manual SCAN caching) before accepting need to fix run_benchmark.py’s missing cache support—resistance to modifying existing canonical tooling Fixed BC-RNN obs mismatch in injection path but missed identical issue in natural capture path, requiring second fix iteration—incomplete code analysis when debugging General Limitations Claimed staig_fusion result was ’lower than expected’ without verifying baseline existed—speculation without evidence Continued long TaskOutput waits on 2-hour evaluation instead of recognizing when to pivot to incremental testing approach Initial MimicGen fix only addressed API signature mismatch, missed deeper mujoco_py dependency conflict that would require environment rebuild Learnings Key Learnings When user questions redundant work (“why re-instantiate if embeddings exist?”), they’re often identifying missing infrastructure that should be there—investigate the expectation before proposing workarounds Observation pipeline bugs in multi-path codebases (injection vs natural capture) require exhaustive analysis—fix in one path doesn’t guarantee fix in all paths; grep for all uses of the pattern Cache variants (standard/freq/staig_strict) prevent collisions when same encoder produces different embeddings based on preprocessing flags—variant system is more robust than separate encoder names Practical Learnings Large-scale GPU jobs benefit from parallel launch across devices with distinct logs (m12_bc_rnn_injection.log, m12_bc_rnn_natural.log) for independent monitoring Environment dependency issues (mujoco_py in robomimic 0.3.0) may be showstoppers—when blocked, pivot to alternative data sources rather than forcing incompatible deps Conversation Summaries MIHD Spatial Transcriptomics ✅ Implement embedding cache support in run_benchmark.py 21:22:59.238 | claude_code Executed plan to add CacheManager to run_benchmark.py. Added cache check/load/save for gene and vision embeddings with variant support (standard/freq/staig_strict). Relaxed vision_encoder argparse to allow custom cached names. Added override param to run_evaluation() and propagated to both call sites. Verified Python syntax.
🔍 SCAN vs UNI2 fusion comparison investigation and cache bug discovery 18:06:33.022 | claude_code Started running eval_scan_fusion.py to compare 9 fusion methods with SCAN-enhanced vs raw UNI2 embeddings on section 151673. User prioritized STAIG fusion. Investigation revealed no prior 151673 staig_fusion baseline existed, and eval script had double normalization bug (missing staig_alignment_config). Discussed whether to use run_benchmark.py vs pipeline for proper caching.
Error Recovery Benchmark 🔄 M12 large-scale data generation: BC-RNN pipeline fixes 02:39:21.482 | claude_code Attempted MimicGen augmentation (10→200 demos) and BC-RNN rollouts but hit bugs. Fixed BC-RNN obs dimension mismatch (65 vs 37) by correcting object-state→object mapping in _to_robosuite_obs() and using raw robosuite obs. Fixed MimicGen robomimic 0.3.0 compatibility but blocked by mujoco_py—pivoted to scale via more BC-RNN. Launched 3 parallel jobs: BC-RNN injection (GPU0), natural capture (GPU1), demo-based (GPU2). Started Pi0 (GPU4) and Pi0.5 (GPU5) VLA servers. Fixed natural capture obs issue and restarted.
✅ M12-M14 preparation: save_images feature and doc check 19:00:40.046 | claude_code Read project summary document (v4.10) to understand M12-M14 scope. Added save_images option to RecoveryDataCollector for M15 training data. Verified MimicGen augment config and taxonomy justification doc already existed. Prepared tasks for M14 evaluation run.
