Issues with MuJoCo API compatibility for friction/pose_perturb/gripperbias injectors have been fixed (mj_name2id → body_name2id + _main suffix processing). 103 pose_perturb and 100 friction scenes were generated (database increased from 251 to 454). BC-RNN training lasted 600 cycles (15 minutes per 800 training sessions). Issues with VLA server openpi API calls and image key mapping have been resolved (Pi0/Pi0.5 servers are ready). M6 multi-strategy evaluation has been debugged—the random strategy works, but BC-RNN crashes due to obs format incompatibility (object and object state key names do not match + dimension 37 ≠ 65). ✅ BC-RNN integration: Training, obs key mapping fixes, and multi-strategy evaluation setup 2026-02-17 | claude_code A comprehensive BC-RNN integration workflow has been established. Checkpoints from MimicGen pre-training were searched, and no issues were found (only dataset issues). BC-RNN was trained from scratch using robomimic (2-layer LSTM, GMM head, trained on pick_place dataset for 600 cycles, checkpoint size 14.3MB). Obs key mapping errors were fixed: an alias object-state → object was implemented in policy_adapter.py, applicable to Strategy 1 (original obs) and Strategy 3 (StateExtractor fallback). Verified through 79 unit tests and GPU integration tests (5 iterations, eliminated KeyError, but SR rate was 0%). Visual videos demonstrating robot hovering behavior were generated, confirming that un-trained checkpoints were not framework-related. Project documentation updated to version v4.6. VLA servers were deployed (Pi0 port 5556, GPU 1; Pi0.5 port 5557, GPU 3). A script/5_baseline_accuracy.py was created for zero-error baseline testing. Multi-strategy evaluation started (50 scenarios × 3 seeds × 4 strategies).**
✅ Stable SSH Connection Solution for Remote Development via Jump Host 2026-02-17 | claude_code An unstable proxy-based SSH connection was identified, causing Claude Code sessions to be lost. A five-tier solution was designed: (1) SSH keep-alive configuration (ServerAliveInterval 15 seconds), (2) enhanced.tmux.conf file with history and stability settings, (3) ~/bin/claude-tmux startup script supporting automatic proxy connections, (4) commented-out global proxy settings added to.bashrc files for user isolation on shared accounts (manual enabling/disabling proxies), (5) documentation/local_ssh_setup_guide.md created, including ControlMaster client, automatic SSH scripts, and VS Code settings. All components were verified to work properly. A stable remote development workflow was established, avoiding session loss.**
🔄 Model Checkpoint Integration and Visualization Workflow Design 2026-02-17 | claude_code Six model checkpoints were listed: BC-RNN PickPlace (164MB, 12 cycles), Pi0 LIBERO (12GB), Pi0.5 Base (12GB), Pi0 Base (12GB), Pi0 Fast Base (11GB), Robomimic Lift (7.6MB). Total size exceeds 47GB. It was found that the existing script only handled replay of error scenarios and did not support visualization of real-time strategy demonstrations. A checkpoint/symbolic link structure was designed to avoid duplication. The visualize_policy_rollout.py script was created, combining the demonstration logic from 5_baseline_accuracy.py and the rendering function from 2_visualize_scene.py. Random/bc_rnn/vla strategies are supported, with configurable seeds and camera perspectives. Plans are in place, but implementation is still pending.**
Tools (Tool Overview) 🔄 Diagnosed and fixed ccusage opus-4-6 pricing errors, revealing a 13-fold underestimation of costs 2026-02-17 | claude_code Someone reported that ccusage returned a cost of 0, causing token cost tracking to fail. AI explored the sources of ccusage, LiteLLM pricing database, and actual log data. A mismatch in model names was identified: Claude Code used ‘claude-opus-4-6’, while LiteLLM database used ‘anthropic.claude-opus-4-6-v1’. A backup pricing mechanism was implemented in dailySummary.py: a _FALLBACK_PRICING dictionary was added (opus-4-6: input $5/minute, output $25/minute, cache creation $6.25/minute, cache retrieval $0.50/minute), along with a _fix_zero_cost_models() post-processing function. Initial implementation failed due to field name errors (used ‘model’, but ccusage output ‘modelName’). Fixed to mb.get(‘modelName’) or mb.get(‘model’). Logs from February 13th to 17th were re-exported (5 days). After fixing, actual costs were displayed as $18-48 per day, while the displayed costs were only $0-7 per day (13-fold underestimation, with an overall error of about $150+). Attempting to regenerate the report (merge and deploy) was unsuccessful due to LLM API connection failures. Data corrections are complete, but report generation still depends on API availability.**
Tools (Daily Report System) ✅ Integration of Daily Reports for Multiple Devices from February 13th to 16th 2026-02-17 | claude_code Users called the daily report analyzer during conversations from February 13th to 16th. This included projects on multiple devices: CalendarPro (JSON parsing, AI timeout handling, message queue stability, learning data collection, Random Thoughts feature on February 13th), MIHD benchmark optimization + error recovery framework (visual repairs, forced injection debugging, PreGrasp detector on February 14th), error recovery VLA work (Pi0/MimicGen checkpoint research, Gemini VLM integration, 50 iterations of natural error capture on February 16th, generating 150 scenarios). The system processed all conversations and generated structured JSON reports, including task, problem/solution, analysis of human and AI interactions, and conversation summaries for knowledge management and cross-device progress tracking.**
Motion-Based Self-Reflection Framework 🔄 Initiated a comprehensive dual-language document (CLAUDE.md + README/Tutorial) 2026-02-17 | claude_code The init command was executed, exploring a three-module architecture: MPM (motion prediction using LLaVA), MCM (motion correction), and diffusion strategy. The CLAUDE.md document was created, documenting training/inferencing commands, Hydra configurations, and dataset formats. Users requested a detailed bilingual README document (Chinese-English mix), covering all components, including 11 dependency modules (openpi, GraspVLA, MimicGen, LeRobot, robosuite, robomimic, etc.). Three parallel exploration agents were launched to analyze diffusion_policy/, llava/, failure_case_analysis/, and the entire dependency tree. A document plan of approximately 1200 lines was developed, covering architecture, data conversion pipelines (RLDS→LeRobot→robomimic), training workflows (three stages: LLaVA fine-tuning, MCM training, diffusion strategy), and all integration points across repositories. The CLAUDE.md document is completed, but a comprehensive README document has not yet been written.**
CalendarPro ✅ Fixed Google Calendar 403 errors and bulk deletion functionality 00:50:22.155 | claude_code calendar_service.py file was fixed, using settings().get() API instead of calendarList().get() to avoid 403 InsufficientPermissions errors. Bulk deletion functionality was implemented: the DeleteData model was extended, adding date_from/date_until/batch fields, updating LLM prompts to support custom time ranges, rewriting the handle_delete function for bulk operations, and adding a Discord confirmation interface for BatchDeleteApprovalView. Backoff mechanisms were added for LLM timeouts, including regular expression extraction and Discord crash handling for empty messages.**
Token Usage Total expenditure: $62.95 — 90.3 million tokens
summary: 我们已经修复了MIHD视觉编码器中存在的关键坐标交换问题，需要重新运行286次实验才能完全解决这一问题。同时，我们开发了多设备日常报告工具，以便处理来自四个项目在五个不同日期的对话日志。在M5/M6错误恢复基准测试中，我们成功构建了一个包含454个场景的数据库，并通过BC-RNN训练以及VLA双服务器集成实现了性能提升。此外，我们还解决了远程开发时SSH系统的稳定性问题，并修正了ccusage定价方面的错误，从而避免了13倍的成本低估现象（累计误差约为150美元以上）。
title: Bug Journal 2026-02-17

content:
Daily Report — 2026-02-16 Daily Overview What was done: Executed three major parallel workstreams: (1) diagnosed MIHD spatial embedding collapse and corrected error recovery documentation, (2) implemented and validated complete VLA integration infrastructure with natural error capture generating 150+ scenarios, (3) designed conversation summary feature for gadget summarization tool How it was done: Systematic engineering approach across devices: DCC performed root-cause analysis of 64-dim embedding bottleneck; tianhe server completed VLA policy integration (v4.4), error classification system (v4.3), API fixes, GPU-accelerated rollout validation, and natural error generation architecture (v4.5); Windows desktop designed JSON schema and prompt modifications for conversation summaries Impact: Transformed error recovery benchmark from synthetic injection to natural policy failure capture (271 scenarios, 8 error types, M5/M9/M10 milestones achieved); identified critical MIHD dimension bottleneck causing 91% cluster collapse; established foundation for enhanced daily report readability with per-conversation narratives DCC What was done: Diagnosed STAIG fusion model collapse on DLPFC section 151672, traced root cause to 64-dim embedding bottleneck combined with spatial majority voting How it was done: Embedding inspection (std=0.12), KMeans re-clustering verification, spatial refinement analysis showing minority clusters eliminated via radius-15 majority voting, cross-method dimension comparison (concat:1586, attention:256, STAIG:64) Impact: Revealed 94-96% information loss from dimension compression; explained why 5-cluster mclust produces only 2 effective clusters; proposed dimension ablation study (64/128/256/512) to address collapse systematically TzJsDesktop What was done: Designed conversation summary feature for gadget/summarize tool, planning JSON structure, prompt modifications, and Markdown rendering logic How it was done: Code review and planning agent design approach: defined per-conversation summary structure (project/source/timestamp/topic/summary/outcome), single-file modification strategy with 4 targeted edits (SUMMARY_PROMPT, Anthropic tool mode, generate_markdown, max_tokens) Impact: Completed backward-compatible architecture design enabling narrative summaries for each AI conversation, enhancing daily report readability and completeness tianhe What was done: Implemented VLA integration infrastructure (v4.4), policy error classification system (v4.3), fixed critical API issues, validated with comprehensive testing and 150+ natural error scenarios, designed natural error generation system architecture (v4.5) How it was done: Multi-phase development: corrected 15+ documentation errors via registry-based verification; implemented 30+ files (~3000 LOC) for VLA/classification; fixed openpi API calls, observation preprocessing, camera key mapping; executed GPU-accelerated rollouts (8xA800 cluster); explored VLA checkpoints and designed 7-step implementation plan for v4.5 Impact: Established operational VLA pipeline with Pi0 model (despite 100% PickPlace task failure revealing generalization limits); generated 271 total scenarios (121→271) across 8 error types; achieved M5/M9/M10 milestones; identified joint_limit_approach (47%) and grasp_wrong_pose (30%) as dominant natural failure modes; completed architecture design for v4.5 natural error generation using pretrained encoder + rule-based offline analysis Completed comprehensive VLA integration pipeline for error recovery benchmark (v4.3-v4.5), diagnosed and analyzed MIHD clustering collapse, and designed conversation summary feature for gadget daily reports
Tasks Architecture & Strategy ✅ VLA policy rollout validation and natural error capture — Fixed critical image passing bug in _get_current_obs(); ran 10 injection-mode + 50 natural_capture rollouts with Pi0 model on GPU; generated 153 new scenarios (3 injection + 150 natural_capture); updated milestone status M5/M9/M10. Total scenarios: 121→271, error types: 4→8. ✅ Diagnose STAIG fusion clustering collapse on DLPFC 151672 — Investigated why uni+staig_fusion produced only 2 clusters instead of 5. Found embedding dimension bottleneck (64-dim, std=0.12) combined with spatial refinement eliminates minority clusters via majority voting. ✅ VLA Model Integration Infrastructure (v4.4) — Implemented complete VLA integration: vla_server.py (TCP server), PolicyServerAdapter (client), camera obs support, PolicyErrorDetector, capture_natural_errors() method. Fixed API calls to use create_trained_policy(), corrected image key mapping, resolved observation preprocessing format issues. All 79 unit tests passing. ✅ Policy Error Detection & Classification System (v4.3) — Implemented 19 classifiers across 3 families (manipulation/planning/progress), error taxonomy (25 types), PolicyErrorMonitor coordinator, VLM analyzer (Gemini + Claude Code CLI), error analysis module, collector integration. All 73 unit tests passing. ✅ VLA Natural Error Generation System Architecture Design (v4.5) — Explored v4.4 infrastructure, confirmed VLA checkpoints (Pi0/Pi0.5/Phoenix), designed 7-step implementation plan including BC-RNN checkpoint downloads, offline vision error detector using pretrained encoder (DINOv2/SigLIP) + rule-based analysis. ✅ Conversation summary feature design for gadget/summarize — Designed JSON structure (per-conversation granularity with project/source/timestamp/topic/summary/outcome fields), prompt modifications, Anthropic tool mode updates, Markdown rendering logic (project-grouped with outcome emoji icons). Single-file backward-compatible modification strategy. 🔄 Design dimension ablation experiment for STAIG — Planned configurable hidden_dim support (64/128/256/512) for staig_fusion. User rejected plan execution without explanation. ✅ Fix error recovery benchmark documentation errors — Corrected factual errors in CLAUDE.md, 项目全景总结.md, and error_taxonomy.py: detector count (6→5), classifier count (19→20), error types (25→24), categories (10→8), test count (73→79), scene count (30→118). Added missing modules and dev commands. Implementation & Fixes 🔄 Download VLA checkpoints and BC-RNN models — Pi0/Pi0.5/Phoenix models confirmed downloaded (250GB HuggingFace cache in zhaoganlong directory). BC-RNN checkpoints partially missing (lift succeeded, can/square/transport 404 errors from Stanford URLs). MimicGen datasets download status unknown. Problems & Solutions Critical Issues 1. STAIG fusion produces only 2 clusters instead of 5, with ARI=0.1149 indicating severe collapse Solution: Root cause identified: 64-dim embedding bottleneck compresses 1536-dim UNI features by 96% (std=0.12), then spatial refinement’s radius-15 majority voting eliminates minority clusters. Proposed dimension ablation study to test 128/256/512-dim alternatives matching other fusion methods.
Key Insight: Cascading failures compound: GCN training produces near-collapsed embeddings, then spatial smoothing eliminates remaining small clusters entirely. The 64-dim bottleneck is critical—other fusion methods using 256-512 dims don’t exhibit this pathology.
2. VLA server initial obs missing camera images, causing ‘observation/image’ KeyError Solution: Fixed rollout_generator.py _get_current_obs() to add include_images parameter; detect PolicyServerAdapter type in _generate_from_single_rollout() and capture_natural_errors() to pass include_images=True with camera_names=[‘agentview’,‘robot0_eye_in_hand’]
Key Insight: VLA policies require visual input, but state_extractor.extract() doesn’t include images by default; need explicit policy type detection and include_images flag. This is a generic issue affecting all camera-dependent policies (VLA, VisuoMotor BC).
3. VLA server used wrong openpi API - manually loaded norm_stats causing model initialization failures Solution: Replaced manual loading with openpi’s create_trained_policy(train_config=get_config(config_name), checkpoint_dir=…) which handles norm_stats, transforms, and model architecture automatically. Added –config_name CLI arg and IMAGE_KEY_MAP for correct camera key mapping.
Key Insight: The openpi library provides high-level create_trained_policy() function that encapsulates all model loading complexity. Using low-level APIs manually is error-prone and breaks when the library evolves.
4. Pi0 (LIBERO checkpoint) achieved 100% failure rate on PickPlace task during natural_capture rollouts Solution: Identified failure mode distribution (joint_limit_approach 47%, grasp_wrong_pose 30%, misalignment_pre_grasp 17%, overshoot 5%, premature_release 1%); recorded as natural error data; determined need for PickPlace-specific checkpoint or domain adaptation for production use.
Key Insight: Pretrained VLA generalization is limited; cross-task distribution (LIBERO→robosuite PickPlace) causes significant performance degradation. However, these failure modes are valuable natural error data, aligning with benchmark’s natural error capture goals.
5. VLA integration appeared complete (code + tests passing) but discovered non-operational when attempting actual rollout Solution: Deep exploration revealed blockers: wrong openpi API usage, missing norm_stats.json handling, incomplete checkpoint paths, unsupported –policy vla_server flag. Created detailed fix plan and systematically implemented all corrections.
Key Insight: ‘Tests passing’ ≠ ‘system operational’—integration testing with real dependencies (model checkpoints, external APIs) essential before claiming completion. Synthetic unit tests validate code correctness but not end-to-end functionality.
6. _preprocess_obs() failed when StateExtractor returned state_info dict (different format from raw robosuite obs) Solution: Refactored _preprocess_obs() to detect input format and normalize to common structure {“state”: […], “images”: {…}}. Added robust key lookup with fallback patterns and proper float-to-uint8 image conversion.
Key Insight: Observation dict format changes depending on source: StateExtractor.extract() returns state_info with EEF-centric keys, while env.step() returns raw robosuite obs with robot0_* prefixes. Need unified preprocessing.
7. Documentation contained 15+ factual errors contradicting actual codebase state across multiple files Solution: Performed systematic registry-based verification (DETECTOR_REGISTRY, CLASSIFIER_REGISTRY, ErrorType enum), corrected all instances via targeted edits across 3 files, ran test suite to verify no breakage (79/79 passing).
Key Insight: Even code’s own docstrings were wrong. Truth source is the registered code, not docs. Verification workflow: grep all old values after edits to ensure complete cleanup.
Human vs AI Approaches Strategic Level VLA integration operational readiness assessment Role Approach Human User asked ‘can we rollout pi0 now?’—implicitly checking for end-to-end readiness beyond just code completion AI AI initially considered VLA integration ‘complete’ once code written and tests passing—assumed operational without verifying actual model loading and inference flow Difference Analysis: Human correctly intuited that ‘implementation complete’ ≠ ‘system operational’. AI over-optimistically equated passing unit tests with deployment readiness, missing need for integration validation with real dependencies.
Identifying the root cause of clustering collapse Role Approach Human User questioned whether 64-dim embedding was too small after seeing visualization, then directed AI to investigate the specific section AI AI performed systematic investigation: loaded embedding file, computed statistics (std=0.12), re-ran KMeans to verify collapse, traced through refinement logic, compared dimensions across all fusion methods Difference Analysis: Human had intuition about dimension size from domain knowledge; AI followed mechanical investigation workflow. Human’s hypothesis was correct and directed investigation efficiently.
Bug location strategy Role Approach Human Human didn’t directly point out bug, but let AI run end-to-end test to discover problem through runtime errors AI AI discovered bug through actual execution failure (VLA server error: ‘observation/image’), backtraced to policy_adapter.py _preprocess_obs(), then traced to rollout_generator.py _get_current_obs() to identify root cause Difference Analysis: Human adopted ’learning by doing’ strategy letting AI discover bugs; AI performed systematic debugging via error stack traces and code analysis; this approach led to deeper understanding rather than superficial fixes.
VLA model selection and implementation priorities Role Approach Human Human explicitly stated via Q&A: skip Flare model (not found), download all 4 BC-RNN checkpoints (can/square/transport), adopt pretrained encoder + rule-based offline analysis, choose offline over real-time detection AI AI posed 4 open-ended questions for user decision: Flare source, BC-RNN checkpoint scope, vision detector method (three options), real-time vs offline, providing detailed trade-off analysis for each option Difference Analysis: Human made pragmatic technical choices based on project constraints (model availability, implementation complexity, time budget); AI provided comprehensive option analysis framework but needed human domain knowledge and priority judgment for final decisions.
VLM backend type clarification Role Approach Human Human clarified wanting Claude Code CLI (local interactive tool) rather than Claude API (cloud API calls), emphasizing these are different usage modes AI AI initially understood as Claude API, only after AskUserQuestion realized Claude Code CLI is invoked via ‘claude -p’ command as local tool Difference Analysis: Human distinguished Claude Code CLI (interactive+scripting local tool) from Claude API (cloud HTTP API); AI initially conflated these concepts, requiring explicit correction.
Conversation summary granularity design Role Approach Human Human’s original requirement was ‘I need a part in the final file to summarize my conversations with AI’, without specifying concrete structure AI AI through planning agent systematically analyzed three granularity options (project aggregation, timeline aggregation, per-conversation independent), ultimately recommending per-conversation summary approach for complete context preservation, flexible rendering, and backward compatibility Difference Analysis: Human provided high-level requirement; AI transformed it into concrete architecture design decisions, considering readability, maintainability, and backward compatibility engineering constraints.
AI Limitations Critical Limitations Over-optimistic definition of ‘completion’—equated passing unit tests with operational readiness, missing that VLA integration requires actual model checkpoint validation and API integration testing beyond synthetic mocks. Initially missed that openpi config uses repack_transform to rename keys, leading to incorrect assumptions about input key format. Only caught this after checking actual LiberoInputs source code and make_libero_example(). Didn’t anticipate include_images requirement when writing VLA integration code; needed runtime error to discover the issue; lacked prior knowledge check of VLA policy input format requirements. Generated dimension ablation plan but user rejected without executing. AI doesn’t know why plan was rejected or how user wants to proceed differently. General Limitations Struggled to find correct BC-RNN checkpoint URLs despite multiple web searches and Stanford server probing. Official robomimic documentation references ‘model zoo’ but doesn’t provide direct checkpoint URLs. When multiple tool uses were rejected by user during checkpoint exploration, AI didn’t proactively ask for clarification about user’s true intent, instead continuing with similar tool calls. Confused Claude Code CLI with Claude API, lacking accurate understanding of own tool ecosystem; required explicit user correction. Lacked proactive workflow pattern recognition—needed explicit user instruction to encode ‘update 项目全景总结.md when planning’ and ‘store checkpoints in specific location’ rules. Learnings Key Learnings Embedding dimension bottlenecks cause catastrophic model collapse: STAIG’s 64-dim output compresses 1536-dim vision features by 96%, producing embeddings with std=0.12 that are nearly indistinguishable. Spatial refinement then eliminates minority clusters via majority voting. Other fusion methods (attention/qformer: 256-512 dim) don’t exhibit this pathology, suggesting 64-dim is fundamentally insufficient. VLA end-to-end integration critical requirements: ensure policy observation input format is correct, especially camera image passing; need explicit include_images=True in state_extractor.extract() and camera_names specification for all camera-dependent policies (VLA, VisuoMotor BC). When integrating third-party ML libraries (openpi, robomimic), always check actual API signatures via inspect.signature() and read example code in source repo. Documentation is often incomplete or outdated. The openpi library’s create_trained_policy() provides high-level encapsulation; using low-level APIs manually is error-prone. Pi0 (LIBERO checkpoint) demonstrates poor generalization on robosuite PickPlace task (100% failure rate); dominant failure modes: joint_limit_approach (47%) and grasp_wrong_pose (30%). This reveals VLA cross-task generalization remains challenging, but these failures provide valuable natural error data for benchmark purposes. Natural error capture (natural_capture mode) is more efficient than injection mode: 50 rollouts generated 150 scenes (~3 per rollout), while injection mode 10 rollouts generated only 3 scenes (~0.3 per rollout); natural failure modes are more diverse and realistic. Plugin-based architecture pattern in benchmark systems: detectors, classifiers, injectors all use base ABC + registry pattern for extensibility. Error classification system demonstrates this with 19 classifiers registered via CLASSIFIER_REGISTRY, enabling easy addition of new error types without modifying core logic. Documentation verification requires checking actual code registries and enums, not just reading docstrings or prior docs. DETECTOR_REGISTRY, CLASSIFIER_REGISTRY, and ErrorType enum are ground truth. Systematic grep after all edits prevents residual errors. Cross-conda-env communication architecture: VLA server uses TCP+pickle protocol to decouple model inference (openpi/openpi05 env) from benchmark framework (mimicgen_env). Length-prefix framing prevents message fragmentation, action chunking reduces inference calls. Environment isolation is critical for VLA/robotics projects due to conflicting dependencies. Observation preprocessing must handle multiple input formats gracefully: StateExtractor (framework-internal), raw env.step() (robosuite), and model-specific (VLA servers). Robust key lookup with fallbacks prevents silent failures. The observation dict format changes depending on source—need unified preprocessing layer. Spatial majority voting refinement (radius=15 nearest neighbors) can amplify rather than fix clustering errors when underlying embeddings are already collapsed. Refinement should be conditional on embedding quality metrics (e.g., disable if silhouette score < threshold). Dual VLM backend design (Gemini API + Claude Code CLI) provides flexibility: API suits batch automation, CLI suits interactive deep analysis and debugging; complementary coverage of different use cases. VLA integration for natural error generation requires architectural shifts: (1) policy server pattern for cross-conda-env execution, (2) camera observation support in environment wrapper, (3) observation preprocessing (quaternion→axis-angle, BGR→RGB, action chunking), (4) dual rollout modes (synthetic injection + natural capture). Fundamentally different from demo-replay synthetic perturbation approach. Systematic codebase exploration strategy (infrastructure check → model file location → config parameter confirmation → user decision confirmation) effectively supports complex multi-step project planning, avoiding missing critical dependencies. Backward compatibility is key consideration in data structure design: new conversation_summaries field through optionality and graceful degradation (missing field → no section rendering) ensures old reports continue working. This pattern applies broadly to evolving data formats. Error classification system’s 24 error types only triggered 5 types in actual VLA rollouts (joint_limit_approach, grasp_wrong_pose, misalignment_pre_grasp, overshoot, premature_release), indicating some error types require specific scenarios to manifest. Practical Learnings GPU batch processing for visualization (5 GPUs, 20 videos, zero failures) demonstrates value of parallel infrastructure. The subprocess.Popen + GPU assignment pattern scales well for embarrassingly parallel tasks. Falsy value handling subtlety in Python: 0 or default evaluates to default because 0 is falsy, causing bugs in 0-indexed counters. PrematureReleaseClassifier had step - (self._grasp_start_step or step) always returning 0 when grasp started at step 0. Solution: explicit is None checks for optional 0-indexed values. Conversation Summaries MIHD 🔄 STAIG fusion clustering collapse diagnosis and dimension analysis 22:26:09.691 | claude_code User found section 151672 visualization showed only 2 clusters instead of 5. Investigation revealed 64-dim STAIG embedding bottleneck (std=0.12, 96% compression from 1536-dim input) caused near-total collapse, with spatial refinement eliminating remaining minority clusters. Cross-method comparison showed other approaches use 256-512 dims without collapse. User decided to pursue dimension ablation experiments but rejected the implementation plan without explanation.
Error Recovery Benchmark ✅ VLA end-to-end test + critical bug fix + large-scale natural error capture 16:23:03.741 | claude_code User requested VLA end-to-end test (10 injection rollouts). Started Pi0 server, resolved environment issues (–no-banner, port occupancy). First run failed with ‘observation/image’ KeyError; traced to rollout_generator.py _get_current_obs() missing image passing, fixed by adding include_images parameter. Successfully generated 3 injection scenarios. Then ran 50 natural_capture rollouts generating 150 scenarios, revealing Pi0 100% failure on PickPlace task with dominant error types: joint_limit_approach (47%), grasp_wrong_pose (30%). Total scenarios: 121→271, M5/M9/M10 milestones achieved. Updated project documentation and milestone status.
✅ Make VLA policy rollouts operational with auto error injection 06:51:17.532 | claude_code User provided detailed plan to fix VLA integration issues (wrong openpi API, missing argparse, conda mismatch). Implemented all 4 tasks: rewrote VLA server to use create_trained_policy(), added VLA support to entry script with mode selection, fixed observation preprocessing to handle multiple formats, updated config/Makefile/docs. All 79 tests passed. Ran batch visualization (20 videos across 5 GPUs, zero failures). User requested end-to-end VLA test but interrupted.
✅ Policy Error Detection & Classification System (v4.3) complete implementation 00:37:58.421 | claude_code Implemented comprehensive error classification system: error taxonomy (3 Family → 10 Category → 25 Type), 19 classifiers across manipulation/planning/progress, PolicyErrorMonitor coordinator, VLM analyzer, error analysis module. Integrated into collector.py with minimal changes (3-line step loop addition). Created 32 unit tests covering all classifiers, fixed 3 test failures (severity logic, drop threshold, hold_duration calculation). Final result: 73/73 tests passing (32 new + 41 existing), production-ready v4.3 system.
🔄 VLA checkpoint confirmation and v4.5 natural error generation system design 04:59:17.779 | claude_code User asked if VLA checkpoints were downloaded. Systematic filesystem search confirmed Pi0/Pi0.5/Phoenix models in zhaoganlong directory (250GB HuggingFace cache total), but BC-RNN can/square/transport checkpoints missing. Explored v4.4 existing infrastructure (vla_server.py, PolicyServerAdapter, PolicyErrorDetector, capture_natural_errors). Through user Q&A, confirmed technical choices: skip Flare, download 4 BC-RNN checkpoints, adopt pretrained encoder + rule-based offline vision detection. Completed 7-step implementation plan design, but user rejected ExitPlanMode request.
✅ Documentation fact-checking and correction across 3 files 04:35:19.032 | claude_code User executed /init to improve CLAUDE.md. Exploration revealed 15+ factual errors: detectors (6→5), classifiers (19→20), error types (25→24), categories (10→8), tests (73→79), scenes (30→118). Generated Chinese-language plan covering docs and error_taxonomy.py docstrings. Implemented all corrections via systematic edits, verified with grep and test suite (79/79 passing). Added missing modules and commands.
🔄 VLA model integration architecture exploration and checkpoint download 04:45:08.678 | claude_code User clarified goal: inject errors into VLA models (Pi0, Pi0.5, Phoenix, Flare) during rollouts for natural error generation, plus vision+state detector. Explored server filesystem and found Pi0 (~12GB), Phoenix checkpoints. Identified integration requirements: policy server for cross-env execution, camera observations, observation preprocessing. Launched parallel downloads (Pi0, BC-RNN, MimicGen datasets), but BC-RNN URLs partially failed (lift OK, can/square/transport 404).
✅ Fill gaps in policy error classification system implementation 02:19:08.022 | claude_code User requested implementation of comprehensive policy error detection plan (6 phases). Discovered 95% already implemented from prior session, identified 4 specific gaps (GraspWrongPoseClassifier, subpackage exports, ErrorMetricsComputer, tests), and filled them systematically. All 79 tests passed. User then asked about checkpoint downloads; all three (Pi0, MimicGen, Robomimic) were missing at that time.
🔄 VLA Model Integration + State-based Error Detector (v4.4) implementation 05:30:32.536 | claude_code Implemented complete VLA integration infrastructure: vla_server.py (TCP server for Pi0/Pi0.5/Phoenix), PolicyServerAdapter (client), camera observation support, PolicyErrorDetector, capture_natural_errors() method. Updated configs and documentation. All 79 unit tests passing. However, user question ‘can we rollout pi0 now’ triggered deep exploration revealing implementation non-operational—required API fixes, checkpoint path corrections, entry script updates before actual use.
✅ Implement VLM error detector with Gemini + Claude Code CLI backends 07:14:56.791 | claude_code User requested VLM error detector implementation. Found Gemini VLM implementation in zhaoganlong directory (api_client.py), explored existing vlm_analyzer.py. User clarified wanting Gemini + Claude Code CLI (not Claude API). Completed 5 tasks: added Gemini backend to vlm_analyzer.py, updated benchmark_v4.yaml config, created extract_error_frames.py and classify_error_vlm.py scripts, wrote §12 VLM tutorial in 项目全景总结.md. Created complete Chinese tutorial.md with 9 sections covering environment setup, usage methods, examples, and FAQ.
gadget/summarize ✅ Conversation summary feature architecture design for daily reports 22:51:46.447 | claude_code User requested adding conversation summary section to daily_summary.py generated reports. Session resumed from truncated context. Read existing codebase, then used planning agent to design implementation. Solution: add conversation_summaries field to JSON (per-conversation granularity with project/source/timestamp/topic/summary/outcome), modify SUMMARY_PROMPT, update Anthropic tool mode, enhance generate_markdown function, adjust max_tokens. Plan written to graceful-beaming-leaf.md and successfully exited plan mode.
Token Usage Total: $72.41 — 104.8M tokens
summary: Completed comprehensive VLA integration pipeline for error recovery benchmark (v4.3-v4.5), diagnosed and analyzed MIHD clustering collapse, and designed conversation summary feature for gadget daily reports
title: Bug Journal 2026-02-16

content:
Daily Report — 2026-02-15 Daily Overview What was done: Parallel development across four major areas: (1) Hugo website publishing system deployment for daily reports; (2) MIHD Q-Former and scGPT benchmark monitoring on DCC cluster GPU nodes; (3) CalendarPro codebase refactoring and periodic self-check implementation; (4) gadget/summarize tool robustness improvements with atomic writes, structured LLM output, and multi-device rclone sync; (5) Error Recovery Benchmark expansion from 30 to 118 scenes with policy-based generation framework How it was done: Plan-driven development with systematic implementation: read existing code, write detailed plans with line numbers, execute modifications, run comprehensive tests (26+18+41 unit tests passing), deploy via automated scripts (update.sh for Hugo, rclone for multi-device sync), monitor long-running GPU experiments with checkpoint recovery Impact: Established complete workflow from AI conversation summarization to public website; ensured expensive GPU experiments continued without data loss after process crashes; reduced code duplication in CalendarPro (8→1 provider selections, 3→1 time parsers); eliminated JSON corruption risks in gadget tool; simplified multi-device workflow from manual file copying to single –sync command; expanded benchmark scene library by 293% with policy-agnostic injection framework DCC What was done: Monitored MIHD benchmark experiments: pca_uni2_qformer (recovered from crash at 6/11, resumed to 8/11 by end of day), scgpt evaluation (65/77 successful, 12 failures: 11 CUDA OOM due to GPU contention, 1 NaN from data issue in section 151676) How it was done: Checked experiment progress via log files and npz file counts; detected Q-Former process crash, verified GPU idle (1 MiB usage), restarted pipeline which auto-detected completed sections and resumed from section 151672; analyzed failure causes (GPU memory conflict between parallel Q-Former runs, section-specific data quality issue) Impact: Validated checkpoint recovery mechanism (断点续跑) in production - avoided re-running 6 completed sections (~9 hours GPU time); identified Q-Former monopolizes ~22GB GPU preventing parallel execution; total progress 240/286 experiments (~84%) with ~13.5 hours remaining for 10 Q-Former sections MacBook What was done: Published three historical daily reports (2026-02-12: MIHD enhancement design, 2026-02-13: 7-phase implementation, 2026-02-14: two-stage pipeline architecture) to Hugo bugJournal section and deployed to GitHub Pages How it was done: Read report markdown files from gadget/summarize/reports/, extracted summary from blockquote lines (> …), generated Hugo YAML front matter (title/date with -05:00 timezone/keywords/summary/draft fields), wrote to website/content/bugJournal/, executed update.sh script (Hugo build + public/ push to GitHub Pages) Impact: Hugo built 139 pages successfully; three new bugJournal entries now live at https://tzj2006.github.io/bugJournal/; established standard workflow for publishing AI conversation summaries; confirmed website directory deployment model (public/ subdirectory as GitHub Pages repo, source files in separate repo) TzJsDesktop What was done: Dual-track work: (1) CalendarPro codebase refactoring (deleted redundant files, merged tests, extracted shared utilities for time parsing and AI provider selection, unified keyword/meal time definitions) + periodic self-check with recurring tasks; (2) gadget/summarize tool enhancements (atomic writes, LLM structured output, multi-device rclone sync, smart skip logic, ccusage fix, device tagging, test/ submodule to regular files) How it was done: CalendarPro: created new utility modules (time_utils.py, provider_selector.py), updated imports across 8+ files, ran tests to verify (26/26 passing); implemented RecurringTaskStore + PeriodicChecker for background scheduling. Gadget: modified daily_summary.py to use atomic writes at 5 locations, configured Anthropic tool_use schema and OpenAI json_object format, added rclone subdirectory parameters, implemented smart-skip logic (read _merged_devices list, check current device, skip API if already merged), wrapped ccusage list output, git operations for submodule removal, updated README/CLAUDE.md/tutorial.md Impact: CalendarPro: 26/26 tests passed; reduced code duplication (8 AI provider selection instances → 1, 3 time parsing functions → 1); enabled automatic meal/sleep scheduling and user-defined recurring tasks (e.g., daily LeetCode) with 15-minute background check cycle. Gadget: eliminated JSON corruption risk (atomic writes ensure either old complete file or new complete file, never half-written); reduced API costs (smart-skip prevents redundant summarization); simplified multi-device workflow from manual file copying to single merge --sync command; reduced LLM JSON parsing failures via SDK-level structured output; simplified repo structure (no submodules) tianhe What was done: Error Recovery Benchmark major feature development: (1) batch visualization (60 videos with 8-GPU parallelization); (2) fixed visualization bug (neutral action during force injection); (3) scene generation expansion (30→118 scenes with mixed error types); (4) BC policy loading and trajectory saving; (5) policy-based generation framework design (25 error types taxonomy, rule classifier + VLM dual-layer architecture, PolicyAdapter abstraction) How it was done: Created batch_visualize.py with GPU parallelization (fixed EGL device ID relative indexing), generated 30 baseline + 30 randomized videos; modified Phase 3 to use neutral actions during injection+settling window (added –settle_steps parameter), verified with 3 test cases showing 90mm displacement; modified ProximityDetector and CollisionDetector to generate mixed specs (impulse+friction+pose_perturb), configured max_scenes_per_demo from 3→10; implemented _run_episode returning (summary, trajectory) tuple, _save_episode writing compressed NPZ (actions/rewards/eef_pos/gripper/objects), 3_collect_data.py loading BC checkpoint from config; designed 543-line Chinese plan with 3 Family → 10 Category → 25 Type error taxonomy, rule-based classifier (state thresholds), VLM analysis layer (video + natural language explanation), PolicyAdapter pattern with robomimic integration Impact: Visualization pipeline verified (60 videos covering 30 scenes × 3 demos × force levels); visualization bug root cause identified and fixed (OSC controller compensation eliminated by neutral actions during disturbance); scene library expanded by 293% (30→118) with diverse error types; BC policy integration completed for evaluation pipeline; policy-based generation framework designed for future VLA/Pi0 integration, validating that existing detection-injection-validation pipeline is already policy-agnostic at action level Multi-project development day: published historical daily reports to Hugo website, monitored long-running MIHD benchmark experiments on DCC cluster, completed CalendarPro codebase refactoring with recurring tasks, enhanced gadget/summarize tool with atomic writes and multi-device sync, advanced Error Recovery Benchmark with 118 scenes and policy-based generation framework
Tasks Architecture & Strategy ✅ Fix visualization video neutral action problem — Identified root cause (OSC controller compensating injected force when tracking demo trajectory), modified Phase 3 to use neutral actions during injection+settling window, added –settle_steps parameter, verified with 3 test cases showing 90mm displacement ✅ Implement policy-based error scene generation framework — Created policy_adapter.py (230 lines), extended rollout_generator.py with generate_from_policy(), replaced BCPolicy stub with robomimic integration, updated database dedup logic, added CLI script and Makefile targets; all 41 unit tests pass 🔄 Monitor MIHD Q-Former benchmark progress — Monitored pca_uni2_qformer experiments (6/11 completed when process died at ~15:18, restarted at 15:55, progressed to 8/11 by 18:00), each section takes ~90 minutes with 50 epochs, estimated 3 hours remaining for last 3 sections ✅ Recover from Q-Former process crash — Detected Q-Former pipeline crash (process stopped, 6/11 sections completed), restarted pipeline which automatically detected completed sections and resumed from 151672 (remaining 5 sections), verified GPU was idle (1 MiB usage) before restart ✅ Implement JSON atomic write to prevent file corruption — Added _atomic_write() helper function using tempfile + os.replace pattern, replaced 5 file write points (export log, save_report Markdown/JSON, Hugo post, config) to ensure write interruption doesn’t cause half-written JSON ✅ Implement multi-device rclone sync workflow — Modified _rclone_upload to add subdirectory parameter (export → logs/, merge → reports/), added _rclone_download_logs() function to download logs from remote, merge subcommand supports –sync flag for one-command download+merge ✅ Expand error scene generation (30→118 scenes) — Modified ProximityDetector and CollisionDetector to generate mixed specs (impulse+friction+pose_perturb instead of impulse-only), configured max_scenes_per_demo from 3→10, covered 10 demos, generated 100 new scenes 🔄 Design policy-driven error injection framework (25 error types taxonomy) — Designed 25 error type classification system (3 Family: Manipulation/Planning/Progress → 10 Category → 25 Type), dual-layer detection architecture (rule-based classifier + VLM analysis), PolicyAdapter abstraction (supporting Random/BC-RNN/VLA), researched MimicGen/Pi0 checkpoint availability ✅ Execute scGPT benchmark experiments — Ran scGPT Phase 2 evaluation (77 experiments planned), completed 65/77 successfully with 12 failures (11 scgpt_uni2_qformer CUDA OOM due to parallel GPU usage with pca_uni2_qformer, 1 scgpt_uni_staig_fusion/151676 NaN failure) ✅ Add LLM structured JSON output support — Configured Anthropic API to use tool_use for structured JSON (defined submit_report tool schema), added OpenAI response_format: json_object parameter, kept _parse_json_response as fallback ✅ Implement export phase smart skip for already-summarized devices — Moved path calculation before _call_summarize, read _merged_devices list from existing file, check if current device in list to decide whether to skip API call, changed device_summary to per-device dict format ({device_name: summary}), updated _merged_devices and _source_device fields ✅ Batch generate 60 visualization videos for error benchmark — Created scripts/batch_visualize.py with 8-GPU parallel rendering (fixed EGL device ID relative indexing); generated 30 baseline + 30 randomized parameter videos covering all 30 scenes across 3 demos and force levels (3N/15N/20N) ✅ BC policy loading and trajectory saving functionality — Implemented _run_episode returning (EpisodeSummary, trajectory) tuple, _save_episode writing compressed NPZ (actions/rewards/eef_pos/gripper_closed_norm/object_positions), modified 3_collect_data.py to load BC checkpoint path from config ✅ CalendarPro codebase refactoring — Deleted redundant files (check_add.py, check_methods.py, nul, gemini.bat, ai_planner/ directory, 4 duplicate Claude test files), merged tests into test_claude_provider.py, extracted shared utilities (time_utils.py, provider_selector.py), unified keyword constants and meal time definitions across modules, all 26 tests passing ✅ Implement CalendarPro periodic self-check and recurring tasks — Created RecurringTaskStore (JSONL-based storage for user-defined recurring tasks) + PeriodicChecker (background asyncio loop with 15-minute check cycle), integrated with Discord bot, added commands (/recurring add/remove/pause/resume/list), implemented automatic meal/sleep scheduling, 18 new tests all passing ✅ Change default behavior to export-only — Changed no-subcommand behavior from cmd_legacy to cmd_export, added –summarize flag to top-level parser (default False), ensuring no API call unless explicitly specified –summarize ✅ Convert gadget test/ submodule to regular files — Removed git submodule tracking (git rm –cached test), cloned TzJ2006/test content, removed embedded .git, updated all documentation references (README.md, CLAUDE.md), decoupled from standalone repo Implementation & Fixes ✅ Update README and tutorial documentation — Updated 3 docs (gadget/README.md, summarize/README.md, summarize/tutorial.md): added merge –sync usage examples, updated default behavior description, revised rclone_remote field description, expanded cloud sync section, updated quick command reference ✅ Fix ccusage output format parsing bug — ccusage sometimes returns list instead of {daily:[], totals:{}} format, added type check: if list, wrap as {“daily”: data, “totals”: {}} to prevent AttributeError on dict.get ✅ Add _source_device field to gadget export JSON — Modified daily_summary.py to append device metadata (platform, user, hostname) to export JSON when –summarize flag not used, enabling quick identification of unsummarized logs ✅ Smoke test and end-to-end verification — Ran complete test workflow: GPU check → 41 unit tests → 4-step smoke test (scene generation/rejection analysis/data collection/result analysis) → policy generation test (random policy 2 rollouts → 1 scene) ✅ Publish daily reports to Hugo bugJournal — Read gadget/summarize/reports/2026-02-{12,13,14}.md files, added Hugo front matter (title/date/keywords/summary/draft fields using -05:00 timezone), wrote to website/content/bugJournal/ directory, deployed via update.sh ✅ Generate structured daily report for 2026-02-15 — As daily report analyst role, consolidated AI conversation logs from multiple devices (Mac + DCC cluster + TzJsDesktop + tianhe), generated structured JSON report with task list, problem-solution analysis, human-AI collaboration differences ✅ Update config –show to display structured remote paths — Added logs: and reports: subdirectory paths in rclone config display for user clarity on upload targets ✅ Update error benchmark project documentation to v4.2.0 — Updated 项目全景总结.md with 30 scenes (vs 3), 60 videos, 8×A800 GPU verification, revised milestones M5/M7 status, updated file counts and progress metrics ✅ Deploy website to GitHub Pages — Ran website/update.sh script, which processed 4 markdown files (replaced image/video links), ran Hugo build (139 pages), pushed public/ directory to GitHub Pages repository Problems & Solutions Critical Issues 1. Visualization videos showed no visible error effects despite logged force application and body displacement Solution: Root cause: Demo actions during injection caused OSC controller (kp=150) to compensate external force; fix: use neutral actions (zero vector) during injection+settling phases, allowing force to accumulate without controller interference
Key Insight: OSC position controller actively resists external forces when tracking a trajectory; must use neutral actions to observe disturbance effects. Validation phase (drop.py) was correct all along with neutral actions.
2. Visualization video shows robotic arm barely moves, but logs show force applied and body position changed (15mm-117mm) Solution: First fix: re-apply force every step during duration window (mimic drop.py), solving MuJoCo’s per-step xfrc_applied clearing. Verification confirmed force re-application (Test 1: 11 times, Test 3: 21 times). Discovered deeper issue: visualization uses demo actions, OSC controller (kp=150) compensates external force effect
Key Insight: Layer 1 problem: MuJoCo physics engine auto-clears xfrc_applied after each physics step (prevent accumulation), must re-apply every step. Layer 2 problem: validation uses neutral actions (zero vector), controller only maintains position; visualization uses demo actions, controller tracks demo trajectory at every substep (20 substeps), generating corrective torques far exceeding external force. True fix requires using neutral actions during force window + settling period
3. Q-Former process crashed/died after completing 6/11 sections, potentially losing hours of GPU compute work Solution: Pipeline’s checkpoint recovery mechanism (checking for existing *_embeddings.npz files) automatically detected completed sections and resumed from section 151672, skipping already-finished work
Key Insight: 断点续跑 (checkpoint/resume) mechanism worked as designed; run_pipeline.py’s is_completed() check prevented re-running expensive experiments, validating the recovery architecture from original implementation plan
4. JSON file writes interrupted by Ctrl+C/crash/power loss lead to half-written invalid JSON, causing parse failures on next read Solution: Implemented _atomic_write() function: write to temp file (mkstemp), flush, os.replace for atomic rename, cleanup temp on failure. Ensures either old complete file or new complete file exists, never half-written state
Key Insight: Atomic write is standard pattern for preventing file corruption: write temp → rename atomically. Applies to all JSON/config file persistence scenarios
5. Multi-device workflow requires manually copying log files from each device to one machine for merge, tedious operations Solution: Export uploads to /logs/, merge uploads to /reports/, added –sync flag to auto-download all device logs from remote (filter with –include ‘{date}_*.json’), dedupe paths then call API to generate report
Key Insight: rclone subdirectory isolation + –sync flag simplifies multi-device collaboration from ‘manual copying’ to ‘one command’, significantly reducing mental burden
6. scgpt_uni2_qformer experiments failed with CUDA out of memory (11/12 failures), tried to allocate 2 MiB but GPU only had 2.31 MiB free, process already using 21.46 GiB Solution: Identified root cause: pca_uni2_qformer was monopolizing GPU (21.5 GB), leaving insufficient memory for parallel scgpt_uni2_qformer runs; determined scgpt Q-Former experiments must wait until pca Q-Former completes to avoid GPU contention
Key Insight: Q-Former training requires ~22GB GPU memory; running two Q-Former experiments in parallel on 32GB GPU leads to OOM; sequential execution is required despite being technically independent tasks
7. LLM occasionally returns invalid JSON (markdown code blocks, extra text), causing json.loads to fail Solution: For Anthropic use tool_use to force structured output (define submit_report tool schema + tool_choice), for OpenAI use response_format: json_object, keep 3-layer fallback parsing logic as degradation path
Key Insight: Cloud LLM APIs now support structured output; should prioritize SDK features over fragile regex/brace-matching parsing
8. Same device running export –summarize multiple times on same day repeatedly calls API, wasting cost and time Solution: Added _merged_devices list in JSON to track already-summarized devices, read existing file on export to check if current device in list, skip API call if present and reuse existing summary
Key Insight: Implementing idempotency through persistent state avoids redundant operations; device_summary changed to {device_name: summary} format makes each device’s contribution clearly visible
9. EGL rendering assertion failed: MUJOCO_EGL_DEVICE_ID must be contained in CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES Solution: Fixed batch_visualize.py GPU assignment: set MUJOCO_EGL_DEVICE_ID=0 (always) and adjust CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES per process to match (e.g., CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=3 → MUJOCO_EGL_DEVICE_ID=3 incorrect; should both use relative ID 0)
Key Insight: MuJoCo EGL uses relative GPU indexing within CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES, not absolute system GPU IDs
10. ProximityDetector and CollisionDetector only generate impulse type specs, leading to single-type scene library (all 30 scenes are impulse/tip_over) Solution: Modified both detectors’ detect() methods to replace _generate_impulse_specs() with _generate_mixed_specs(), generating impulse+pose_perturb+friction candidates simultaneously, configured max_scenes_per_demo increased to 10
Key Insight: Detector design pattern proposing multi-type specs (GraspPrecondDetector already has this) needs to be extended to all frequently-triggered detectors to avoid single type consuming quota
11. One staig_fusion failure (scgpt_uni_staig_fusion/151676) due to ‘Input X contains NaN’ error, same section also failed for pca_uni_staig_fusion Solution: Identified as section-specific data issue (151676) causing model collapse (zero-variance embeddings after training), not a code bug; same section consistently fails across different gene encoders (pca, scgpt), indicating fundamental data quality issue
Key Insight: When same section fails across multiple configurations with NaN/zero-variance symptoms, it’s likely a data issue (missing spatial coordinates, corrupted expression values) rather than code bug; STAIG model collapse is a known failure mode for problematic inputs
12. CalendarPro had 8 instances of duplicate AI provider selection logic scattered across different files (discord_bot: 4, api_routes: 2, executor: 1, planner: 1) Solution: Created src/ai/provider_selector.py with unified get_first_available_provider() and generate_with_fallback() functions, replaced all 8 instances with imports, consolidated error handling and timeout logic in one place
Key Insight: When same pattern repeats 8 times it must be extracted; beyond reducing duplication, unified error handling and timeout logic prevents inconsistent behavior; Single Source of Truth principle applies to logic patterns not just data
13. BCPolicy fallback bug: adapter assigned before start_episode() call, leaving broken state on model load failure Solution: Refactored _ensure_loaded() to create adapter in local variable, call start_episode(), only assign to self._adapter after both succeed; on exception, set _fallback=True and keep _adapter=None
Key Insight: Lazy loading must be transactional: all initialization steps must succeed before committing state; partial success leads to broken objects
14. collector.py’s _save_episode() had trajectory saving TODO unimplemented, BC policy loading logic also TODO Solution: _run_episode() changed to return (summary, trajectory) tuple, _save_episode() saves NPZ (actions/rewards/eef_pos/gripper/objects), 3_collect_data.py added BCPolicy loading logic (read checkpoint path from config.evaluation.models, device from policy_generation)
Key Insight: Trajectory data needs complete state preservation (not just actions/rewards, but also EEF position, gripper state, all object positions), NPZ compressed storage (~64KB each), controlled by config flag
15. Three duplicate _parse_time() implementations in energy_projection, intelligent_scheduler, and essential_scheduler (lines 137-144, 362-369, 251-258 respectively) Solution: Created src/core/time_utils.py providing parse_time() and parse_datetime_with_tz() functions, all three files changed to import and use shared implementation
Key Insight: Time parsing is infrastructure-level functionality that should have unified handling rather than per-module implementations; eliminates risk of inconsistent time parsing behavior across modules
General Issues 16. ccusage in some environments returns list instead of {daily:[], totals:{}}, causing data.get(‘daily’) to throw AttributeError Solution: Added type check after json.loads: if list, wrap as {“daily”: data, “totals”: {}}, ensuring subsequent dict.get calls don’t crash
Key Insight: External tool output formats may change with version/environment; defensive programming should check types and handle gracefully
17. Git submodule complexity for gadget test/ directory causing confusion and clone overhead Solution: Converted to regular files: git rm –cached test, deleted .gitmodules, cloned repo content, removed .git, updated all docs to remove submodule references
Key Insight: Submodules add complexity for single-maintainer projects with infrequent changes; flattening simplifies workflow when cross-repo development isn’t needed
18. First implementation attempt interrupted by user during ExitPlanMode phase, plan needed re-execution in second session Solution: User provided complete plan text in second session requesting direct implementation, AI read source files and executed file writing and deployment
Key Insight: Writing plan to file is more reliable than relying on ExitPlanMode workflow across sessions; user can paste plan text in new session to drive execution directly
19. Daily report .md files lacked Hugo front matter, preventing direct use in Hugo site Solution: Read existing bugJournal entries to understand front matter format, extracted summary from report blockquote lines (> …), generated standard YAML front matter with title/date (-05:00 timezone matching existing entries)/keywords/summary/draft fields
Key Insight: Summary field can directly reuse the blockquote summary line from reports for consistency; timezone must match existing entries (-05:00) to maintain chronological ordering
20. BC policy loading when config checkpoint path is null, causing load failure but error message unclear Solution: Optimized error message: distinguish 3 cases (1) config key exists but value null → prompt to set path in benchmark_v4.yaml; (2) key doesn’t exist and not valid file path → list available models; (3) found checkpoint → normal load
Key Insight: Error messages should provide actionable fix guidance (tell user which file to set which field), not just report error state
21. User requested ‘push to github’ but website directory is not a git repository, causing confusion about what needs pushing Solution: AI checked git status of both repositories (gadget: clean working tree, website: not a git repo), explained that website deployment completed via update.sh script pushing public/ subdirectory, gadget already up to date
Key Insight: Hugo site deployment commonly uses public/ subdirectory as git repo pushed to GitHub Pages, source files repo and deployment repo are separate; AI should check status before acting rather than blindly executing commands
Human vs AI Approaches Strategic Level Root cause diagnosis strategy for visualization bug Role Approach Human User immediately suspected controller compensation when seeing logged displacement without visual effect, directed focus to action source (demo vs neutral) AI AI initially focused on re-applying force (first fix attempt), only recognized controller interference pattern after explicit context about validation phase using neutral actions Difference Analysis: Human applied domain knowledge (control theory: position controllers resist disturbances) to jump to root cause; AI fixed symptoms first, then needed guided comparison to validation code
Q-Former process crash recovery Role Approach Human Detected process stopped, asked AI to check status and restart if needed, trusted the checkpoint mechanism to avoid re-running completed work AI Verified GPU idle (1 MiB usage), counted completed sections (6/11), restarted pipeline which auto-detected completions and resumed from section 151672 (5 remaining) Difference Analysis: Human correctly anticipated checkpoint mechanism would work; AI executed recovery systematically (status check → identify restart point → restart → verify); collaboration demonstrated trust in designed recovery architecture
Handling scGPT Q-Former CUDA OOM failures Role Approach Human Observed 12 failures, asked AI to investigate causes AI Analyzed error messages (CUDA OOM: tried to allocate 2 MiB, only 2.31 MiB free, process using 21.46 GiB), identified pca_uni2_qformer monopolizing GPU, concluded scgpt_uni2_qformer must wait for pca Q-Former completion Difference Analysis: AI provided root cause analysis (GPU memory contention) and proposed sequential execution strategy; human accepted analysis and understood the need to defer scgpt Q-Former experiments despite them being logically independent
Task breakdown and plan formulation Role Approach Human User explicitly required ‘make a plan’ in multiple tasks, reviewed plan before saying ‘implement’, separating planning from execution AI AI follows user instructions: first enter Plan Mode to research code and formulate detailed plan (marking line numbers, code snippets), exit after completion, wait for user confirmation before implementation Difference Analysis: Human reduces risk through step-by-step control; AI doesn’t write code without explicit ‘implement’ instruction; both form good planning-review-execution closed loop
Plan execution interruption handling Role Approach Human User interrupted during ExitPlanMode in first session, directly pasted complete plan text in second session requesting implement AI AI read plan text in second session and executed immediately (read files → generate front matter → write → run deploy), didn’t depend on tool call chain state Difference Analysis: Human solved cross-session state loss by writing plan to file and pasting in new session; this ‘plan text’ pattern is more robust than relying on ExitPlanMode flow
Plan-driven development workflow Role Approach Human User writes complete implementation plans in markdown with context, file-by-file changes, and verification steps; provides plans via paste in new sessions to overcome context loss AI AI follows plans step-by-step, creates task tracking, executes modifications, runs verification; relies on plans for coordination Difference Analysis: Human compensates for AI’s session boundaries and context window by externalizing plans; AI executes mechanically but can’t bridge sessions without explicit artifacts
Policy injection framework motivation understanding Role Approach Human User asked ‘can’t we already inject error to human demo? why can’t we inject to VLA?’, directly pointing out gap between current capability and goal is action source, not entire detection-injection-validation pipeline AI Initially designed 8-step complex plan (create PolicyAdapter, modify RolloutGenerator, add obs preprocessing, etc.), after user challenge quickly converged to core: just replace action = demo_actions[step] with action = policy.predict(obs), rest of pipeline reuses as-is Difference Analysis: Human used rhetorical question to quickly grasp problem essence (‘why can’t inject to VLA’), AI initially inclined toward designing complete architecture expansion; after dialogue AI understood core is just action source replacement, existing pipeline already policy-agnostic
CalendarPro codebase refactoring scope Role Approach Human Requested implementation of complete plan covering three categories: delete unnecessary files, merge test files, extract shared utilities (8 provider selection sites, 3 time parsing functions, keyword/meal time definitions) AI Executed all three categories systematically, created new utility modules (time_utils.py, provider_selector.py), updated imports across 8+ files, ran tests to verify (26/26 passing) Difference Analysis: Human provided comprehensive refactoring plan upfront; AI executed methodically with test verification at each step; plan’s completeness enabled smooth execution without mid-course corrections
Understanding rclone mechanism Role Approach Human User saw code calling _rclone_upload, uncertain ‘why sync works without providing info’, proactively asked about working principle and prerequisite configuration steps AI AI checked _load_config and _rclone_upload logic, confirmed dependency on rclone_remote field in ~/.config/summarize/config.json, returns directly when not configured, explained to user and detailed rclone config process Difference Analysis: Human maintains vigilance against ‘seemingly automatic’ behavior (worried about mistaken data transmission), AI provides definitive answer through code tracing; human’s security awareness drove this deep discussion
Default behavior adjustment Role Approach Human User explicitly requested ‘default should not merge’ during plan review, AI understood as merge –sync being opt-in, but user clarified again ’no subcommand should default to export only without summarize’ AI AI initially understood as –sync behavior default value issue, after user correction realized it’s cmd_legacy → cmd_export main entry switch, modified plan Difference Analysis: User’s natural language expression of intent (‘default should not merge’) can be ambiguous, AI needs context to understand; after multiple rounds of clarification both reached consensus
Policy adapter architecture decision Role Approach Human User specified adapter pattern with graceful fallback in BCPolicy, clear separation between direct use (RobomimicPolicyAdapter throws) and wrapped use (BCPolicy falls back to zero-action) AI AI implemented as specified, initially had transactional bug in fallback logic, fixed after testing revealed broken state on load failure Difference Analysis: Human designed layered error handling strategy (strict at low level, forgiving at high level); AI implemented but needed iteration to make fallback truly transactional
Implementation Level Documentation update detail control Role Approach Human After AI completed code modifications, user separately requested ‘please update readme and tutorial’ to ensure doc-code sync AI AI read three docs, identified 12 places needing updates (default behavior description, rclone_remote description, quick command examples, cloud sync section, config –show output, etc.), modified each one Difference Analysis: Human proactively requests documentation sync to avoid code-doc inconsistency; AI execution exhausts all relevant mention points to ensure global consistency
Batch video parameter randomization scope Role Approach Human User specified concrete range (1x-2x) and which parameters to randomize (force, duration, settle, steps); asked for 10 videos per group with explicit baseline vs randomized distinction AI AI designed implementation matching spec: random.uniform(1.0, 2.0) multiplier per parameter, 30-scene selection stratified by demo and force level, parallel GPU execution Difference Analysis: Human provided clear requirements; AI translated to implementation with no ambiguity. Good spec → straightforward implementation.
Chinese plan document organization structure Role Approach Human Requested ‘update plans in Chinese, include big goals, medium goals, small goals, nested together, write all context and plans to file’, asked AI to write plan to file before ExitPlanMode AI First attempt directly called ExitPlanMode, rejected then changed to first Write to plan file, organized as big goals (M1/M2/M3) → medium goals → small goals (S1-S8) nested structure, wrote complete context and implementation details in Chinese Difference Analysis: Human explicitly required documentation-first (write plan to file before ExitPlanMode), AI’s first reaction was direct ExitPlanMode with plan text; human’s requirement for hierarchical documentation organization (big/medium/small goal nesting) reflects engineering project management thinking
Deployment workflow ownership Role Approach Human Proactively provided complete plan text in second session, bypassing the interrupted ExitPlanMode workflow from first session, directly driving execution with explicit plan content AI Followed plan step-by-step: read files, generate front matter, write to target directory, run deploy script Difference Analysis: Human solved cross-session state loss problem by externalizing plan to text and pasting in new session; AI execution layer was smooth; key decisions (format, paths) were locked in by plan text provided upfront
Handling ‘git push’ request Role Approach Human Requested ‘push to github’, intending to ensure code is saved AI Checked status of both repos (gadget: clean, website: not a git repo), discovered website doesn’t need push, gadget already up to date, explained current state to user rather than blindly executing Difference Analysis: AI prioritized checking state before acting, avoided meaningless operations; could have been more proactive in suggesting next steps when explaining website is not a git repo
AI Limitations Critical Limitations Failed to recognize controller compensation as root cause of invisible force effects until explicitly guided via comparison to validation code that used neutral actions Based on log numerical values (body position change 15mm-117mm) judged visualization fix successful, but ignored visualization’s core goal of visible movement in video. Only after user challenge launched deeper investigation and discovered layer 2 problem (demo actions + OSC controller compensation) Cannot bridge session boundaries without explicit artifacts (plans, files); relies on user to paste plans into new conversations when context is lost Initial BCPolicy fallback implementation was not transactional (assigned adapter before start_episode verification), required testing and bugfix iteration When designing policy injection framework initially inclined toward complexity (8-step plan, creating multiple new files), after user’s rhetorical question ‘why can’t inject to VLA’ realized core is just action source replacement, existing pipeline already policy-agnostic General Limitations Had ambiguous understanding of natural language instruction ‘default should not merge’, initially understood as –sync behavior rather than main entry switch, required user’s second clarification First approach to batch GPU assignment used incorrect EGL device ID mapping (didn’t understand relative vs absolute indexing), needed correction after error During first session, AI called ExitPlanMode without anticipating user might interrupt, causing plan to need re-implementation in second session, demonstrating poor continuity across sessions When acting as daily report analyzer tool, AI can only process conversation records passed in, cannot proactively retrieve other conversations from the same day not included in input When researching Pi0/Pi0.5 checkpoint availability found robosuite has no fine-tuned checkpoint, but didn’t proactively propose LIBERO environment integration alternative or assess workload When handling website not being an independent git repo, AI only explained current state passively, didn’t proactively ask if user wants to establish version management for website source files Learnings Key Learnings OSC position controllers (kp=150) generate joint torques that far exceed typical external forces (15-20N), actively compensating disturbances when tracking trajectories. Neutral actions disable tracking, allowing force effects to accumulate. Checkpoint/resume mechanisms validate their design when actual crashes occur; MIHD’s is_completed() file-existence check successfully prevented 6 sections (~9 hours of GPU work) from being re-run after process crash Atomic write is standard pattern for preventing file corruption: tempfile + write + rename. Applies to all JSON/config file persistence scenarios Multi-device collaboration tool design pattern: local generation → upload to cloud subdirectories → one-command download+merge, encapsulating complex workflow into single command via –sync flag MuJoCo automatically zeros xfrc_applied after each physics step (0.002s default); must re-apply force every control step for sustained effect. Validation code does this correctly in 10-step loop. Error scene generation pipeline is fully modular: detectors→injectors→validators all follow plugin pattern and can be reused unchanged for policy-based generation by just swapping action source (policy.predict vs demo_actions[i]). Robosuite’s env.step() contains 20 physics substeps (default control_freq=20, each control step 0.04s = 20×0.002s physics steps). OSC controller tracks target pose at every substep (kp=150 stiffness), external force smaller than control torque will be compensated away Error injection pipeline is already policy-agnostic at action level (EnvWrapper.step(action) doesn’t care about action source). Core of extending from demo injection to policy injection is just action source replacement (demo_actions[step] → policy.predict(obs)), detection-injection-validation pipeline reuses as-is Writing plans to .md files and pasting in new sessions is effective way to preserve context across sessions, more robust than depending on tool call chains Q-Former training requires ~22GB GPU memory; on 32GB GPU, parallel Q-Former experiments cause OOM despite being logically independent; memory-intensive experiments require sequential execution even when compute graph is independent Cloud LLM API’s structured output features (Anthropic tool_use, OpenAI response_format) should be prioritized, can significantly reduce JSON parsing failure rate Idempotency design: through _merged_devices list tracking processed state, skip completed operations on re-run, saving API cost MUJOCO_EGL_DEVICE_ID uses relative indexing within CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES, not absolute system GPU IDs. For single-GPU processes, always set MUJOCO_EGL_DEVICE_ID=0 regardless of which physical GPU. Lazy loading with fallback requires transactional initialization: create object in local scope, validate all initialization steps, only assign to instance variable after complete success. Partial success creates broken state. Detector design should let high-frequency triggered detectors propose multi-type specs to avoid single type consuming max_scenes_per_demo quota. GraspPrecondDetector has correct design (impulse+pose_perturb+gripper_bias), needs extension to ProximityDetector and CollisionDetector When same data section (e.g., 151676) consistently fails across multiple model configurations with NaN/zero-variance symptoms, root cause is likely data quality issue (missing coordinates, corrupted values) not code bug Code duplication threshold: when same pattern repeats 8 times, it must be extracted into shared utility; beyond reducing repetition, centralized error handling and timeout logic prevents inconsistent behavior Trajectory data saving should include complete state (actions/rewards + EEF position + gripper state + all object positions), NPZ compressed storage (~64KB/episode), controlled by config flag (evaluation.save_trajectories) Infrastructure-level functionality (time parsing, provider selection) should have unified implementations rather than per-module copies; Single Source of Truth principle applies to logic patterns not just data definitions BC policy loading needs to read checkpoint path and device from config, error messages should provide actionable fix guidance (tell user which file to set which field). Pi0/Pi0.5 and other foundation models currently have no robosuite fine-tuned checkpoint, need self fine-tune or integrate LIBERO environment Practical Learnings rclone remote configuration requires two steps: install rclone + run rclone config to bind cloud account, rclone_remote in script just specifies ‘where to upload’ Hugo deployment commonly uses public/ subdirectory as git repo pushed to GitHub Pages, with source file repo and deployment repo separated as standard practice External tool output formats may change with version/environment, defensive programming should check types and handle gracefully (e.g., ccusage returning list case) Git submodule cleanup requires: git rm –cached , delete .gitmodules, clone fresh copy, remove .git directory, stage as regular files. Also need to update all documentation references and remove .git/modules/ if present. Hugo front matter summary field can directly extract from report blockquote lines for content reuse Conversation Summaries Gadget (daily report publishing tool) ✅ Implement report publishing plan and deploy to GitHub Pages 05:16:45.089 | claude_code User provided complete plan text in new session, requested direct implementation. AI read three reports (2026-02-12/13/14), generated Hugo front matter for each, wrote to website/content/bugJournal/, ran update.sh successfully building and deploying 139 pages to GitHub Pages.
