summary: Multi-project development day: published historical daily reports to Hugo website, monitored long-running MIHD benchmark experiments on DCC cluster, completed CalendarPro codebase refactoring with recurring tasks, enhanced gadget/summarize tool with atomic writes and multi-device sync, advanced Error Recovery Benchmark with 118 scenes and policy-based generation framework
title: Bug Journal 2026-02-15

content:
Daily Report — 2026-02-14 Daily Overview What was done: Delivered major improvements across four projects: (1) MIHD: architectural refactoring to eliminate redundant computation, (2) error recovery benchmark: fixed force injection and detector triggering bugs, (3) CalendarPro: added batch delete with interactive confirmation UI and fixed 403 permission errors, (4) gadget/summarize: implemented config system, rclone sync, and Claude CLI backend How it was done: Through architectural redesign (two-phase pipeline separation), deep debugging (force mechanics, task phase detection), feature development (batch operations with safety confirmations), infrastructure improvements (config files, cloud sync, multi-API support), and systematic code quality enhancements Impact: MIHD: reduced embedding computation by 62% (286→110 deduplicated tasks), enabled model reuse across sections. Error recovery: successfully generated PreGrasp scenes, implemented per-detector cooldown. CalendarPro: eliminated 403 warnings, enabled pattern-based multi-event operations. Gadget/summarize: established cross-device workflow with automatic cloud synchronization DCC What was done: Deep work on MIHD project: monitored benchmarks, designed and implemented complete two-phase pipeline refactoring (extract embeddings → evaluate fusions), analyzed vision encoding bottlenecks, created optimization plans How it was done: Tracked benchmark progress and timeouts, removed inefficient strategies. Implemented 11 pipeline modules with 3 entry scripts. Ran end-to-end tests. Analyzed code to identify bottlenecks: serial patch extraction, excessive GPU cache clearing, lack of encoder-level caching Impact: Transformed architecture from O(encoders × fusions × sections) to O(encoders × sections + fusions × sections). Cut UNI2 embedding computation from ~77 times to 11 times (86% reduction). Identified concrete optimization targets for 2-5x speedup MacBook What was done: Quick maintenance on gadgets toolkit: ran /init to update CLAUDE.md, then cleaned up documentation after user deleted obsolete tools How it was done: Analyzed codebase structure, rewrote CLAUDE.md to reflect current state (only summarize/ and benchmark/ remain), removed references to 6 deleted tool categories Impact: Synchronized documentation with actual codebase, future Claude instances will have accurate project overview tianhe What was done: Fixed error recovery benchmark: force injection mechanism bugs, PreGrasp detector triggering, per-detector cooldown implementation, force visualization enhancements How it was done: Root cause analysis (task_phase stub, body name mismatch, global cooldown blocking), code refactoring (get_task_phase, rollout_generator cooldown tracking, visualizer force override), iterative testing with scene generation and video rendering Impact: PreGrasp detector now functional (generates scenes successfully), force injection mechanism works correctly (施加到正确body), per-detector cooldown prevents interference. Force visibility issue remains under investigation (30N insufficient against OSC controller) TzJsDesktop What was done: Two major feature developments: (1) CalendarPro improvements (Google Calendar 403 fix, batch delete with interactive Discord UI, code quality), (2) gadget/summarize tool upgrades (config system, rclone sync, Claude CLI backend, ccusage fixes, documentation) How it was done: CalendarPro: modified API calls (settings vs calendarList), extended data models for batch operations, created Discord button confirmation views, added fallback logic for LLM failures. Gadget/summarize: implemented config file system, integrated rclone CLI, added claude_cli backend via subprocess, fixed Windows/nested environment issues, enhanced JSON parsing robustness Impact: CalendarPro: eliminated recurring 403 errors, enabled users to delete multiple events at once with safety confirmations. Gadget/summarize: established complete cross-device workflow (export → sync → merge → deploy) with automatic cloud synchronization, no API key required for default Claude CLI backend Completed major architectural refactoring of MIHD benchmarking pipeline (two-phase extraction-evaluation architecture), fixed critical error recovery benchmark bugs (force injection mechanism, detector triggering, per-detector cooldown), enhanced CalendarPro with batch delete and Google Calendar permission fixes, and upgraded gadget/summarize tool with config system, rclone cloud sync, and multi-API backend support
Tasks Architecture & Strategy ✅ MIHD two-phase pipeline architecture implementation — Designed and implemented complete pipeline refactoring: created 11 pipeline modules and 3 entry scripts with pipeline_config.yaml. Phase 1: extract and cache all encoder embeddings (deduplicated). Phase 2: load cached embeddings and run fusion+evaluation. Tested successfully on section 151508 across 4 fusion strategies. ✅ Fix error recovery benchmark force injection mechanism — Fixed critical bug where force was applied to wrong body (bin2 instead of gripper EEF). Root cause: _get_eef_body_name() missing ‘gripper0_eef’ in possible_names list. Also implemented persistent force mechanism to fix MuJoCo xfrc_applied auto-clearing issue. ✅ Fix PreGrasp detector not triggering — Root cause: get_task_phase() always returned ‘unknown’ due to stub _check_phase_condition(). Fixed by using get_task_completion_stages() for phase detection. Also fixed lookahead to use demo actions, proximity gate bypass, per-detector cooldown, and target body naming. Simplified detection logic to check N-step-ahead state change instead of continuous approaching. 🔄 Investigate force visibility issue in error recovery benchmark — Even with 30N force override, robot arm shows no visible movement in videos. Requires force mechanics investigation (OSC control loop dominance, force duration too short, or injection mechanism issue). ✅ Implement batch delete functionality for CalendarPro — Extended DeleteData model with date_from/date_until/batch fields, updated LLM prompts, modified search_events to accept custom time ranges, rewrote _handle_delete handler, created BatchDeleteApprovalView with Discord buttons, added batch delete utterances to semantic router. ✅ Implement gadget/summarize config system — Created ~/.config/summarize/config.json configuration system supporting device_name, logs_dir, reports_dir, rclone_remote. Implemented config –show and –init subcommands. Resolution order: CLI flag > env var > config file > default. ✅ Implement rclone cloud sync for gadget/summarize — Integrated rclone CLI tool for automatic cloud upload after export/merge. Supports headless server scenario. Added rclone_path config for no-sudo environments. ✅ MIHD vision encoding performance analysis — Analyzed vision encoding bottlenecks: identified serial patch extraction (10-30s), excessive torch.cuda.empty_cache() calls (~100 times), and lack of encoder-level caching. Created detailed three-part optimization plan. ✅ Implement per-detector cooldown for error recovery benchmark — Changed from global cooldown_counter to per-detector cooldown_counters dict so one detector’s cooldown doesn’t block others (proximity cooldown was blocking pregrasp). ✅ Fix Google Calendar 403 insufficientPermissions error — Changed get_user_timezone() from calendarList().get() API to settings().get() API which only requires calendar.events scope, eliminating 403 errors that appeared on every user message. ✅ Add fallback logic for batch delete when LLM fails — When LLM times out (due to nested Claude Code environment), added _raw_input passthrough and _extract_delete_query() regex extraction to parse search keywords, date ranges, and batch flags from raw user input. ✅ Add Claude CLI as default API backend for summarize — Implemented summarize_with_claude_cli function via subprocess calling ‘claude –print’. No API key required. Set as default backend replacing anthropic. ✅ Fix Windows subprocess and nested Claude Code issues — Added shell=True for npx and claude calls to support Windows .cmd files. Removed CLAUDECODE environment variable to avoid nested session detection. ✅ Implement export merge on existing files — Added deduplication logic based on (source, project, timestamp) tuple. Multiple exports of same day/device won’t lose previous conversation records. 🔄 Improve Discord response details in CalendarPro — Enhanced delete operation responses to show event names with dates/times, but other intents still lack sufficient detail (e.g., schedule confirmations should specify what was scheduled at what time). ✅ Fix ccusage Opus fee calculation bug — Discovered –offline mode showed Opus 4.6 pricing as $0. Removed –offline parameter to fetch latest pricing online. Fixed fee display from $0 to $19.11. ✅ Optimize ccusage timing and data format — Moved fetch_ccusage after AI summary to include summary’s own token consumption. Stored ccusage raw JSON format directly. Rewrote _merge_token_usages to support per_device details. ✅ Enhance JSON parsing robustness for summarize — Rewrote _parse_json_response with three-step fallback: direct parse → extract code block → find {to} substring. Handles LLM adding explanatory text before/after JSON. Implementation & Fixes ✅ MIHD benchmark monitoring and timeout configuration — Monitored slow-running training-based fusion methods. Increased timeout from 1 hour to 2 hours. Removed staig_fusion_e2e from configuration after user decision. ✅ Add force override and update config force ranges — Added –force_override parameter to visualizer for dynamic force testing. Increased force_norm_range from [3.0, 15.0] to [15.0, 45.0], force_clip to 60.0. ✅ Fix empty message crash in Discord bot — Added ‘if response:’ check before sending messages to prevent Discord API errors when handlers return empty strings after sending messages via channel directly. ✅ Update gadget/summarize documentation — Rewrote tutorial.md and README.md, added config file, cloud sync, rclone no-sudo installation, three API backend explanations. Created requirements.txt. ✅ CalendarPro code quality fixes — Added default replies for 4 new intents in dual_verify.py; changed bare except to except (ValueError, TypeError) in executor.py; moved Scheduler instantiation outside loop in periodic_checker.py. ✅ Gadgets project documentation cleanup — Updated CLAUDE.md via /init command, then cleaned up documentation after deletion of 6 tool categories. Updated both README.md and CLAUDE.md to reflect current state (only summarize/ and benchmark/). ✅ Clean up dead rejection tracking code — Removed add_rejected_candidate(), get_rejected_candidates(), analyze_rejected() from database.py; removed total_rejected/rejection_stats from DatabaseMeta; updated all references. Problems & Solutions Critical Issues 1. MIHD benchmark: Redundant embedding computation - same gene/vision encoders run hundreds of times for different fusion strategies (e.g., PCA computed 55 times, UNI2 computed 77 times) Solution: Designed and implemented two-phase pipeline: Phase 1 extracts and caches all encoder embeddings (deduplicated, 110 tasks for 286 experiments = 62% reduction), Phase 2 loads cached embeddings and only runs fusion+clustering+evaluation. UNI2 model now loads once for all 11 sections instead of 77 times.
Key Insight: Current caching uses {gene}{vision}{fusion}/{section}_embeddings.npz as key, including fusion name, so changing fusion forces re-extraction. Encoder-level caching (gene_cache/{encoder}/{section}.npz, vision_cache/{encoder}/{section}.npz) enables massive reuse. Model instance reuse across sections provides additional speedup.
2. Error recovery benchmark: Force applied to wrong body (bin2 instead of gripper EEF), causing no visible robot disturbance in videos Solution: Through detailed logging discovered force was applied to bin2. Root cause: _get_eef_body_name() missing ‘gripper0_eef’ in possible_names list, fallback geom search matched robot0_screen_collision (contains ’ee’) → bin2. Fixed by adding ‘gripper0_eef’, ‘robot0_right_hand’, ‘gripper0_gripper_base’ to possible_names.
Key Insight: EEF body names vary across robots (Sawyer uses gripper0_eef, not generic gripper_link), need explicit inclusion in possible names list. Fallback to geom search can mismatch strings containing ’ee’ with unrelated geoms.
3. Error recovery benchmark: PreGrasp detector never triggered despite being registered - get_task_phase() always returned ‘unknown’ Solution: Root cause: get_task_phase() called _check_phase_condition() which is a stub always returning False. All task_phase-dependent detectors (PreGrasp, GraspPrecond) were affected. Complete phase detection logic exists in get_task_completion_stages(). Fixed by rewriting get_task_phase() to use sensor-based detection. Also fixed multiple chained bugs: lookahead using neutral actions instead of demo actions, proximity gate blocking pre-contact detectors, global cooldown preventing simultaneous detection, MuJoCo body name mismatch.
Key Insight: Codebase had functional implementation (get_task_completion_stages) and always-fail stub (_check_phase_condition) coexisting, caller used wrong method. Symptom is detector not triggering, root cause is infrastructure-layer dead code. When observing logical contradictions (no reject but no output), prioritize infrastructure audit over symptom debugging.
4. Error recovery benchmark: duration_steps config parameter not working - all forces last only 1 step (0.05 seconds) Solution: MuJoCo automatically clears xfrc_applied after each sim.step(). Solution: modified collect_rollout_stats() to loop-call injector.apply() during first duration_steps of validation rollout, call injector.clear() after duration ends.
Key Insight: MuJoCo’s xfrc_applied is persistent field but gets auto-cleared, requiring application layer to explicitly reapply each step for sustained force. This is systemic bug affecting all error types.
5. Error recovery benchmark: 30N force shows no visible robot arm movement in visualization videos Solution: Added –force_override parameter to test larger forces without regeneration; updated config force ranges to 15-45N. Issue persists at 30N - requires force mechanics investigation (possibly OSC control loop too strong, force duration too short at 1 step, or xfrc_applied not correctly applied).
Key Insight: Force injection mechanism works (xfrc_applied is set/cleared) but 30N may be insufficient against OSC controller’s 150 N/m stiffness, or 1-step duration (~20ms) too short for visible effect. OSC control loop may suppress external forces within one control cycle.
6. CalendarPro: Users couldn’t delete multiple calendar events matching a pattern (e.g., ‘cancel all GB teacher meetings before March 1st’) - bot would only say ‘Please specify which event to delete’ Solution: Extended data model to support date ranges and batch operations, updated LLM prompt to extract these fields, modified search to accept custom time windows, and added interactive confirmation UI with Discord buttons showing preview + explicit approval.
Key Insight: Single-item CRUD operations are insufficient for productivity tools - batch operations with safety confirmations (preview + explicit user approval) are essential for user workflows involving patterns.
7. Gadget/summarize: Headless server cannot install cloud drive app for automatic sync Solution: Researched and integrated rclone CLI tool, supports token copy authentication method (rclone authorize on machine with browser, copy token to headless server).
Key Insight: rclone supports 70+ cloud providers, headless authentication via token copy, is best cross-platform cloud CLI choice.
8. Error recovery benchmark: Global cooldown blocks pregrasp detector when proximity triggers first Solution: Implemented per-detector cooldown tracking (cooldown_counters dict) so each detector has independent cooldown periods. Filter detection_results to remove detectors still in cooldown.
Key Insight: Global cooldown is inappropriate for multi-detector systems where different detectors target different phases/conditions. Multi-detector systems need per-detector cooldown tracking.
9. CalendarPro: Google Calendar API returned 403 insufficientPermissions on every user message, causing console warnings Solution: Switched from calendarList().get() API to settings().get(setting=‘timezone’) API which only requires calendar.events scope instead of broader calendar access.
Key Insight: Different Google Calendar API endpoints require different OAuth scopes - settings API has lower permission requirements than calendarList API for timezone retrieval.
10. CalendarPro: When LLM timed out (nested Claude Code environment), batch delete failed because intent.data was empty - semantic router correctly classified intent but no structured data was extracted Solution: Added _raw_input passthrough to handlers, implemented _extract_delete_query() with regex patterns to parse search keywords from Chinese/English phrases, and added date extraction patterns to identify ranges like ‘3月1号前’.
Key Insight: Dual-verification systems need independent extraction capabilities in both paths - SR-only fallback cannot rely solely on LLM-generated structured data.
11. Gadget/summarize: ccusage –offline mode showed Opus 4.6 fee as $0 Solution: Removed –offline parameter, switched to online mode to fetch latest model pricing.
Key Insight: Offline mode’s pricing table doesn’t include latest models (like Opus 4.6), need online fetch for accurate billing. Though online mode slightly slower, data accuracy more important.
12. Error recovery benchmark: Proximity gate (max_eef_object_distance=0.05m) blocks pregrasp which operates at 0.05-0.1m Solution: Added detector names to bypass list for gate check - detectors with their own distance validation (pregrasp, grasp_precond) skip the gate.
Key Insight: Proximity gate was designed for contact-based detectors but incorrectly applied to all detectors regardless of their operating distance. Detector-specific constraints should not be overridden by global gates.
13. CalendarPro & gadget/summarize: Nested Claude Code sessions block Claude CLI subprocess calls, causing LLM classification to always time out during development Solution: CalendarPro: attempted to unset CLAUDECODE environment variable when spawning subprocess, but still failed - ultimate solution was to test outside nested environment. Gadget/summarize: successfully unset CLAUDECODE and CLAUDE_CODE_ENTRY in subprocess env.
Key Insight: Development environment tooling can interfere with the system under test in non-obvious ways - subprocess-based AI provider calls are particularly fragile to environment contamination. Subprocess inherits parent environment variables, need explicit removal.
14. Gadget/summarize: Windows subprocess couldn’t find npx (.cmd file) Solution: Added shell=True parameter in subprocess.run calls.
Key Insight: Windows npm global commands are actually .cmd files, Python subprocess doesn’t parse by default, need execution through shell.
15. Gadget/summarize: LLM-returned JSON may include code blocks or have explanatory text before/after Solution: Implemented three-step JSON parsing: direct parse → extract ```json code block → find {to} substring.
Key Insight: LLM output format is unstable, need multi-layer fallback to ensure robustness.
16. Gadget/summarize: Multiple exports of same day/device would overwrite existing conversation records Solution: When detecting existing file, read and merge after deduplication based on (source, project, timestamp) tuple.
Key Insight: Deduplication key should be minimal field combination uniquely identifying conversation. device_summary and token_usage use latest value as they are cumulative statistics.
General Issues 17. CalendarPro: BatchDeleteApprovalView returned empty string after sending confirmation via channel.send(), causing main loop to attempt Discord API call with empty message which fails Solution: Added ‘if response:’ check before message.channel.send(response) to skip sending when response is empty.
Key Insight: When handlers use channel directly to send messages (e.g., interactive views), they should return empty string to signal ‘already handled’ - the main loop needs to respect this signal.
18. MIHD benchmark: basic_contrastive fusion timing out with 1-hour limit, actually needs ~77 minutes per section Solution: Increased timeout to 2 hours (7200s) in run_all_benchmarks.py
Key Insight: Training-based fusion strategies require significantly more time than simple fusion strategies due to 300-550 epoch training loops
Human vs AI Approaches Strategic Level MIHD pipeline architecture decision Role Approach Human User immediately identified core inefficiency (redundant embedding computation) from high-level understanding and proposed solution: ‘for each slide, first you need to get the embedding of every slide… store them into a file, and then do the fusion for the same embeddings.’ User insisted on ‘complete rewrite’ rather than incremental patches. AI AI explored existing codebase in detail to understand exact implementation, then designed detailed module structure and wrote all code. AI focused on maintaining backward compatibility and reusing existing encoder implementations. Difference Analysis: User had architectural vision and made strategic decision (two-phase separation, complete rewrite). AI translated this into detailed technical plan with proper abstractions, handled edge cases, wrote implementation, and debugged issues. User drove ‘what’ and ‘why’, AI executed ‘how’ with technical depth.
Error recovery benchmark: Problem root cause tracing strategy Role Approach Human User questioned: ‘Why are candidates rejected? If no reject, why no output? Since this part not written, please delete dead code’. AI AI spent significant time debugging at symptom level (adding logs, adjusting parameters, analyzing trajectories), didn’t directly question infrastructure code correctness. Difference Analysis: User through logical reasoning quickly located contradiction (no reject but no output = infrastructure bug), while AI tends to assume existing code correct and look for causes at parameter/config level. User’s questioning drove discovery of STUB root cause.
CalendarPro: Recognizing need for batch delete functionality Role Approach Human User immediately identified current delete workflow was inadequate when they said ‘在3月1号前都没有GB老师组会了，请帮我取消这些日程’ and it failed - they understood users need to operate on patterns, not single items. AI AI’s initial implementation only supported single-item delete, requiring exact specification - didn’t anticipate batch operations as core requirement. Difference Analysis: Human recognized real-world usage patterns require bulk operations with date ranges; AI started with CRUD-style single-item paradigm.
Error recovery benchmark: PreGrasp detector logic simplification Role Approach Human User pointed out: if it’s a step change process, just check if current gripper in 0.0-0.3 range and changes after 10 steps, no need for complex ‘approaching’ logic (consecutive frame distance decreasing). AI AI initially implemented complex _check_approaching() method requiring consecutive frame distance decrease, maintained _prev_distance and _approaching_count state. Difference Analysis: User’s simplification is more robust: for step-change gripper actions, continuity checks fail; directly checking N-step-ahead state change is more essential criterion. AI tends to over-engineer, introducing unnecessary state tracking.
CalendarPro: Identifying 403 permission error root cause Role Approach Human User provided exact log line and immediately recognized it as OAuth scope mismatch, pointing to calendarList API call as culprit. AI AI initially proposed a fix plan but didn’t verify if fix actually addressed the specific API call causing 403. Difference Analysis: Human had domain knowledge of Google Calendar API scope requirements and could pinpoint exact API endpoint mismatch; AI followed more general debugging process.
Gadget/summarize: Cross-device sync requirements Role Approach Human User proposed constraints ‘don’t want to push to GitHub but want automatic cross-device transfer’ and proactively asked about headless server CLI upload solutions. AI AI through AskUserQuestion confirmed device environment (Windows+Mac+Linux) and preferences (cloud sync), researched rclone and other tools, provided complete solution. Difference Analysis: User clearly defined problem and constraints; AI filled details through research and technical solution design. User’s emphasis on headless scenario avoided solution defects.
MIHD timeout and fusion strategy selection Role Approach Human User observed training-based fusions are too slow and made pragmatic decisions: increase timeout to 2 hours, skip staig_fusion_e2e entirely, reduce Q-Former epochs from 200 to 50. User balanced completeness vs. runtime. AI AI monitored progress, reported timing statistics, and asked user for decision when Q-Former was blocking other experiments. AI provided options but deferred decision to user. Difference Analysis: User made resource allocation decisions based on domain priorities. AI provided data and options but recognized these are research judgment calls, not pure technical problems.
CalendarPro: Need for detailed confirmation messages Role Approach Human User requested more details in Discord responses, specifically wanting to see what tasks were scheduled at what times - they value transparency and verification. AI AI initially provided minimal success/failure messages without specifics. Difference Analysis: Human understands users need to verify system actions through detailed feedback; AI defaulted to terse confirmations.
Gadget/summarize: ccusage Opus billing issue diagnosis Role Approach Human User manually executed npx ccusage daily –json and found Opus fee displays normally, but script output incorrect, directly pointed out –offline might be the problem. AI AI first investigated from code logic, checked ccusage call and output handling, then compared with/without –offline output differences, finally located pricing table issue. Difference Analysis: User’s experience-based intuition quickly located issue; AI through systematic comparison verified hypothesis. User’s domain knowledge accelerated problem diagnosis.
Gadget/summarize: ccusage data format Role Approach Human User proposed ‘ccusage can export json, should be more convenient’, ‘want to see detailed ccusage analysis’, ‘when aggregating, aggregate all ccusage for the day’. AI AI changed fetch_ccusage to directly store raw JSON, rewrote _merge_token_usages to support per_device details, added cache hit rate and model cost breakdown in markdown. Difference Analysis: User provided requirements from usage perspective (detailed, complete, readable); AI translated requirements into technical implementation (preserve raw data, enhance rendering).
AI Limitations Critical Limitations Did not proactively suggest the two-phase architecture for MIHD despite analyzing the codebase. User had to identify the inefficiency and propose the solution. AI should have spotted the redundant computation pattern during initial exploration. Error recovery benchmark: Did not initially recognize get_task_phase() is STUB, spent significant time debugging at symptom level (adjusting detector parameters, analyzing trajectory data) rather than directly auditing infrastructure code correctness. CalendarPro: Didn’t initially recognize single-item delete operations were insufficient for real user workflows - required user to explicitly point out limitation with concrete example. Error recovery benchmark: Initially didn’t identify possibility of force applied to wrong body, first tried increasing force magnitude and duration, took detour before using debug logs to locate root cause. Error recovery benchmark: Didn’t recognize that 30N force showing no movement indicates fundamental mechanism issue (OSC control loop dominance, insufficient duration, or injection bug) rather than just needing more force. CalendarPro: When implementing first fix for 403 error, didn’t verify solution actually worked - user had to come back and point out bug still occurring. CalendarPro: Defaulted to minimal confirmation messages instead of detailed ones - didn’t anticipate users’ need for verification through comprehensive feedback. Gadget/summarize: Didn’t proactively discover ccusage should be called after AI summary (modified only after user pointed out). Gadget/summarize: Insufficient understanding of Claude CLI behavior in nested environments, only cleared environment variables which didn’t fully solve problem, didn’t proactively check stderr output or try other debugging methods. Gadget/summarize: Initial JSON parsing not robust enough, didn’t consider LLM output containing markdown code blocks or surrounding text. Gadget/summarize: Config file solution didn’t consider CLI tools like rclone until user explicitly raised headless requirements. General Limitations Error recovery benchmark: Repeatedly made same SSH command formatting error 40+ times despite multiple corrections - failed to internalize that SSH commands need explicit ‘cd’ before running scripts. Gadget/summarize: Grep tool frequently timed out, needed to rely on Bash grep or Read tool as replacement. Learnings Key Learnings Two-phase pipeline pattern for experiment matrices: When running M encoders × N fusions × K sections, split into Phase 1 (M × K encoding tasks with caching) + Phase 2 (M × N × K evaluation tasks loading from cache). Reduces O(M × N × K) redundant computation to O(M × K + M × N × K) with shared encoding. Cache key design matters: encoder-level caching enables cross-fusion sharing. MuJoCo xfrc_applied behavior: Though documentation says xfrc_applied persists between calls, mj_step() automatically clears it. Implementing sustained force requires application layer to explicitly reapply each step. Must explicitly clear via injector.clear() after duration_steps to implement true impulse behavior. STUB methods and complete implementations coexisting is code smell: When complete logic exists in one function but caller uses always-fail stub, should immediately audit calling relationships rather than debug parameters. When observing logical contradictions (no reject but no output), prioritize infrastructure audit over symptom debugging. Force injection visualization balance: OSC controllers with kp=150 N/m need forces ≥25N for visible displacement, but OSC control loop may suppress external forces within one control cycle (~20ms). Need careful balance between force magnitude and duration. Productivity tools need batch operations with safety mechanisms: (1) pattern matching to find multiple items, (2) preview of what will be affected, (3) explicit user confirmation before executing. Single-item CRUD operations insufficient for real workflows. rclone is best cross-platform cloud CLI choice, supports 70+ providers, headless authentication via token copy (rclone authorize on machine with browser, copy token to headless server). Model reuse across batches: For vision encoders processing multiple sections, load model once and reuse across sections instead of reload per section. Moves model initialization cost from O(sections) to O(1). Critical for large transformers like UNI2. Fallback mechanism mismatch risks: When primary method fails, falling back to looser matching (like geom name search) may produce wrong results (’ee’ matches screen not end-effector). Need additional validation in fallback path. EEF body names vary across robots (Sawyer uses gripper0_eef, not generic gripper_link), need explicit inclusion. Detection logic simplification for step-change systems: For discrete state systems (like gripper 0→1 step change), continuity-based detection (distance decreasing, approaching) is unreliable; should directly check state change (is it different after N steps). Multi-detector systems need per-detector cooldown tracking - global cooldown inappropriately blocks simultaneous detection from different sources. Detector-specific constraints (distance thresholds, phase requirements) should not be overridden by global gates - let detectors self-validate. OAuth scope requirements vary significantly between Google Calendar API endpoints - settings API requires lower permissions than calendarList API for same information (timezone). Dual-verification systems (semantic router + LLM) need independent extraction capabilities in both paths - can’t assume LLM will always succeed in producing structured output. ccusage offline mode’s pricing table doesn’t include latest models (like Opus 4.6), need online fetch for accurate billing. Though online mode slightly slower, data accuracy more important. Windows subprocess calling .cmd files needs shell=True, otherwise can’t find npm globally installed commands. Vision encoding bottlenecks: (1) Serial patch extraction loops are CPU-bound (3000-4000 iterations). (2) torch.cuda.empty_cache() after every batch (~100 times) adds significant overhead. (3) Hardcoded batch_size=32 may be suboptimal. Profile before optimize. Subprocess-based environment switching: When dependencies require different conda environments, use subprocess to invoke scripts in target environment rather than trying to switch within Python. User feedback should be detailed and specific - for calendar operations, always include: what action was taken, which event(s) were affected, and when they occur. Development environment can interfere with system under test - nested Claude Code sessions block subprocess-based AI provider calls. Subprocess inherits parent environment variables, need explicit removal of specific variables (like CLAUDECODE) to avoid nested detection. JSON parsing needs multi-layer fallback to handle LLM output various formats: direct parse → extract code block → find {to} substring. LLM output format unstable, need robustness. Config file priority should be: CLI parameter > environment variable > config file > default value, ensuring flexibility and overridability. Deduplication key selection should be minimal field combination uniquely identifying entity. For conversations: (source, project, timestamp). Practical Learnings When handlers send messages directly via channel (for interactive UI), they should return empty string to signal ‘already handled’ and main loop must check ‘if response:’ before sending. Testing found Windows subprocess couldn’t find npx and Claude CLI nested detection issues, fixed by adding shell=True and clearing CLAUDECODE environment variable. Successfully generated 2026-02-14 report.

title: Bug Journal 2026-02-14

content:
Daily Report — 2026-02-13 Daily Overview Completed Work: Progressed on four major projects: fixing MIHD benchmark data and designing the architecture, refactoring the gadget toolkit and making it open-source, completing the robot error recovery framework, and enhancing the production version of CalendarPro with intelligent features. Completion Method: Through systematic root cause analysis, modular architecture design, two-stage pipeline mode, comprehensive test coverage (each project passed 107 tests), appropriate asynchronous lifecycle management, and dual-language documentation. Impact: MIHD is ready for 286 experiments, with complete spatial data; gadgets support cross-device workflows and are open-source; the error recovery infrastructure is complete, including full demo replay functionality; CalendarPro is ready for production, featuring automatic learning, personalized energy prediction, and thought organization functions. DCC Completed Work: MIHD spatial transcriptomics benchmark: data repair (4 failed sections) and complete 7-stage enhancement plan design (standardization/Q-Former/LLaVA MLP/specific domain/batch correction/multi-section/config refactoring). Completion Method: Extracting missing spatial coordinates from Dataset.zip, fixing boundary checks for frequency filters, adding mclust→KMeans as a backup, fixing GPU monitoring thread issues; studying code libraries (config_manager.py 590 lines, run_benchmark.py 2600 lines) and QueST reference implementations, writing independent plans with exact line counts. Impact: All 11 DLPFC sections now work properly, with complete spatial data; comprehensive blueprints for stages 1 to 7 provide a detailed roadmap, including precise integration points and dependencies. MacBook Completed Work: Gadget toolkit: refactoring the daily summary tool into a two-stage multi-device architecture (export→merge), integrating ccusage token statistics, initializing Git repository with complete documentation. Completion Method: Redesigning daily_summary.py, adding CLI subcommands, collecting device information, designing recoverable JSON log format, processing token statistics using sub-processes; creating.gitignore file, managing test/ as a git submodule, writing 7 READMEs for all tools. Impact: The daily report tool now supports cross-device workflows and automatically tracks token usage; the gadget repository is open-source on GitHub with complete documentation, improving maintainability. Tianhe Completed Work: Error recovery benchmark: completed v4.1 infrastructure (4/4 validators, video annotations), diagnosed and fixed critical demo replay environment mismatches through three-tier root cause analysis, reducing documentation from 615 lines to 101 lines. Completion Method: Implemented LargeOffsetValidator and StuckValidator, with 41 tests passed; added frame annotations based on cv2; identified mismatches between PickPlace_D0+controller_configs and bare PickPlace by loading env_args from HDF5 metadata; systematically cleaned up approximately 190 dead lines. Impact: Scene generation capability increased by 10 times (from 3 scenes to 30 scenes), and EEF proximity was adjusted (5 cm vs 22 cm); triggering steps now occur during the robot’s approach to objects; documentation became more operable and concise, reduced in size by 1/6. TzJsDesktop Completed Work: CalendarPro major release: implemented an automated task scheduling system, fixed production bugs (double verification mismatch, incorrect ingestion, modification of repeated events), completed P0 reliability fixes and P1 intelligent upgrades, built an automated organization function for random thoughts, and updated comprehensive documentation. Completion Method: Created RecurringTaskStore with JSONL persistence and a periodic checker with asyncio background loop; fixed dual_verify.py to prioritize LLM intentions over semantic routers; added CalendarServiceError exception propagation; implemented elastic handling of JSON parsing, 30-second timeout for AI providers, message queue callback functions; added automatic collection of learning data and personalized settings based on energy prediction; built a thought capture system with idle detection and AI organization. Impact: 107 out of 110 tests passed; users can now see detailed error messages instead of silent failures; the system can automatically learn user behavior for personalized energy prediction; repeated tasks are automatically scheduled; brief thoughts are captured and organized into target hierarchies during idle periods. This was a comprehensive development day covering multiple projects: fixing MIHD spatial transcriptomics data issues and designing a 7-stage enhancement plan; refactoring the gadget daily report tool into a two-stage multi-device architecture and establishing a Git repository; implementing error recovery benchmark validators and fixing critical demo replay environment mismatches; building CalendarPro’s automated task scheduling function, fixing production bugs, and implementing P0/P1 roadmaps, along with random thought functions.
Tasks Architecture and Strategy - ✅ Designing MIHD 7-stage enhancement plan—Studied code libraries and QueST reference documents, designing stages 1 to 7: config refactoring, standardized integration, UNI2+scGPT experiments, Q-Former/LLaVA MLP fusion, niche queries, batch correction, multi-segment mode. Written complete independent plans with specific line counts, titled mutable-riding-eclipse.md. ✅ Fixing demo replay environment configuration mismatches—Using three-tier root cause analysis, identified mismatches between PickPlace_D0+controller_configs and pure PickPlace; loading environment parameters from HDF5 metadata; restoring proximity thresholds after fixes. ✅ Fixing CalendarPro double verification intention mismatches—Modified dual_verify.py to prioritize LLM intentions when mismatches occur, as LLM has complete dialogue context while SR only matches keywords. Logs show “→using LLM intentions” for monitoring. ✅ Adding CalendarPro calendar error reporting function—Refactored calendar_service.py to throw CalendarServiceError instead of silently failing; updated all handlers (discord_bot, scheduler, api_routes) to capture and display detailed error messages. ✅ Fixing data issues in 4 failed MIHD sections—Extracted missing spatial files from Dataset.zip (tissue_positions_list.csv, scalefactors_json.json, images); fixed boundary checks for frequency filters; added backtracking mechanism from mclust to KMeans. ✅ Refactoring gadget daily summary into a two-stage architecture—Redesigned daily_summary.py, adding CLI subcommands (export/merge/legacy); added device information collection; designed versioned JSON log format; implemented token usage statistics. ✅ Implementing CalendarPro periodic task system—Created RecurringTaskStore with JSONL persistence, using PeriodicChecker with asyncio background loop (15-minute intervals); added MANAGE_RECURRING/QUERY_RECURRING intentions, semantic routing, Discord integration. Passed 18 tests. ✅ Fixing CalendarPro periodic event modification issues—Modified update_event() function to detect periodic instances and delete duplicate recurrence/recurringEventId fields before updating, ensuring Google Calendar creates exceptions only for single instances. ✅ Creating CalendarPro PLANNING.md document—Created comprehensive planning document with over 330 lines, including project positioning, module maturity ratings, hierarchy, architectural decisions and reasons, P0-P3 roadmaps, known defects, etc. ✅ CalendarPro P0 reliability fix—Implemented three-stage rollback mechanism, handling AI provider timeouts (asyncio.wait_for + httpx.Timeout), message queue completion callbacks, service layer error handling, conducted 56 intent classification boundary tests. ✅ CalendarPro P1 intelligent upgrade—Added automatic collection of learning data, personalized energy prediction through personalize_rhythm(), integrated learning functions into IntelligentScheduler, improved dialogue context formatting. ✅ Implementing CalendarPro random thought function—Built a complete thought capture system: ThoughtStore uses JSONL storage, IdleDetector reused EssentialScheduler, ThoughtOrganizer has background asyncio loop, integrated with Discord, providing 3 processing methods, passed 25 tests. ✅ Writing comprehensive gadget documentation—Written separate READMEs for 7 tools (summary/video/audio/image/paper/git/test), and a root README with tool overview tables, also wrote summarize/tutorial.md quick start guide. ✅ Fixing MuJoCo speed switch errors—Corrected env_wrapper.py, treating cvel[3:] as linear velocity, cvel[:3] as angular velocity (MuJoCo stores [angular velocity, linear velocity]). ✅ Implementing error recovery validators (LargeOffset, Stuck)—Created large_offset.py and stuck.py validators, with configuration parts and 16 unit tests (8 for each validator), completed 4/4 validator suite. ✅ Streamlining error recovery CLAUDE.md document—Reduced documentation from 615 lines to 101 lines, eliminating duplicates, removing irrelevant chapters, merging relevant sections. ✅ Enhancing visualization with frame annotations—Added cv2-based annotations: colored banners for different stages (green/red/orange), translucent info boxes, detection/injection/verification details, frame counters. Implementation and Fixes ✅ Initializing gadget’s Git repository—Created.gitignore file, executed git init command, added test/ as a submodule to TzJ2006/test.git, then committed to git@github.com:TzJ2006/gadget.git. ✅ Integrating ccusage token statistics—Called npx ccusage to extract ccusage information, parsed JSON output; integrated it into all three entry points; displayed token usage section in markdown reports. ✅ Updating CalendarPro documentation—Updated CLAUDE.md document, adding 10 previously unrecorded systems; expanded README document with 4 types of usage examples, Discord command references, learning system documentation; added PowerShell startup script. 🔄 Cleaning up error recovery code—First stage completed: removed approximately 190 dead lines (ReplaySystem, ReplayDriftError, duplicate detect() method); commented on BCPolicy stubs. Second to fifth stages were interrupted due to reasons. Issues and Solutions Key Issues 1. Error recovery robots never approached objects during demo replays (minimum distance was 0.22 meters, tasks require contact). Solution: Analyzed robosuite source code, HDF5 metadata, and MuJoCo’s body mapping relationships. It was found that demonstrations recorded using PickPlace_D0+controller_configs used pure PickPlace during replays. The same operation produced completely different trajectories. Key Insight: Environmental configurations (kp, damping coefficient, ramp_ratio in controller_configs) fundamentally changed the robot’s dynamics. Always use environmental metadata completely matching the HDF5 dataset for replays—parameter adjustments could mask the underlying problem.
2. CalendarPro’s semantic router and LLM conflicted, causing the system to always use SR, leading to incorrect routing (e.g., “leetcode” keyword → using manage_recurring instead of update). Solution: Modified dual_verify.py to prioritize LLM intentions when mismatches occurred. The LLM had complete dialogue context, while SR only matched keywords. Logs showed “→using LLM intentions” for monitoring. Key Insight: Keyword-based classifiers would fail when dealing with ambiguous expressions. When there was a disagreement between semantic routing and LLM, the more contextually supported model (LLM) should be trusted, rather than the faster model (SR). SR would filter obvious cases, while the LLM acted as an arbitrator.
3. Missing data in 4 MIHD sections caused pipeline failures (missing spatial files, empty frequency filters, STAIG model crashes). Solution: Extracted spatial files from Dataset.zip; added boundary checks (min_w/min_h >= 10); added backtracking mechanism from mclust to KMeans, using try/except wrappers for handling. Key Insight: Spatial transcriptomics data required robust fault tolerance: multi-path candidate searches, image processing boundary checks, statistical model backtracking strategies to handle distribution anomalies.
4. Gadget’s original single-step workflow couldn’t support multi-device collaboration (required manual copying of logs to each device). Solution: Refactored into a two-stage process: (1) Exporting sub-commands to generate local JSON logs with device information (no API key needed); (2) Merging sub-commands, aggregating multiple logs, and calling API to generate final reports; keeping the old mode for backward compatibility. Key Insight: Multi-device workflows required portable intermediate formats (versioned JSON logs) and clear phase division (local parsing and remote merging). Main designs included: device information collection, version support for future expansion, optional device summaries to prevent information loss during merging.
5. All error detectors failed after adding proximity gates—iterative threshold adjustments (0.05→0.15→0.25→0.30 meters) didn’t produce any trigger signals. Solution: Written diagnostic scripts to measure actual EEF-object distances, finding that the issue was environmental mismatch, not threshold problems. The root cause was playback configuration issues, not detector sensitivity issues. Key Insight: When all detectors failed, real-world measurements should be taken through diagnostic logging, followed by threshold adjustment. Symptoms without trigger signals often masked underlying mismatches (e.g., incorrect controller dynamics).
6. CalendarPro calendar API errors (403 unauthorized access, HttpError) were caught and returned as None via print() function, resulting in users receiving a general “failed” message, causing confusion. Solution: Created CalendarServiceError exception; refactored all calendar_service methods to provide detailed context when errors occurred; updated all callers to capture and display user-friendly messages with technical details. Key Insight: Silent error handling (print + returning None) violated error handling conventions. Users couldn’t fix problems they couldn’t see. Always pass contextual errors to the presentation layer, then format them appropriately.
7. CalendarPro AI provider lacked timeout handling mechanism—network hangs could cause the entire robot to freeze indefinitely. Solution: Wrapped all API calls with asyncio.wait_for(timeout=30.0), and added httpx.Timeout(30.0) in the client constructor, returning an AIResponse with error fields when timed out. Key Insight: Timeout protection required dual defense: asyncio coroutine level (wait_for) and HTTP client level (httpx.Timeout), to catch different failure modes.
8. CalendarPro’s JSON parsing failed when AI returned prose before JSON, multiple blocks, or truncated JSON—resulting in intent classification failures. Solution: Implemented extract_json() function, extracting the first complete object by balancing parentheses count; added three-stage backtracking mechanism (direct parsing→tail trailing comma correction→general intent); removed ValueError exception for logging and backtracking purposes. Key Insight: Production-grade AI systems needed graceful degradation—for malformed outputs, they must never crash, always providing safe backtracking intentions.
9. Plan documentation lacked self-contained nature—users repeatedly rejected ExitPlanMode, demanding “writing every sentence of the plan into the plan document.” Solution: Redrew the plan documentation to make it fully self-contained: added user decision records, code snapshots with exact line numbers, QueST reference code details, complete implementation code, with “inserted after line X” locations for each stage. Key Insight: Plan mode documentation must be fully self-contained to be restored later. Users’ rejections indicated insufficient documentation details—all exploration results (exact locations, line numbers, function signatures, existing code) needed to be written into the plan, rather than relying on external context.
10. MuJoCo’s speed switching caused false instability triggers (angular velocity was misinterpreted as linear).#### 11. The CLAUDE.md file has 615 lines, with a lot of repetition between sections. Some content also belongs to the README file. Solution: Rewritten documentation, focusing on: (1) Unimportant information (server restrictions, common pitfalls); (2) Consolidating commands into one block; (3) Removing easily noticeable content (file tree, method signatures); (4) Keeping only the architectures that require multi-file reading.
Key Insight: Documentation for AI agents should focus on content that tools cannot discover, rather than being a comprehensive reference. Simplicity improves usability—101 lines vs. 615 lines, making it clearer.
12. When CalendarPro updates duplicate events, it modifies the entire series instead of individual instances, even though singleEvents=True is used in searches. Solution: Modified the update_event() function to detect duplicate instances (with recurringEventId), and remove duplicate/reoccurringEventId before calling the API. Google Calendar creates exceptions for such instances separately.
Key Insight: Implicit behavior of Google Calendar API: using singleEvents=True in searches gives instance IDs, but updates/deletes require explicitly removing relevant fields to avoid modifying the entire series. Always test duplicate operations on a test calendar.
13. CalendarPro’s asyncio.create_task() for message queues lacks exception handling callbacks—tasks may silently terminate when the queue is full, and user messages will never be processed. Solution: Added done_callback=_on_queue_task_done to all create_task calls; implemented cleanup handlers to record exceptions and remove dead tasks; added close() method for closing.
Key Insight: asyncio background tasks are “after-triggered forgotten,” so done callbacks are always needed for monitoring/cleanup, especially for long-running tasks.
General Issues 14. Manually querying token usage is cumbersome—users want automatic daily statistics. Solution: Integrated ccusage by calling npx ccusage@latest daily –json, parsed output; automated collection at all three entry points; displayed total tables and model details in token usage section; consolidated data across devices.
Key Insight: Automate data collection using existing tools instead of reinventing them. Design considerations include: fault tolerance of subprocesses (skip silently if unavailable), multi-device aggregation strategy (summarizing tokens/costs, combining model details), and readable presentation (tables > lists).
15. MIHD GPU peak statistics always remain zero (ResourceMonitor never calls update_peak_stats()). Solution: Refactored ResourceMonitor: start_monitoring() starts a daemon thread that samples every second, stop_monitoring() terminates the thread and samples the final peak, added threading.Event lifecycle control.
Key Insight: Monitoring peak statistics requires continuous sampling during operation, not just startup/stop snapshots. Background daemon threads are a standard solution, but proper lifecycle management is needed to avoid resource leaks.
16. The gadget test/directory is a nested Git repository with its own remote address, causing conflicts during git add operations. Solution: Used git submodules: moved test folder to a backup location, ran ‘git submodule add git@github.com:TzJ2006/test.git test’ for cloning, removed backup, .gitmodules automatically records configuration.
Key Insight: Git submodules are suitable for nested repositories with independent remote repositories. Key points: submodules record commit hashes rather than branches; git submodule update --init --recursive is required during cloning; updating requires entering the submodule directory, executing git pull, then modifying the commit hash.
17. CalendarPro’s start.bat cannot detect conda environments (cmd doesn’t support ‘conda activate’ without conda init, new cmd window doesn’t have CONDA_PREFIX). Solution: Created start.ps1 script that supports native PowerShell conda functionality; PowerShell can directly execute ‘conda activate calendarpro’. Also updated start.bat to allow it to call PowerShell under -ExecutionPolicy Bypass policy; added PID tracking and stop.ps1 script.
Key Insight: Windows batch files perform poorly in conda integration. For Windows-based conda workflows, PowerShell is a better choice. Always test environment activation in a new shell, rather than using inherited conda sessions.
18. CalendarPro’s simulated patch failed because generate_with_fallback was imported inside the method, not at the module level. Solution: Changed the patch target from ‘src.thoughts.thought_organizer.generate_with_fallback’ to ‘src.aiProvider_selector.generate_with_fallback’ to apply patches to the source module.
Key Insight: During simulation, patches should affect the function definition, not the import location—local imports create new references, thus bypassing module-level patches.
19. After creating test_thoughts.py, CalendarPro’s pytest async tests encountered ‘coroutine never awaited’ warnings. Solution: Installed the pytest-asyncio package, which can automatically detect async test functions and run them in the event loop.
Human-AI Methodology Strategic Level Methods for analyzing root causes of demonstration playback errors Role Method Human Adopt systematic three-layer analysis: (1) Identify mismatch issues by comparing recorded environment with playback environment; (2) Discover other problems (vibration during initialization, lack of proximity door); (3) Realize that the expansion of previous thresholds was actually a symptom masking the problem AI May continue adjusting proximity thresholds and detector parameters, treating problems as symptoms rather than fundamental mismatch issues Difference Analysis: Humans use structured root cause analysis (comparing recorded environment with playback environment), while AI gradually adjusts parameters. Humans see the expansion of thresholds as a symptom masking the real problem; AI sees it as a solution.
Detailed requirements for planning documents Role Method Human Rejected the ExitPlanMode approach multiple times, demanding “writing every sentence of the plan in the planning document” — emphasizing complete self-control, including all exact line numbers and code snippets AI Initially wrote high-level steps and a list of documents, assuming precise line numbers and code snippets would be found during implementation Difference Analysis: Humans emphasized the importance of future session recovery capabilities—the plan must be able to execute completely outside the current context. AI underestimated the importance of self-control, requiring multiple iterations to understand expectations (plan = complete implementation manual, not a short task list).
Robot never approached the object, EEF distance calculation error Role Method Human Immediately questioned the distance measurement method when told the robot never approached 0.22 meters, asking “How did this happen?” and suspecting a measurement error AI Initially accepted 0.22 meters as the baseline, focusing on threshold adjustments (0.05→0.15→0.25→0.30 meters), rather than verifying the measurement itself Difference Analysis: Human domain expertise (contact required for picking and placing operations) made them skeptical about measurement results. AI focused on symptoms (adjusting thresholds) until the measurement validity was corrected.
Diagnosing errors from production logs Role Method Human Read the startup logs, immediately identifying SR/LLM mismatch issues (“leetcode” classified as manage_recurring), and ignoring calendar 403 errors, diagnosing the root cause without prompts AI Did not actively analyze logs. Needed human intervention to point out specific issues before investigation Difference Analysis: Humans analyzed domain-related logs (understanding intent classification semantics and error propagation patterns). AI only responded to explicit instructions.
Implementation strategies and technical choices for MIHD improvement plan Role Method Human Choosed “phased full implementation” (rather than partial implementation), first adopting a Q-Former cycle version approach (without batch optimization), requiring multi-part analysis for batch corrections (not just single part) AI Provided three options (full/partial/specific phase implementation), asked about performance optimization (Q-Former batch processing) and feature scope (single mode vs. combined mode) for each technical decision point Difference Analysis: Humans preferred completeness and progressive optimization (first verifying the cycle version, then considering batch processing), while AI provided options for balancing performance. Humans showed understanding of batch correction scenarios (no need for correction for single part).
Documenting concepts for AI agent Role Method Human Documentation should focus on non-obvious information: server restrictions, architecture patterns requiring multi-file understanding, common pitfalls. Eliminate discoverable content (file tree, method signatures) and repetitive content AI AI typically creates comprehensive reference documents containing complete file trees, detailed API signatures, troubleshooting sections, and cross-part repetitive content Difference Analysis: Humans recognized that AI agents could understand structures through tools, eliminating the need for reference materials—they needed context guidance. AI provides comprehensive documentation suitable for human developers.
Implementation planning and task decomposition for CalendarPro Role Method Human Provided very detailed plans, including exact file paths, method signatures, code examples, integration points, and reasons—minimizing ambiguity AI Systematically followed the plan, created TodoWrite tasks, executed them in dependency order, but occasionally had to trial and error for edge cases not covered in the plan (e.g., simulated patch paths) Difference Analysis: Humans anticipated architectural design in advance and foresaw integration challenges, while AI excels at systematic execution but needs to iterate to find edge cases not covered.
Two-stage export/merge workflow for multi-device aggregation Role Method Human Immediately realized the need to export data by device → Due to physical device separation and manual file transfer, centralized merging was required AI Initially proposed a single-stage solution that ran on all devices simultaneously, ignoring the actual limitations of offline device coordination Difference Analysis: Humans anticipated operational limitations (manual file copying, asynchronous device usage), while AI overlooked these limitations during the design process. User domain knowledge drove architectural decisions.
Exploring code libraries using autonomous agents Role Method Human Clearly initiated 3 parallel exploration agents to fully understand CalendarPro’s vision, intelligence layer, and scalability, then wrote the planning document AI Typically reads key files sequentially, gradually understanding the content—while human methods are more comprehensive and parallel Difference Analysis: Humans recognized the value of using professional agents for comprehensive preliminary exploration, which was more strategic than the typical incremental approach of AI.### Actual Learning Outcomes
Automating data collection using existing tools (ccusage) instead of reinventing the wheel. Design considerations include subprocess fault tolerance (silently skipping failures), multi-device aggregation strategies (summing/merging), and easy-to-read presentation formats (tables > lists). Using Git submodules: For independent remote subprojects, use commit hashes instead of branches. Clone with --recursive, and update by cd followed by git pull and committing changes. Monitoring peak statistics: Continuously sample during runtime (background threads), not just at startup/stop. Proper thread lifecycle management is needed to avoid resource leaks. Windows Condana workflow: Use PowerShell instead of cmd.exe. Batch files lack proper Condana integration. Always test environment activation in a new shell. Simulations in pytest: Patch at the function definition level, not at the import level. Local imports create new references, bypassing module-level patching. Semantics of cluster file systems: Shared storage means nodes have the same paths, but SSH sessions default to home directories. Always explicitly execute cd commands in remote commands. Dialogue Summary MIHD ✅ Data repair and seven-stage enhancement plan design 2026-02-13 | claude_code Fixed four failed DLPFC sections (extracting spatial files from Dataset.zip, fixing frequency filtering boundary checks, adding mclust→KMeans rollback, fixing GPU monitoring using background threads). Analyzed code libraries (config_manager.py 590 lines, run_benchmark.py 2600 lines) and QueST reference implementations. Designed comprehensive plans for stages 1-7: configuration restructuring, normalized integration, UNI2+scGPT experiments, Q-Former/LLaVA MLP fusion, niche query (QueST GIN encoder), batch correction (adversarial training), multi-part joint patterns. Created a self-contained plan document with exact line numbers, complete code, and change logs for 13 files. All 11 sections are now running, ready for approximately 286 experiments. ✅ Refactoring the daily summary tool and initializing the Git repository 2026-02-13 | claude_code Refactored daily_summary.py into a two-stage architecture: export subcommands (local JSON logs with device information, optional device-level summaries, no API required); merge subcommands (summarizing multi-device logs and generating final reports via API calls). Retained the old pattern for backward compatibility. Integrated ccusage token statistics through npx ccusage@latest, adding token usage sections in reports. Initialized the Git repository: created.gitignore file, added test/ as a git submodule (TzJ2006/test.git commit 1dc527c), and pushed code to git@github.com:TzJ2006/gadget.git. Written detailed documentation: 7 separate READMEs (about summaries, videos, audio, images, papers, git, and testing), a root README with an overview of the tool, and summarize/tutorial.md. Supports cross-device workflows and automatically tracks token usage. The tool is now open-source and includes complete documentation.
error_recovery_benchmark ✅ Completion of v4.1 infrastructure and fixing demo replay bugs 2026-02-13 | claude_code Completed v4.1 infrastructure construction: implemented LargeOffsetValidator and StuckValidator (S5.1, S5.2), and wrote 16 unit tests. Improved visualization with cv2-based frame annotations (stage color bars, info boxes, frame counters). Updated documentation by increasing the number of validators to 4. Attempted smoke testing on GPU clusters, but encountered SSH path issues. Fixed critical bugs through three-layer root cause analysis: demonstrations were performed using PickPlace_D0+controller_configs (OSC_POSE, kp=150), but there were trajectory mismatches when running PickPlace directly. Modified generate_scenes.py and visualize_scene.py to load environment parameters from HDF5 metadata, improving scene generation efficiency by 10 times (from 3 to 30 scenes). EEF proximity increased from 22 cm to 5 cm, and steps triggered during demonstrations were located within the demonstration process (steps 52-121). Reduced CLAUDE.md from 615 lines to 101 lines, removing duplicate content and focusing on non-obvious information. Code cleanup: removed about 190 deadlines (e.g., ReplaySystem, duplicate detect() method). 41 tests passed, infrastructure completed.
CalendarPro ✅ Repeat tasks, production environment bug fixes, P0/P1 level improvements, random thinking feature 2026-02-13 | claude_code Conducted a large-scale release covering multiple aspects: (1) Implemented an automatic scheduling system for repeat tasks using JSONL’s RecurringTaskStore, with PeriodicChecker having a 15-minute asynchronous background loop, MANAGE_RECURRING/QUERY_RECURRING intentions, semantic routing, Discord integration, totaling 18 tests. (2) Fixed production environment bugs: dual_verify.py prioritized LLM over semantic router when mismatches occurred; added CalendarServiceError exception propagation mechanism in calendar_service; fixed issues where repeated events only affected individual instances by removing duplicate fields. (3) Created comprehensive PLANNING.md document (over 330 lines) documenting architecture, module maturity, goal hierarchy, and P0-P3 roadmaps. (4) Implemented P0 level reliability fixes: elastic handling of JSON parsing, three-stage rollback mechanism; set AI provider timeout to 30 seconds (asyncio.wait_for + httpx.Timeout), message queue completion callbacks, service layer error handling, and conducted 56 intent classification boundary tests. (5) Implemented P1 level AI upgrade: automatically collected learning data through backfill_energy_change(), personalized energy prediction through personalize_rhythm(), integrated learning functions into IntelligentScheduler, and improved dialogue context formatting. (6) Built a random thinking feature: using JSONL’s ThoughtStore, IdleDetector reused EssentialScheduler, ThoughtOrganizer performed AI-driven organization work in idle time through background loops, supporting Discord integration, totaling 25 tests. (7) Updated documentation: CLAUDE.md included 10 unrecorded systems, expanded README, added 4 types of example and learning system documents. Final status: 107/110 tests passed (3 failed due to browser issues), the tool is ready for production use, featuring automatic learning, personalized energy prediction, repeat task scheduling, and thought organization functions.

summary: 全面的多项目开发日：解决了MIHD空间转录组学数据中的相关问题，并制定了为期7阶段的改进计划；对Gadget日常报告工具进行了重构，将其设计成双阶段、支持多设备的架构，同时建立了Git仓库；实现了错误恢复基准测试工具，修复了关键的演示环境不匹配问题；开发了CalendarPro功能，能够自动安排重复任务，修复了生产环境中的一些漏洞，并实现了包含随机思考功能的P0/P1级路线图功能。
title: Bug Journal 2026-02-13

content:
Daily Report — 2026-02-12 Daily Overview What was done: Streamlined MIHD’s CLAUDE.md documentation and architected a multi-phase plan integrating embedding normalization, Q-Former/LLaVA fusion strategies, and QueST-inspired niche querying with batch correction How it was done: Conducted parallel codebase exploration of MIHD and QueST, researched Q-Former architecture, structured 6 user ideas into hierarchical implementation phases with dependency analysis, and clarified scope via user questions Impact: Reduced documentation from 579 to 188 lines while adding missing content; produced implementation-ready plan addressing pipeline inconsistencies and adding 4 new capabilities (~2,850 LOC estimated) with full config-driven hyperparameter control Improved MIHD documentation and designed comprehensive 7-phase enhancement plan for normalization, Q-Former fusion, and QueST-inspired features
Tasks Architecture & Strategy ✅ Design 7-phase MIHD enhancement plan — Created comprehensive implementation plan covering normalization, Q-Former/LLaVA fusion, QueST niche querying, batch correction, and config overhaul with dependency graph ❌ Implement enhancement plan phases 1-7 — User stopped implementation before execution to review the plan; implementation ready to proceed pending user approval ✅ Research Q-Former and QueST architectures — Deep-dive into BLIP-2 Q-Former vs LLaVA MLP connector, QueST’s GIN layers, batch correction via adversarial training, and niche querying via k-hop subgraph pooling Implementation & Fixes ✅ Improve CLAUDE.md documentation — Analyzed existing CLAUDE.md, eliminated redundancy, added missing components (GCN encoder, fusion strategies, test commands, utils modules), reduced from 579 to 188 lines Problems & Solutions Critical Issues 1. MIHD has inconsistent embedding normalization - UNI2/scGPT are L2-normed but HIPT/ResNet50/MLP/GCN are not, and no post-fusion normalization exists Solution: Designed 3-point normalization system (post-encoder, pre-fusion, post-fusion) with configurable methods (L2, standard scaling, none) via new utils/normalization.py module
Key Insight: Normalization inconsistency across encoders hurts downstream clustering; systematic 3-point approach provides fine-grained control
2. QueST uses batch correction via adversarial training but unclear if effective for spatial transcriptomics Solution: Designed plug-in BatchCorrection module following QueST’s discriminator/generator pattern with evaluation metrics to assess effectiveness
Key Insight: Porting batch correction requires empirical validation in MIHD’s context rather than assuming transferability

Tasks Architecture & Strategy ✅ Run STAIG fusion benchmarks on all DLPFC sections — Execute benchmarks with PCA+UNI+STAIG fusion configuration on 11 DLPFC sections using –skip_cached to preserve existing results 🔄 Launch RM-Ideal evaluation across 7 sections — Run RM-Ideal niche query evaluation with WWL graph kernel for all embedding methods across available sections Implementation & Fixes ✅ Add metrics overlay to clustering visualizations — Refactor utils/visualization.py and run_benchmark.py to display ARI/NMI/Silhouette scores as text box below clustering plots ✅ Add metrics overlay to RM-Ideal visualizations — Add Spearman correlation, P@K, and SameLabel@K metrics to spatial heatmap visualizations in evaluate_rm_ideal.py Problems & Solutions Critical Issues 1. Metrics overlay not appearing on plots despite modifying utils/visualization.py Solution: Discovered duplicate create_clustering_visualization() function at line 1644 in run_benchmark.py that shadowed the utils version - modified both
Key Insight: In large codebases, always grep for function definitions across all files before assuming a single modification will take effect
2. Risk of overwriting existing good results from pca_uni_staig_fusion directory Solution: Used –skip_cached flag instead of –override to preserve cached embeddings for completed sections
Key Insight: Always check existing results before re-running experiments - use caching flags to distinguish between incremental completion and full re-run
General Issues 3. Parallel GPU tasks caused OOM kill (exit code 137) when running 3 STAIG fusion experiments simultaneously Solution: Switched to sequential execution by chaining commands with && in a single Bash call
Key Insight: Deep learning benchmarks with large spatial transcriptomics data require exclusive GPU access - parallelize across CPU-bound tasks only
4. Sections 151510, 151672, 151674 fail with missing spatial coordinates or OpenCV errors Solution: Script continues with remaining sections - identified these as data integrity issues requiring separate investigation
Key Insight: Robust benchmark pipelines should validate data prerequisites (spatial coords, image files) and fail gracefully with clear error messages
Human vs AI Approaches Strategic Level Preserving existing good results Role Approach Human Human explicitly stated ’there is a folder named pca_uni_staig_fusion with really good results, do not overwrite' AI AI initially ran with –override flag which would have overwritten existing results Difference Analysis: Human had explicit knowledge of which results were valuable and should be preserved - AI defaulted to re-running everything without checking existing work
Duplicate function definition detection Role Approach Human Human noticed metrics weren’t appearing and prompted investigation of why the modification didn’t work AI AI modified utils/visualization.py but didn’t check for other definitions of the same function in the codebase Difference Analysis: Human’s domain knowledge of the project structure helped identify that run_benchmark.py might have its own local copy - AI relied on grep after the fact rather than proactively checking for shadowing

AI Limitations Critical Limitations Failed to detect duplicate function definitions - modified utils/visualization.py without checking if run_benchmark.py had its own local copy that would shadow it Used –override flag by default without checking if valuable existing results would be lost General Limitations Attempted to run 3 GPU-intensive deep learning tasks in parallel without considering GPU memory constraints, leading to OOM kill Learnings Key Learnings In large codebases with modular utilities, always grep for all occurrences of a function name before modifying - local copies can shadow imports and cause silent modification failures Deep learning benchmarks on spatial transcriptomics data (4000+ spots, 1024-dim embeddings, STAIG training) require exclusive GPU access - parallelize only CPU-bound tasks like RM-Ideal WWL graph kernels Experiment management requires explicit caching strategy: –override for full re-run, –skip_cached for incremental completion, no flags to fail on duplicates Practical Learnings Visualization refactoring pattern: explicit fig/axes creation enables custom annotations (metrics text overlay) that scanpy’s save= parameter doesn’t support Conversation Summaries 🔄 Run STAIG fusion benchmarks across all DLPFC sections 21:50:31.276 | claude_code User requested running benchmarks on all 12 DLPFC sections with STAIG fusion + UNI2 + GCN/PCA/None.
